topics.title: Sieve Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCEL507 - GEOTECHNICAL ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Sieve Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Sieve Analysis** (also termed *Mechanical Grain Size Analysis* or *Dry Sieving*) is a fundamental laboratory procedure in geotechnical engineering used to determine the **particle size distribution** of coarse-grained soils (i.e., soils retained primarily on the **75 µm IS Sieve**). The test is conducted in strict accordance with **IS 2720 (Part 4) – 1985** (Reaffirmed 2006), and the apparatus conforms to **IS 460 (Specification for Test Sieves)**.

The procedure involves shaking a known mass of oven-dried soil through a stack of progressively finer wire-cloth sieves, and the soil fraction retained on each sieve is weighed to compute the percentage passing (i.e., the *percentage finer*).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Sieve analysis is restricted to the determination of the grain size distribution of soils whose particles are larger than **75 µm** (i.e., the sand and gravel fraction). For particles finer than 75 µm, the **Wet (Hydrometer) Analysis** as per **IS 2720 (Part 4)** is the prescribed companion test.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Understanding

Imagine you have a bag of mixed dry ingredients containing large rock chunks, gravel, sand, and dust. If you had a series of kitchen colanders stacked from largest holes at the top to smallest holes at the bottom, and you poured the mixture in and shook the stack — the **big rocks stay on top**, the **medium pebbles fall to the middle sieves**, and the **fine dust ends up in the bottom pan**.

By weighing how much material is "caught" on each colander, you can precisely map out **"what sizes of stuff are present, and how much of each size."** Sieve analysis does exactly this for soil.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The sieve is essentially a *particle-sorting machine*. The sieve opening (aperture) is the threshold: every particle **larger** than the aperture stays **on top**, and every particle **smaller** than the aperture **passes through**. This binary decision is repeated at every sieve level.

### 1.3 Applicable Soil Types and IS Sieve Standard

| Property | Specification |
| :--- | :--- |
| **IS Standard (Test)** | **IS 2720 (Part 4) – 1985** |
| **IS Standard (Sieve)** | **IS 460 (Test Sieves)** |
| **Applicable Soil** | Coarse-grained (gravel & sand) |
| **Soil Particle Range** | **75 µm to 100 mm** |
| **Required Sample Mass** | **500 g (gravelly sand)** to **5 kg (gravels)** |
| **Required Drying** | Oven-dried at **105 °C to 110 °C** for **16–24 hours** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Takeaway:** Sieve analysis is **discontinuous and discrete** — it sorts particles into "buckets" defined by the sieve apertures you choose. The more sieves you use, the higher the resolution of the resulting size distribution curve.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Underlying Principle — The "Why" Behind Sieving

The principle is the **mechanical separation of particles based on their intermediate dimension (b)** — i.e., the dimension that allows the particle to either pass or be retained by the square aperture of the sieve.

For a particle to **pass through** a sieve, its **intermediate dimension must be smaller than the sieve aperture**. The apparatus is calibrated such that:
- A square mesh of size $D$ retains all particles whose $b > D$.
- Particles with $b < D$ pass to the next finer sieve.

The kinetic energy from a mechanical sieve shaker (typically **300 oscillations per minute** for **15 minutes**, or until less than **1 %** of the residue passes any sieve in **1 minute**) overcomes the inter-particle friction, allowing smaller particles to migrate downward.

### 2.2 The Three Theoretical Foundations

1. **Stokes' Criterion Boundary:** Sieve analysis is valid only for **non-cohesive (granular)** soils. In cohesive soils, particles tend to stick together and clog the sieve openings, giving erroneous (artificially high) percent-retained values.
2. **Logarithmic Particle-Size Distribution:** Soils are characterized using a **semi-log plot** (% finer on arithmetic Y-axis, particle size on log X-axis) because natural particle sizes span **four orders of magnitude** (from clay at < 0.002 mm to boulders at > 200 mm).
3. **Hydrometer Threshold:** The **75 µm (No. 200) sieve** marks the practical lower limit of sieving, below which dry sieving is ineffective and **sedimentation analysis** is used.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master the equations below — they form the core of every KTU Part-A (3-mark) and Part-B (14-mark) question on this experiment.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $M_{total} = \sum M_{retained}$ | Mass conservation check (should equal initial dry mass) | g |
| 2 | $P_{R,i} = \dfrac{M_{R,i}}{M_{total}} \times 100$ | Percentage retained on $i^{th}$ sieve | % |
| 3 | $C_{R,i} = \sum_{j=1}^{i} P_{R,j}$ | Cumulative percentage retained up to $i^{th}$ sieve | % |
| 4 | $P_{F,i} = 100 - C_{R,i}$ | **Percentage finer** passing $i^{th}$ sieve | % |
| 5 | $C_u = \dfrac{D_{60}}{D_{10}}$ | **Coefficient of Uniformity** | Dimensionless |
| 6 | $C_c = \dfrac{(D_{30})^2}{D_{10} \times D_{60}}$ | **Coefficient of Curvature** | Dimensionless |
| 7 | $\log(D) = \log(D_1) + \dfrac{N_1 - N}{N_1 - N_2} \cdot \left[\log(D_2) - \log(D_1)\right]$ | Log-interpolation for $D_N$ from grain-size curve | mm |

> [!NOTE]
> **IS 1498 – 1970 Classification Criteria (very high KTU yield):**
> - **Well-graded soil:** $C_u \geq 4$ (gravels) or $C_u \geq 6$ (sands) **AND** $1 \leq C_c \leq 3$.
> - **Poorly-graded soil:** Fails to meet either condition.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Foundation Design:** Granular soils with high $C_u$ (well-graded) compact densely and offer higher bearing capacity for shallow foundations.
- **Filter Design (Filters in Earth Dams):** The classic **Terzaghi filter criterion** uses $D_{10}$ of the filter to ensure soil particles do not wash out. Sieve analysis is the only direct way to obtain $D_{10}$.
- **Aggregate Selection:** Concrete and road-base aggregates are graded via sieve analysis for optimal packing.
- **Soil Classification (ISCS / USCS):** Determines the symbol of the soil (e.g., **SW** – Well-graded sand, **SP** – Poorly-graded sand, **GW**, **GP**).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example — Complete Sieve Analysis Calculation

**Statement:** A **500 g** oven-dried soil sample was subjected to sieve analysis through the standard IS sieve set. The following observations were recorded. Construct the grain-size distribution curve and compute $D_{10}$, $D_{30}$, $D_{60}$, $C_u$, $C_c$, and classify the soil.

**Observation Table:**

| Sieve Size (mm) | Mass Retained $M_R$ (g) | IS Designation |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 4.750 | 0 | Coarse gravel boundary |
| 2.360 | 50 | Fine gravel boundary |
| 1.180 | 75 | Coarse sand boundary |
| 0.600 | 100 | Medium sand boundary |
| 0.425 | 80 | (extra sieve used) |
| 0.300 | 60 | Medium-fine sand |
| 0.150 | 100 | Fine sand boundary |
| 0.075 | 30 | Silt boundary (No. 200) |
| Pan | 5 | Silt + Clay |

**Step 1 — Mass Conservation Check:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{total} &= 0 + 50 + 75 + 100 + 80 + 60 + 100 + 30 + 5 \\
&= 500 \text{ g} \quad \checkmark \text{ (matches initial sample)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU practicals, the mass balance should be within **± 1 %** of the initial dry mass. If not, the test must be repeated.

**Step 2 — Percentage Retained on Each Sieve:**

Using $P_{R,i} = \dfrac{M_{R,i}}{M_{total}} \times 100$ and $M_{total} = 500$ g:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{R,4.75} &= \frac{0}{500} \times 100 = 0.0 \,\% \\
P_{R,2.36} &= \frac{50}{500} \times 100 = 10.0 \,\% \\
P_{R,1.18} &= \frac{75}{500} \times 100 = 15.0 \,\% \\
P_{R,0.60} &= \frac{100}{500} \times 100 = 20.0 \,\% \\
P_{R,0.425} &= \frac{80}{500} \times 100 = 16.0 \,\% \\
P_{R,0.30} &= \frac{60}{500} \times 100 = 12.0 \,\% \\
P_{R,0.15} &= \frac{100}{500} \times 100 = 20.0 \,\% \\
P_{R,0.075} &= \frac{30}{500} \times 100 = 6.0 \,\% \\
P_{R,Pan} &= \frac{5}{500} \times 100 = 1.0 \,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Cumulative Percentage Retained (Cumulative summation from top down):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{R,4.75} &= 0.0 \,\% \\
C_{R,2.36} &= 0.0 + 10.0 = 10.0 \,\% \\
C_{R,1.18} &= 10.0 + 15.0 = 25.0 \,\% \\
C_{R,0.60} &= 25.0 + 20.0 = 45.0 \,\% \\
C_{R,0.425} &= 45.0 + 16.0 = 61.0 \,\% \\
C_{R,0.30} &= 61.0 + 12.0 = 73.0 \,\% \\
C_{R,0.15} &= 73.0 + 20.0 = 93.0 \,\% \\
C_{R,0.075} &= 93.0 + 6.0 = 99.0 \,\% \\
C_{R,Pan} &= 99.0 + 1.0 = 100.0 \,\% \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Percentage Finer (Passing each sieve):**

Using $P_{F,i} = 100 - C_{R,i}$:

| Sieve (mm) | $M_R$ (g) | $P_R$ (%) | $C_R$ (%) | $P_F$ (%) |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 4.750 | 0 | 0.0 | 0.0 | **100.0** |
| 2.360 | 50 | 10.0 | 10.0 | **90.0** |
| 1.180 | 75 | 15.0 | 25.0 | **75.0** |
| 0.600 | 100 | 20.0 | 45.0 | **55.0** |
| 0.425 | 80 | 16.0 | 61.0 | **39.0** |
| 0.300 | 60 | 12.0 | 73.0 | **27.0** |
| 0.150 | 100 | 20.0 | 93.0 | **7.0** |
| 0.075 | 30 | 6.0 | 99.0 | **1.0** |
| Pan | 5 | 1.0 | 100.0 | **0.0** |

**Step 5 — Determine $D_{60}$ (the particle size at 60 % finer):**

60 % finer lies between 0.600 mm ($P_F = 55\%$) and 0.425 mm ($P_F = 39\%$). Since 60 > 55, we need a size **larger** than 0.600 mm, i.e., we interpolate between 0.600 and **1.180 mm** ($P_F = 75\%$).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\log(D_{60}) &= \log(0.600) + \frac{55 - 60}{55 - 75} \times \left[\log(1.180) - \log(0.600)\right] \\
\log(D_{60}) &= (-0.2218) + \frac{-5}{-20} \times \left[(0.0719) - (-0.2218)\right] \\
\log(D_{60}) &= (-0.2218) + (0.25) \times (0.2937) \\
\log(D_{60}) &= (-0.2218) + 0.0734 \\
\log(D_{60}) &= -0.1484 \\
D_{60} &= 10^{-0.1484} \approx 0.711 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Determine $D_{30}$ (the particle size at 30 % finer):**

30 % finer lies between 0.425 mm ($P_F = 39\%$) and 0.300 mm ($P_F = 27\%$). Since 30 < 39, we interpolate between 0.425 and 0.300 mm.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\log(D_{30}) &= \log(0.425) + \frac{39 - 30}{39 - 27} \times \left[\log(0.300) - \log(0.425)\right] \\
\log(D_{30}) &= (-0.3716) + \frac{9}{12} \times \left[(-0.5229) - (-0.3716)\right] \\
\log(D_{30}) &= (-0.3716) + (0.75) \times (-0.1513) \\
\log(D_{30}) &= (-0.3716) + (-0.1135) \\
\log(D_{30}) &= -0.4851 \\
D_{30} &= 10^{-0.4851} \approx 0.327 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Determine $D_{10}$ (the particle size at 10 % finer):**

10 % finer lies between 0.300 mm ($P_F = 27\%$) and 0.150 mm ($P_F = 7\%$). Since 10 < 27, we interpolate between 0.300 and 0.150 mm.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\log(D_{10}) &= \log(0.300) + \frac{27 - 10}{27 - 7} \times \left[\log(0.150) - \log(0.300)\right] \\
\log(D_{10}) &= (-0.5229) + \frac{17}{20} \times \left[(-0.8239) - (-0.5229)\right] \\
\log(D_{10}) &= (-0.5229) + (0.85) \times (-0.3010) \\
\log(D_{10}) &= (-0.5229) + (-0.2559) \\
\log(D_{10}) &= -0.7788 \\
D_{10} &= 10^{-0.7788} \approx 0.1665 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 8 — Compute Coefficients $C_u$ and $C_c$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_u &= \frac{D_{60}}{D_{10}} = \frac{0.711}{0.1665} \approx 4.27 \\
C_c &= \frac{(D_{30})^2}{D_{10} \times D_{60}} = \frac{(0.327)^2}{0.1665 \times 0.711} = \frac{0.1069}{0.1184} \approx 0.903
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 9 — Classification (IS 1498 – 1970):**

> [!NOTE]
> - $C_u \approx 4.27 < 6$ (fails the $C_u \geq 6$ criterion for sands)
> - $C_c \approx 0.90$ is **not** within $1 \leq C_c \leq 3$
> - **Conclusion: Poorly-graded sand (SP)** under ISCS.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Why the Interpolation Formula Works

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The grain-size curve is **linear only on a semi-log plot** (% finer arithmetic, particle size logarithmic). This is because particle sizes span orders of magnitude. Direct linear interpolation in the particle-size domain gives **wrong** results. Always interpolate using $\log(D)$ values.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Slope between two points} &= \frac{\log(D_2) - \log(D_1)}{P_{F,2} - P_{F,1}} \\
\text{Offset for target } P_F &= \frac{P_{F,1} - P_{F,target}}{P_{F,1} - P_{F,2}} \\
\log(D_{target}) &= \log(D_1) + \text{Slope} \times \text{Offset}
\end{aligned}
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid — Laboratory Procedure Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["START: Receive Soil Sample"] --> B["Oven Dry at 105 to 110 deg C for 16 to 24 hrs"]
    B --> C["Weigh 500 g of Dry Soil as M total"]
    C --> D["Disaggregate with Rubber Pestle in Mortar"]
    D --> E["Stack IS Sieves in Descending Aperture Order Top to Bottom"]
    E --> F["Pour Soil onto Top Sieve 4.75 mm"]
    F --> G["Place Lid and Pan Assemble on Mechanical Shaker"]
    G --> H["Shake for 10 to 15 minutes at 300 rpm"]
    H --> I["Weigh Soil Retained on Each Sieve as M R i"]
    I --> J["Check Mass Balance Total M R equals M total within 1 percent"]
    J --> K["Compute P R, C R, and P F for Each Sieve"]
    K --> L["Plot Grain Size Distribution on Semi Log Graph"]
    L --> M["Read D10, D30, D60 from Curve"]
    M --> N["Compute Coefficient of Uniformity C u"]
    N --> O["Compute Coefficient of Curvature C c"]
    O --> P["Classify Soil as GW, GP, SW, SP, etc per IS 1498"]
    P --> Q["END: Report Results"]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid — Sieve Stack Cross-Section (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Stack["IS SIEVE STACK (Top to Bottom)"]
        S1["Lid (Cover)"]
        S2["Sieve 4.75 mm No 4"]
        S3["Sieve 2.36 mm No 8"]
        S4["Sieve 1.18 mm No 16"]
        S5["Sieve 0.600 mm No 30"]
        S6["Sieve 0.425 mm No 36"]
        S7["Sieve 0.300 mm No 50"]
        S8["Sieve 0.150 mm No 100"]
        S9["Sieve 0.075 mm No 200"]
        S10["Receiving Pan (Silt + Clay)"]
    end
    subgraph Out["Sieve Outputs (after shaking)"]
        R1["Mass Retained Coarse Gravel"]
        R2["Mass Retained Fine Gravel"]
        R3["Mass Retained Coarse Sand"]
        R4["Mass Retained Medium Sand"]
        R5["Mass Retained Fine Sand"]
        R6["Mass Retained Silt + Clay in Pan"]
    end
    S2 --> R1
    S3 --> R2
    S4 --> R3
    S5 --> R4
    S8 --> R5
    S10 --> R6
```

### 4.3 Block Architecture — Data Reduction Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN["Raw Lab Data: Mass Retained on each sieve"] --> PROC["Step 1: Percentage Retained P R"]
    PROC --> STEP2["Step 2: Cumulative P R"]
    STEP2 --> STEP3["Step 3: Percentage Finer P F"]
    STEP3 --> PLOT["Step 4: Plot Semi Log Curve"]
    PLOT --> READ["Step 5: Read D10, D30, D60 graphically"]
    READ --> CU["Step 6: Compute C u = D60 divided by D10"]
    READ --> CC["Step 7: Compute C c = D30 squared divided by D10 times D60"]
    CU --> CLS["Step 8: IS 1498 Classification"]
    CC --> CLS
    CLS --> OUT["Output: Soil Symbol e.g. SP or SW"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading aid:** The arrows in Mermaid diagrams above trace the **physical data flow** of the experiment — from raw balance readings all the way to the final ISCS classification symbol. This is the same sequence KTU examiners expect in a Part-B (14-mark) full experiment write-up.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**What is sieve analysis? State the IS code and the range of particle sizes for which this test is applicable.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Sieve analysis is a laboratory method used to determine the **particle size distribution of coarse-grained soils** by passing a known dry mass of soil through a stack of sieves arranged in descending order of aperture size.

- **IS Code:** **IS 2720 (Part 4) – 1985** (test method) using sieves as per **IS 460**.
- **Applicable particle size range:** **75 µm to 100 mm** (i.e., the sand and gravel fraction).
- Particles finer than 75 µm are determined separately by **sedimentation (hydrometer) analysis**, as sieving is ineffective for fines.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define the terms (a) Coefficient of Uniformity ($C_u$), and (b) Coefficient of Curvature ($C_c$). What are their typical values for a well-graded sand?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **(a) Coefficient of Uniformity $C_u$:** Defined as the ratio of the 60 %-finer size to the 10 %-finer size:
$$C_u = \dfrac{D_{60}}{D_{10}}$$

- **(b) Coefficient of Curvature $C_c$:** Defined as:
$$C_c = \dfrac{(D_{30})^2}{D_{10} \cdot D_{60}}$$

- **For a well-graded sand (IS 1498 – 1970):** $C_u \geq 6$ **and** $1 \leq C_c \leq 3$. Both conditions must be satisfied simultaneously.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full Descriptive Question (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Mass balance error:** Examiners deduct 1 mark if the sum of masses retained does **not** equal the original dry mass — *always* perform the mass balance check.
> 2. **Log-interpolation sign error:** The most common mistake is flipping the numerator/denominator during log-interpolation. Use the *correct orientation* — the formula in Section 2 must be applied consistently.
> 3. **Classification criteria mix-up:** Students confuse the $C_u$ threshold (4 for gravels vs. 6 for sands). *Always check the soil type first* using the dominant size before applying $C_u$ criteria.
> 4. **Forgetting to plot on semi-log paper:** A grain-size curve on **linear-linear paper is meaningless** in geotechnics.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Full Sieve Analysis Computation**

`[KTU University Exam – July 2024 Model]`

**(a)** A **600 g** oven-dried soil sample was passed through the standard IS sieves. The masses retained on the sieves (in mm) are: 4.75 mm → 0 g; 2.36 mm → 60 g; 1.18 mm → 90 g; 0.600 mm → 120 g; 0.425 mm → 96 g; 0.300 mm → 72 g; 0.150 mm → 120 g; 0.075 mm → 36 g; Pan → 6 g. Determine the percentage retained, cumulative percentage retained, and percentage finer for each sieve. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** From the results of part (a), determine $D_{10}$, $D_{30}$, $D_{60}$, and compute the coefficients of uniformity and curvature. Classify the soil as per **IS 1498 – 1970**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Tabulation (7 marks):**

- $M_{total} = 0+60+90+120+96+72+120+36+6 = 600$ g ✓
- $P_{R,i} = (M_{R,i} / 600) \times 100$

| Sieve (mm) | $M_R$ (g) | $P_R$ (%) | $C_R$ (%) | $P_F$ (%) |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 4.75 | 0 | 0.00 | 0.00 | 100.00 |
| 2.36 | 60 | 10.00 | 10.00 | 90.00 |
| 1.18 | 90 | 15.00 | 25.00 | 75.00 |
| 0.600 | 120 | 20.00 | 45.00 | 55.00 |
| 0.425 | 96 | 16.00 | 61.00 | 39.00 |
| 0.300 | 72 | 12.00 | 73.00 | 27.00 |
| 0.150 | 120 | 20.00 | 93.00 | 7.00 |
| 0.075 | 36 | 6.00 | 99.00 | 1.00 |
| Pan | 6 | 1.00 | 100.00 | 0.00 |

**Note:** These are the same data (proportionally scaled) used in the Section 3 worked example — values are consistent.

**(b) Determination of $D_{10}$, $D_{30}$, $D_{60}$ and classification (7 marks):**

Using the log-interpolation from the table above:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{60} &\approx 0.711 \text{ mm (interpolated between 0.600 and 1.180 mm)} \\
D_{30} &\approx 0.327 \text{ mm (interpolated between 0.425 and 0.300 mm)} \\
D_{10} &\approx 0.1665 \text{ mm (interpolated between 0.300 and 0.150 mm)} \\
C_u &= \frac{D_{60}}{D_{10}} = \frac{0.711}{0.1665} \approx 4.27 \\
C_c &= \frac{(D_{30})^2}{D_{10} \cdot D_{60}} = \frac{(0.327)^2}{0.1665 \times 0.711} \approx 0.903
\end{aligned}
$$

**Classification (IS 1498 – 1970):**
- Dominant fraction lies between 4.75 mm and 75 µm → **Sand (S)**.
- $C_u = 4.27 < 6$ → **fails** the well-graded criterion.
- $C_c = 0.903$ — **not** within $1 \leq C_c \leq 3$ → **fails** also.

**Result: Poorly-graded sand — SP.**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **standard set of IS sieves** used for the grain size analysis of soils. State the IS code for the test and the minimum mass of soil required for (i) a sandy soil and (ii) a gravelly soil. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The grain size analysis of a soil sample gave the following data: $D_{10} = 0.18$ mm, $D_{30} = 0.40$ mm, $D_{60} = 0.85$ mm. Compute $C_u$ and $C_c$. Comment on the gradation, and classify the soil. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Apparatus & Procedure (7 marks):**

- The standard set of IS sieves for grain size analysis consists of sieves with the following apertures (in mm): **4.75, 2.36, 1.18, 0.600, 0.425, 0.300, 0.150, 0.075** (No. 200), plus a lid and a receiving pan.
- **IS Code for the test:** **IS 2720 (Part 4) – 1985.**
- **IS Code for sieves:** **IS 460.**
- **Minimum sample mass:**
  - (i) Sandy soil: **500 g** of oven-dried material.
  - (ii) Gravelly soil: **5 to 10 kg** of oven-dried material (because of large particle sizes).
- **Procedure summary:** Oven-dry at 105–110 °C → weigh → disaggregate → stack sieves top to bottom (largest to smallest aperture) → pour sample on top sieve → mechanical shaking for 10–15 min → weigh retained soil on each sieve → compute $P_F$ → plot semi-log grain-size curve → read $D_{10}$, $D_{30}$, $D_{60}$.

**(b) Computation of $C_u$, $C_c$ and classification (7 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_u &= \frac{D_{60}}{D_{10}} = \frac{0.85}{0.18} \approx 4.72 \\
C_c &= \frac{(D_{30})^2}{D_{10} \times D_{60}} = \frac{(0.40)^2}{0.18 \times 0.85} = \frac{0.160}{0.153} \approx 1.046
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comment on gradation:**
- Since $C_u = 4.72$ — if the soil is a sand, it must be $\geq 6$ for well-graded → **fails** for sand.
- $C_c = 1.046$ lies within $1 \leq C_c \leq 3$ → **satisfied**.
- Overall: **the soil does not meet both criteria simultaneously** for a well-graded sand.

**If classified as sand:** $C_u = 4.72 < 6$ → **Poorly-graded sand — SP.**
**If classified as gravel:** $C_u = 4.72 \geq 4$ and $C_c$ is in range → **Well-graded gravel — GW** (provided the dominant size is gravel).

**Final answer depends on the dominant particle fraction** — the student must first identify whether the soil is sand or gravel from the curve before applying the criteria.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density revision checklist — re-read this 5 minutes before the exam:**

- **Applicable code:** Test → **IS 2720 (Part 4) – 1985**; Sieves → **IS 460**; Classification → **IS 1498 – 1970**.
- **Lower sieve limit:** **75 µm (No. 200)**. Below this, use **wet (hydrometer) analysis**.
- **Required drying:** Oven at **105 °C – 110 °C** for **16 – 24 h**.
- **Sample masses:** **~500 g for sand**, **5–10 kg for gravel**.
- **Four core quantities** (always compute in this order):
  (i) $P_R = (M_R / M_{total}) \times 100$
  (ii) $C_R = \Sigma P_R$
  (iii) $P_F = 100 - C_R$
  (iv) Plot $P_F$ vs. $\log(D)$ on **semi-log paper**.
- **Three characteristic sizes:** $D_{10}$, $D_{30}$, $D_{60}$ — read from the **grain-size curve** (use log-interpolation between plotted points).
- **Coefficient of Uniformity:** $C_u = D_{60} / D_{10}$.
- **Coefficient of Curvature:** $C_c = (D_{30})^2 / (D_{10} \times D_{60})$.
- **Well-graded criteria (IS 1498):**
  - For **gravels:** $C_u \geq 4$ **and** $1 \leq C_c \leq 3$.
  - For **sands:** $C_u \geq 6$ **and** $1 \leq C_c \leq 3$.
- **Mass balance check:** $\Sigma M_R = M_{total} \pm 1\%$.
- **Always use log-interpolation** when reading $D_N$ from the curve — direct linear interpolation gives wrong values.
- **Soil classification symbols (ISCS):** **GW** (well-graded gravel), **GP** (poorly-graded gravel), **SW** (well-graded sand), **SP** (poorly-graded sand).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of Specific Gravity - Pycnometer & Specific Gravity Bottle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCEL507 - GEOTECHNICAL ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of Specific Gravity - Pycnometer & Specific Gravity Bottle

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Specific Gravity (G or G<sub>s</sub>)** of soil solids is formally defined as the ratio of the weight (or mass) of a given volume of soil solids to the weight (or mass) of an equal volume of distilled water, the latter being measured at a standard reference temperature of **27 °C** (as per Indian Standard specifications for tropical climatic conditions).

$$G_s = \frac{\text{Weight of soil solids of given volume}}{\text{Weight of equal volume of distilled water at } 27^\circ\text{C}}$$

In precise mathematical terms, it is the ratio of the **mass density of the soil solids (ρ<sub>s</sub>)** to the **mass density of water (ρ<sub>w</sub>)** at 27 °C:

$$G_s = \frac{\rho_s}{\rho_w}$$

Since both numerator and denominator have identical units of mass/volume, **Specific Gravity is a dimensionless (pure) number**, and for most natural soils it lies between **2.65 and 2.80**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** As per **IS 2720 (Part 3/Sec 1) – 1980 (Reaffirmed 2002)**, the test shall be conducted at a constant temperature of 27 °C. Examiners frequently award partial credit when students correctly cite the IS code and reference temperature.

## 1.2 The Pycnometer and the Specific Gravity Bottle

A **Pycnometer** is a thin-walled, accurately calibrated glass flask of approximately **500 mL to 1000 mL** capacity, fitted with a ground-glass stopper that contains a small capillary hole running through its centre. This capillary hole is the defining feature of the apparatus — it allows air and excess water to escape during filling, ensuring a **constant, precise volume** of liquid (and thus soil + water) inside the bottle at any given reference temperature.

A **Specific Gravity Bottle** is a smaller version of the same apparatus, typically of **50 mL to 250 mL** capacity, used when only a small quantity of oven-dried soil is available (typical for fine-grained soils or when working with limited samples).

> [!NOTE]
> **Apparatus Identification Tip for KTU Viva:**
> - **Capacity > 500 mL → Pycnometer** (used for coarser soils or larger samples)
> - **Capacity ≤ 250 mL → Specific Gravity Bottle** (used for fine-grained soils like clays, silts, or limited samples)
> 
> Both function identically; only the volume capacity differs.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Floating Crystal" Intuition

Imagine you have a tiny, perfectly sealed crystal of soil that contains no air bubbles inside it. You drop this crystal into a glass filled *exactly* to the brim with water. The water that overflows and spills out represents the **volume** of that crystal.

Now imagine that:
- The crystal weighed **W<sub>s</sub>**
- The same volume of pure water (the overflow) weighed **W<sub>w</sub>**

Then the ratio **W<sub>s</sub> / W<sub>w</sub>** tells you *how many times "heavier" the soil particles are compared to water*. If the number is 2.70, the soil is **2.70 times denser than water**.

This is exactly what the pycnometer measures — it precisely determines the **mass of soil solids alone** and the **mass of the displaced water** (i.e., the mass of water occupying the same volume as the soil solids), and divides them.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world Connection:** Engineers use G<sub>s</sub> in computing the **void ratio (e)**, **porosity (n)**, **degree of saturation (S)**, and **unit weight of solids (γ<sub>s</sub>)**. A small error in G<sub>s</sub> propagates through every subsequent index property calculation, which is why IS 2720 mandates such precise volumetric measurement using the calibrated pycnometer bottle.

## 1.4 Standard Reference Values (For Quick Sanity Check)

| Soil Type | Typical G<sub>s</sub> Range |
|---|---|
| Sand | 2.65 – 2.70 |
| Silt | 2.67 – 2.72 |
| Clay | 2.70 – 2.80 |
| Organic soil / Peat | 1.90 – 2.40 |
| Iron-rich laterite | 2.80 – 3.00 |
| Pure quartz sand | 2.65 – 2.66 |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Principle of the Experiment

The experiment relies on the **Archimedes' Principle of Buoyancy** — the apparent loss of weight of a body when immersed in a fluid equals the weight of the fluid displaced. The pycnometer method is essentially a **constant-volume displacement method**, in which:

1. A precisely known **volume (V)** of the bottle is filled with water alone → mass **W<sub>1</sub>** is recorded.
2. The bottle is emptied, and a known mass of **oven-dried soil (W<sub>s</sub>)** is introduced.
3. The bottle is then filled again with water to the same calibrated volume V → mass **W<sub>2</sub>** is recorded.
4. The mass of the bottle + water + soil together → **W<sub>3</sub>** (or **W<sub>4</sub>** depending on notation).

The key insight: when soil occupies part of the calibrated volume V, **less water** can fit inside. The difference in water mass between the two fill-ups gives the mass of water displaced by the soil solids alone.

## 2.2 The Two Standard Observation Sequences (KTU Pattern)

### 2.2.1 Sequence A — Empty Bottle Reference (Most Common in KTU Labs)

| Observation | Mass Recorded | Symbol |
|---|---|---|
| Mass of empty, clean, dry pycnometer | m<sub>1</sub> | m<sub>1</sub> |
| Mass of pycnometer + oven-dry soil | m<sub>2</sub> | m<sub>2</sub> |
| Mass of pycnometer + soil + water (filled to mark) | m<sub>3</sub> | m<sub>3</sub> |
| Mass of pycnometer + water only (filled to mark) | m<sub>4</sub> | m<sub>4</sub> |

From these:
- Mass of dry soil: W<sub>s</sub> = m<sub>2</sub> − m<sub>1</sub>
- Mass of water filling the bottle: W<sub>w(full)</sub> = m<sub>4</sub> − m<sub>1</sub>
- Mass of water actually filling the bottle when soil is inside: W<sub>w(soil)</sub> = m<sub>3</sub> − m<sub>2</sub>
- Mass of water displaced (equal volume to soil solids): W<sub>disp</sub> = (m<sub>4</sub> − m<sub>1</sub>) − (m<sub>3</sub> − m<sub>2</sub>)

### 2.2.2 Sequence B — Bottle + Water Reference

Some textbooks use W<sub>1</sub>, W<sub>2</sub>, W<sub>3</sub>, W<sub>4</sub> where the reference weight is taken with water already in the bottle. The formula is mathematically identical.

## 2.3 Why Each Step Matters (KTU Reasoning)

- **Oven-drying the soil at 105 °C to 110 °C for 24 hours** removes *all* free and adsorbed moisture, ensuring that the soil mass we weigh is **truly the mass of solids only**, with no trapped water inflating the result.
- **De-aired / boiled distilled water** is used to prevent air bubbles clinging to soil particles, because any trapped air would *artificially increase* the displaced volume, *decreasing* the calculated G<sub>s</sub> (a classic error).
- **Constant temperature of 27 °C** ensures ρ<sub>w</sub> is known and reproducible. ρ<sub>w</sub> at 27 °C ≈ **0.9965 g/cm³**, but since both numerator and denominator use the *same* water, the value of ρ<sub>w</sub> cancels out and G<sub>s</sub> becomes strictly a mass ratio.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Specific Gravity | $G_s = \dfrac{W_s}{W_{w,\text{displaced}}} = \dfrac{W_s \cdot \rho_w}{V_s \cdot \rho_w}$ | Dimensionless |
| 2 | Mass of soil solids | $W_s = m_2 - m_1$ | grams |
| 3 | Mass of water displaced | $W_{\text{disp}} = (m_4 - m_1) - (m_3 - m_2)$ | grams |
| 4 | Mass of water filling empty bottle | $W_{w,\text{full}} = m_4 - m_1$ | grams |
| 5 | Mass of water when soil is present | $W_{w,\text{soil}} = m_3 - m_2$ | grams |
| 6 | Volume of pycnometer | $V = \dfrac{m_4 - m_1}{\rho_w}$ | cm³ or mL |
| 7 | Volume of soil solids | $V_s = \dfrac{W_{\text{disp}}}{\rho_w}$ | cm³ |
| 8 | Density of soil solids | $\rho_s = \dfrac{W_s}{V_s}$ | g/cm³ |
| 9 | G<sub>s</sub> — Final Working Formula | $G_s = \dfrac{m_2 - m_1}{(m_4 - m_1) - (m_3 - m_2)}$ | **Most used in KTU exams** |
| 10 | Temperature correction (if test not at 27 °C) | $G_s^{27} = G_s^T \cdot \rho_w^T / \rho_w^{27}$ | ρ<sub>w</sub> in g/cm³ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Table Formatting Note:** All absolute values and "given" delimiters are written using `\vert` notation in the LaTeX equations (e.g., $\vert G_s \vert$) to keep the markdown table parser intact. **Do not use the raw pipe `|` symbol inside the table cells** — it will break the column structure.

## 2.5 Engineering Utility of Specific Gravity

Specific Gravity is **not** an end in itself — it is a **gateway parameter** used to compute almost every other phase-relationship property in soil mechanics:

- **Void ratio (e)** = $(G_s \cdot \gamma_w / \gamma_d) - 1$
- **Porosity (n)** = $e / (1 + e)$
- **Degree of saturation (S)** = $w \cdot G_s / e$ (where *w* is water content)
- **Unit weight of solids (γ<sub>s</sub>)** = $G_s \cdot \gamma_w$
- **Relative density (D<sub>r</sub>)** for granular soils (uses *e<sub>min</sub>*, *e<sub>max</sub>*, which themselves depend on G<sub>s</sub>)

A single misreading of G<sub>s</sub> in a soil report can cascade into incorrect design parameters for **bearing capacity, settlement, slope stability, and earth-pressure calculations** — which is why KTU examiners mark this experiment for *precision and IS-code compliance*.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Working Formula

Let us define each mass term rigorously and derive the expression for G<sub>s</sub> step by step.

### Step 1 — Define the bottle volume V

The pycnometer is a rigid glass vessel of fixed internal volume V (calibrated at 27 °C). When filled completely with distilled water of density ρ<sub>w</sub>, the mass of water it contains is:

$$
m_{\text{water,full}} = m_4 - m_1
$$

Therefore the **calibrated volume** of the bottle is:

$$
V = \frac{m_4 - m_1}{\rho_w}
$$

### Step 2 — Define the mass of dry soil solids

The mass of oven-dried soil introduced into the bottle is the difference between the bottle-with-soil mass and the empty bottle mass:

$$
W_s = m_2 - m_1
$$

### Step 3 — Define the mass of water present when soil is inside the bottle

When the bottle is filled with both soil and water, the total mass m<sub>3</sub> comprises: empty bottle (m<sub>1</sub>) + soil (W<sub>s</sub>) + water occupying the remaining volume. Hence, the mass of water alone is:

$$
m_{\text{water,soil}} = m_3 - m_2
$$

This represents the **mass of water filling the void spaces between soil particles plus the volume above the soil up to the calibration mark**, i.e., the *complement* of soil volume inside the bottle.

### Step 4 — Determine the mass of water displaced by the soil solids

The volume of the bottle V is fixed. Before soil was added, water of mass (m<sub>4</sub> − m<sub>1</sub>) filled the entire bottle. After soil is added, only water of mass (m<sub>3</sub> − m<sub>2</sub>) can fit in the remaining space. The *deficit* is the mass of water that would have occupied the **same volume as the soil solids**:

$$
m_{\text{water,displaced}} = (m_4 - m_1) - (m_3 - m_2)
$$

### Step 5 — Apply the definition of specific gravity

By definition, the specific gravity of soil solids equals the ratio of the mass of soil solids to the mass of an equal volume of water:

$$
G_s = \frac{W_s}{m_{\text{water,displaced}}}
$$

### Step 6 — Substitute all measured quantities

Substituting the expressions from Steps 2 and 4:

$$
G_s = \frac{m_2 - m_1}{(m_4 - m_1) - (m_3 - m_2)}
$$

### Step 7 — Simplify the denominator algebraically

Expanding the denominator:

$$
(m_4 - m_1) - (m_3 - m_2) = m_4 - m_1 - m_3 + m_2
$$

Reordering:

$$
G_s = \frac{m_2 - m_1}{m_2 + m_4 - m_1 - m_3}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **canonical KTU formula** that examiners expect students to write in the answer booklet. Memorize the form: **(soil mass) / (water-mass-difference)**.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Numerical Worked Example

A KTU-style numerical is illustrated below. Use this as a template for any calculation that appears in the exam.

**Given observations** (oven-dried soil, room temperature 27 °C):

| Observation | Mass (g) |
|---|---|
| m<sub>1</sub> — Empty pycnometer | 145.20 |
| m<sub>2</sub> — Pycnometer + dry soil | 245.60 |
| m<sub>3</sub> — Pycnometer + soil + water | 625.40 |
| m<sub>4</sub> — Pycnometer + water only | 580.10 |

### Step-by-step Evaluation

**Step A** — Mass of soil solids:

$$
W_s = m_2 - m_1 = 245.60 - 145.20 = 100.40 \text{ g}
$$

**Step B** — Mass of water filling the empty bottle:

$$
W_{w,\text{full}} = m_4 - m_1 = 580.10 - 145.20 = 434.90 \text{ g}
$$

**Step C** — Mass of water in bottle with soil present:

$$
W_{w,\text{soil}} = m_3 - m_2 = 625.40 - 245.60 = 379.80 \text{ g}
$$

**Step D** — Mass of water displaced (equal to mass of equal-volume water):

$$
W_{\text{disp}} = 434.90 - 379.80 = 55.10 \text{ g}
$$

**Step E** — Specific gravity:

$$
G_s = \frac{W_s}{W_{\text{disp}}} = \frac{100.40}{55.10} = 1.822
$$

> [!CAUTION]
> **Sanity check:** A value of 1.82 is unrealistically low for soil — typical soils give 2.65–2.80. This implies either an experimental error (trapped air, soil not fully de-aired, or temperature deviation) **or** it is an organic/peat sample. Always cross-check the result against the soil's known geological origin.

## 3.3 Two-Reading Shortcut Formula (For Quick Verification)

Many KTU textbooks (Punmia, Gopal Ranjan, Arora) present an alternative formula when the empty-bottle mass is **not** recorded:

$$
G_s = \frac{W_2 - W_1}{W_4 - W_3}
$$

where:
- W<sub>1</sub> = Mass of bottle + water (at calibration mark)
- W<sub>2</sub> = Mass of bottle + soil + water (at calibration mark)
- W<sub>3</sub> = Mass of bottle + soil + water (at calibration mark) — *after removing some water and adding soil*
- W<sub>4</sub> = Mass of bottle + water (at calibration mark) — *re-filled to mark*

This form is **algebraically identical** to the primary formula and is useful for **re-checking** your calculation in the lab record.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Apparatus Identification — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid block represents the **functional architecture** of the pycnometer apparatus and its role in the experiment. This replaces a physical line drawing (which Mermaid cannot render) with a process-flow schematic of the test procedure.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Clean and dry the pycnometer] --> B[Step 2: Record empty mass m1]
    B --> C[Step 3: Add oven-dry soil 5 to 10 g for fine soil / 20 to 50 g for coarse soil]
    C --> D[Step 4: Record bottle plus soil mass m2]
    D --> E[Step 5: Add deaired distilled water to cover soil]
    E --> F[Step 6: Apply vacuum or gentle boil to remove entrapped air]
    F --> G[Step 7: Top up to calibration mark with water at 27 C]
    G --> H[Step 8: Record bottle plus soil plus water mass m3]
    H --> I[Step 9: Empty bottle and rinse thoroughly]
    I --> J[Step 10: Refill bottle with deaired water to calibration mark]
    J --> K[Step 11: Record bottle plus water only mass m4]
    K --> L[Step 12: Apply working formula to compute Gs]
    L --> M[Step 13: Repeat for two more trials and report average Gs]
```

## 4.2 Mass-Tracking Schematic — The "Bottle-State" Model

The diagram below shows how the four mass readings (m<sub>1</sub>, m<sub>2</sub>, m<sub>3</sub>, m<sub>4</sub>) inter-relate as different *states* of the same bottle.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[State 1: Empty bottle m1] -->|+ soil| S2[State 2: Bottle plus soil m2]
    S1 -->|+ water| S4[State 4: Bottle plus water m4]
    S2 -->|+ water to mark| S3[State 3: Bottle plus soil plus water m3]
    S4 -.->|subtract| S3
    S2 -.->|subtract| S3
    S1 -.->|subtract| S4
```

## 4.3 Phase-Relationship Connection (Where G<sub>s</sub> Flows)

This schematic shows the downstream engineering properties that depend on G<sub>s</sub> — emphasizing why this single laboratory value is so important.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Gs[Specific Gravity Gs] --> E[Void Ratio e]
    Gs --> N[Porosity n]
    Gs --> S[Degree of Saturation S]
    Gs --> Gams[Unit Weight of Solids gamma_s]
    Gs --> Dr[Relative Density Dr for granular soils]
    E --> BC[Bearing Capacity]
    E --> ST[Settlement Analysis]
    E --> SL[Slope Stability]
    E --> EP[Earth Pressure]
    S --> Liquefaction[Liquefaction Assessment]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Viva Tip:** When asked *"Why is G<sub>s</sub> important?"*, walk the examiner through this flow — from G<sub>s</sub> → void ratio → bearing capacity/settlement. Examiners reward students who demonstrate awareness of *downstream applications* of a lab measurement.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Define Specific Gravity of soil solids. State the standard reference temperature at which it is determined as per IS code.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Specific gravity of soil solids (G<sub>s</sub>) is defined as the ratio of the mass of a given volume of soil solids to the mass of an equal volume of distilled water, both measured at the same temperature.

$$
G_s = \frac{W_s}{V_s \cdot \rho_w}
$$

As per **IS 2720 (Part 3/Sec 1) — 1980**, the standard reference temperature is **27 °C**. (1 mark for definition, 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for IS code + temperature.)

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**What is the role of the capillary hole in the stopper of a pycnometer?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The capillary hole passing through the ground-glass stopper of a pycnometer serves **two critical functions**:
1. It allows **trapped air and excess water to escape** when the stopper is inserted, ensuring the bottle is filled to a **precise, reproducible volume V** at the calibration mark.
2. It enables the bottle to be filled **identically each time** to the *same* calibration mark, regardless of minor variations in water volume added — this repeatability is the very basis of the constant-volume displacement method. (1.5 marks for function 1, 1.5 marks for function 2.)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the procedure for determining the specific gravity of soil solids using a **pycnometer**. State the relevant IS code. (7 marks)

**(b)** The following observations were recorded during a specific gravity test on a fine-grained soil using a 500 mL pycnometer:

- Mass of empty pycnometer, m<sub>1</sub> = 162.40 g
- Mass of pycnometer + dry soil, m<sub>2</sub> = 212.45 g
- Mass of pycnometer + soil + water, m<sub>3</sub> = 642.10 g
- Mass of pycnometer + water only, m<sub>4</sub> = 591.80 g

Calculate the specific gravity of the soil solids. Comment on the result. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution to A(a) — Procedure (7 marks)

**Apparatus Required (1 mark):** Pycnometer (500 mL), ground-glass stopper with capillary, weighing balance (accuracy 0.01 g), vacuum desiccator or boiling setup, distilled water, oven (105–110 °C), desiccator, thermometer.

**Procedure (5 marks):**
1. **Clean and dry** the pycnometer thoroughly. Weigh it with the stopper to get m<sub>1</sub>.
2. Take about **5–10 g** of fine soil (or 20–50 g for coarse soil) that has been oven-dried for 24 hours at 105–110 °C and cooled in a desiccator.
3. Transfer the soil into the pycnometer and weigh to get m<sub>2</sub>.
4. Add **deaired distilled water** to cover the soil (about half-full). Stir gently to dislodge trapped air.
5. **Apply vacuum** (or boil gently for 10 minutes, then cool) to remove all entrapped air bubbles.
6. Top up the pycnometer with deaired distilled water and insert the stopper. **Wipe dry** the outside. Weigh to get m<sub>3</sub>.
7. Empty the pycnometer, rinse it thoroughly, refill with deaired distilled water at **27 °C** up to the calibration mark via the capillary, wipe dry, and weigh to get m<sub>4</sub>.
8. **Repeat** the test for at least two additional trials and report the average G<sub>s</sub> to two decimal places.

**IS Code (1 mark):** IS 2720 (Part 3/Sec 1) — 1980 (Reaffirmed 2002). Reference temperature: 27 °C.

---

#### Model Solution to A(b) — Calculation (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Mass of dry soil solids (1 mark):**

$$
W_s = m_2 - m_1 = 212.45 - 162.40 = 50.05 \text{ g}
$$

**Step 2 — Mass of water filling the empty pycnometer (1 mark):**

$$
W_{w,\text{full}} = m_4 - m_1 = 591.80 - 162.40 = 429.40 \text{ g}
$$

**Step 3 — Mass of water in pycnometer when soil is present (1 mark):**

$$
W_{w,\text{soil}} = m_3 - m_2 = 642.10 - 212.45 = 429.65 \text{ g}
$$

**Step 4 — Mass of water displaced (equal to soil-solids volume) (1 mark):**

$$
W_{\text{disp}} = (m_4 - m_1) - (m_3 - m_2) = 429.40 - 429.65 = -0.25 \text{ g}
$$

**Step 5 — Re-check calculation (1 mark):**

Let us recompute using the alternative form:

$$
W_{\text{disp}} = (m_2 + m_4) - (m_1 + m_3) = (212.45 + 591.80) - (162.40 + 642.10) = 804.25 - 804.50 = -0.25 \text{ g}
$$

The result is **physically impossible** — a negative displaced mass cannot occur.

**Step 6 — Diagnose the error (2 marks):**

The negative sign indicates an **error in the observations**, most likely a **transcription error in m<sub>3</sub>**. The expected range of m<sub>3</sub> can be back-calculated. With W<sub>s</sub> = 50.05 g, and assuming a reasonable G<sub>s</sub> = 2.70:

$$
W_{\text{disp,expected}} = \frac{W_s}{G_s} = \frac{50.05}{2.70} = 18.54 \text{ g}
$$

Therefore, m<sub>3</sub> should have been approximately:

$$
m_3 = m_2 + (W_{w,\text{full}} - W_{\text{disp}}) = 212.45 + (429.40 - 18.54) = 212.45 + 410.86 = 623.31 \text{ g}
$$

**Conclusion:** The recorded value m<sub>3</sub> = 642.10 g is **inconsistent with physical reality** — the test must be repeated.

> [!CAUTION]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Read Carefully:**
> Always **check for the sign** of the displaced water mass. A negative value is a **red flag** indicating either a calculation slip or — more commonly in KTU answer sheets — a **mistranscribed observation**. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks if a student blindly reports a negative G<sub>s</sub> without commenting on the inconsistency.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]`

**(a)** Differentiate between a **pycnometer** and a **specific gravity bottle**. When is each preferred? List the sources of error in the pycnometer method. (7 marks)

**(b)** A specific gravity test was conducted on a sandy soil sample. The following readings were taken at 27 °C:

- Mass of empty bottle = 138.60 g
- Mass of bottle + dry soil = 388.60 g
- Mass of bottle + soil + water = 720.50 g
- Mass of bottle + water only = 565.00 g

Determine the specific gravity of the soil solids, the void ratio (given natural water content *w* = 12 % and bulk unit weight γ = 18.5 kN/m³), and the degree of saturation. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution to B(a) — Theory (7 marks)

**Distinction Table (3 marks):**

| Feature | Pycnometer | Specific Gravity Bottle |
|---|---|---|
| Capacity | 500 – 1000 mL | 50 – 250 mL |
| Preferred for | Coarse-grained soils, larger samples | Fine-grained soils, limited sample |
| Sensitivity | Lower (larger V → smaller relative error) | Higher (smaller V → larger relative error per gram) |
| Calibration precision | Slightly less critical | More critical (small absolute volume) |
| Stopper design | Same — capillary hole for air escape | Same — capillary hole for air escape |

**When to prefer each (2 marks):**
- **Pycnometer** → sandy or gravelly soils, field samples with abundant material, when the soil mass used is large (≥ 20 g).
- **Specific gravity bottle** → silts, clays, organic soils; when only a small oven-dried sample is available (≤ 10 g); for high-precision work on fine soils.

**Sources of error (2 marks):**
1. **Incomplete removal of entrapped air** (most common — leads to *lower* G<sub>s</sub>).
2. **Insufficient oven-drying** of the soil (residual moisture → *lower* apparent W<sub>s</sub> → *lower* G<sub>s</sub>).
3. **Temperature deviation** from 27 °C (alters ρ<sub>w</sub> and bottle volume).
4. **Inaccurate balance readings** (parallax, uncalibrated weights, balance drift).
5. **Soil loss during transfer** to the pycnometer.
6. **Improper filling** — water level not exactly at the calibration mark.

---

#### Model Solution to B(b) — Calculation (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Specific gravity (3 marks):**

$$
W_s = 388.60 - 138.60 = 250.00 \text{ g}
$$

$$
W_{w,\text{full}} = 565.00 - 138.60 = 426.40 \text{ g}
$$

$$
W_{w,\text{soil}} = 720.50 - 388.60 = 331.90 \text{ g}
$$

$$
W_{\text{disp}} = 426.40 - 331.90 = 94.50 \text{ g}
$$

$$
G_s = \frac{250.00}{94.50} = 2.645
$$

**Step 2 — Void ratio (2 marks):**

The relationship between dry unit weight, specific gravity, and void ratio is:

$$
\gamma_d = \frac{G_s \cdot \gamma_w}{1 + e}
$$

where the dry unit weight is computed from the bulk unit weight and water content:

$$
\gamma_d = \frac{\gamma}{1 + w} = \frac{18.5}{1 + 0.12} = 16.518 \text{ kN/m}^3
$$

Solving for *e*:

$$
1 + e = \frac{G_s \cdot \gamma_w}{\gamma_d} = \frac{2.645 \times 9.81}{16.518} = 1.571
$$

$$
e = 0.571
$$

**Step 3 — Degree of saturation (2 marks):**

Using the phase-relationship formula:

$$
S \cdot e = w \cdot G_s
$$

$$
S = \frac{w \cdot G_s}{e} = \frac{0.12 \times 2.645}{0.571} = \frac{0.3174}{0.571} = 0.556 = 55.6\%
$$

**Final Answers:** G<sub>s</sub> = 2.645, e = 0.571, S = 55.6 %.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Specific Gravity Problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract m<sub>1</sub>** when computing the displaced water mass — this is the #1 error. Always write the *full* expression $(m_4 - m_1) - (m_3 - m_2)$ in the answer booklet, not just $m_4 - m_3$.
> 2. **Mixing up the four m-values** when reading from the question paper. Label each reading with its symbol *before* starting any calculation.
> 3. **Neglecting units** in intermediate steps — examiners specifically look for "g" or "cm³" annotations.
> 4. **Not citing the IS code** in descriptive answers — 1 mark is reserved for "IS 2720 Part 3/Sec 1" plus reference temperature 27 °C.
> 5. **Reporting G<sub>s</sub> to excessive decimal places** (e.g., 2.6453) — KTU expects **two decimal places** (e.g., 2.65). Anything beyond is unnecessary and may suggest use of a calculator without understanding.
> 6. **Failing to comment** on unusual results (G<sub>s</sub> < 2.5 or > 3.0). Always interpret the result — does it match the soil type?
> 7. **Confusing G<sub>s</sub> with bulk specific gravity** or **apparent specific gravity** — these are different concepts tested in advanced courses.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Specific Gravity by Pycnometer**

- **Definition (1 line):** G<sub>s</sub> = mass of soil solids / mass of equal volume of distilled water, both at 27 °C. **Dimensionless.**

- **IS Code:** IS 2720 (Part 3/Sec 1) — 1980 (Reaffirmed 2002). Reference temperature: **27 °C**.

- **Apparatus:** Pycnometer (500–1000 mL) or Specific Gravity Bottle (50–250 mL). Both have a **ground-glass stopper with a central capillary hole** that allows air/water escape for precise volume control.

- **Four Essential Readings:** m<sub>1</sub> (empty), m<sub>2</sub> (bottle + dry soil), m<sub>3</sub> (bottle + soil + water), m<sub>4</sub> (bottle + water only).

- **Master Formula (memorize this!):**

$$
\boxed{\;G_s = \dfrac{m_2 - m_1}{(m_4 - m_1) - (m_3 - m_2)}\;}
$$

- **Why deair the water?** Trapped air bubbles cling to soil particles, artificially increasing displaced volume and *lowering* G<sub>s</sub>.

- **Typical G<sub>s</sub> Ranges:** Sand 2.65–2.70, Silt 2.67–2.72, Clay 2.70–2.80, Organic soil < 2.40, Iron-rich soil > 2.80.

- **Downstream Properties That Use G<sub>s</sub>:** void ratio (e), porosity (n), degree of saturation (S), unit weight of solids (γ<sub>s</sub>), relative density (D<sub>r</sub>).

- **Lab Tip:** Take **at least 2–3 trials** and report the **average** G<sub>s</sub> to **two decimal places**. Outliers should be discarded with justification.

- **Sanity Check:** If G<sub>s</sub> < 2.5, suspect organic soil or experimental error. If G<sub>s</sub> > 3.0, suspect heavy minerals (iron, magnetite) — verify with geological origin.

- **Negative displaced water mass → STOP and re-check the readings.** This is a sign of either a transcription error or a balance malfunction.

- **Viva Power Phrases:** "constant-volume displacement method", "Archimedes' principle", "de-aired distilled water", "capillary calibration mark", "phase relationships".
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of Water Content - Oven Drying Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCEL507 - GEOTECHNICAL ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of Water Content - Oven Drying Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Water Content (w)** of a soil mass is defined as the **ratio of the mass of water** present in the soil voids to the **mass of solid particles (solids)**, expressed as a percentage. It is also commonly called **Natural Moisture Content (NMC)** or simply **Moisture Content (m.c.)**.

Mathematically, as per **IS 2720 (Part 2) - 1973** (the governing Indian Standard for laboratory determination of water content):

$$w = \frac{M_w}{M_s} \times 100 \, (\%) $$

Where:
- $w$ = Water content (%)
- $M_w$ = Mass of water present in the soil sample
- $M_s$ = Mass of solid particles (oven-dry soil)

Water content is a **dimensionless quantity** but is conventionally expressed in **percentage (%).** It can theoretically range from **0%** (completely dry soil, e.g., oven-bone-dry sand) to over **500%** (highly organic soils like peat).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Sponge Model

Imagine a **kitchen sponge** soaked in water. If you weigh the wet sponge, then squeeze out *all* the water and weigh the dry sponge alone, the difference gives you the weight of water. The **water content** is simply the weight of that water divided by the weight of the *dry* sponge (not the wet one).

The same logic applies to soil:
- The **dry soil skeleton** plays the role of the squeezed sponge.
- The **water in the voids** is what we are measuring.
- Crucially, we divide by the **mass of solids only**, not the total mass — this is a key conceptual point.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why divide by dry mass and not total mass?**
> Because the dry soil mass is the *constant* reference. The water mass varies with weather, drainage, and disturbance. The dry skeleton represents the *inherent mineral content* of the soil, providing a stable denominator for comparison.

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics

- **Standard Oven Temperature:** **105 °C to 110 °C** (strictly controlled)
- **Minimum Drying Duration:** **16 to 24 hours** (typically overnight)
- **Minimum Sample Mass:** As per IS code (depends on soil type and maximum particle size)
- **Balance Accuracy Required:** **0.01 g (for fine-grained)** to **0.1 g (for coarse/gravelly)** soils

## 1.4 Categories of Water Content Determination

| Method | Suitable For | Accuracy |
|---|---|---|
| **Oven Drying Method** | All soil types except those with organic matter, gypsum, or volatile substances | **Most accurate** (Standard reference) |
| Sand Bath Method | Field use, coarse-grained soils | Moderate |
| Infrared Lamp Method | Field/lab, quick results | Moderate |
| Torsion Balance Drying Method | Quick lab determination | Moderate to High |
| Calcium Carbide (Speedy) Method | Field use, very rapid | Approximate |
| Pycnometer Method | Cohesive soils in field | Moderate |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU 2024 lab examinations, the **Oven Drying Method is the standard** and is the only method accepted for end-semester practical record evaluation.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Background

Every soil in nature is a **three-phase system**: solid particles, water, and air. The water content tells us *how much* of the voids space is occupied by water (or how much water is chemically/physically bound to the soil grains).

In geotechnical engineering, water content is fundamental because it directly influences:

1. **Shear strength** of soil (more water = less strength in cohesive soils)
2. **Compressibility and settlement** behavior
3. **Compaction characteristics** (Optimum Moisture Content, OMC)
4. **Consolidation rate**
5. **Atterberg limits** (Liquid Limit, Plastic Limit, Shrinkage Limit)
6. **Bearing capacity** of foundations

## 2.2 Principles of the Oven Drying Method

The method is based on the following scientific principles:

- At a temperature of **105 °C to 110 °C**, **free water, capillary water, and most adsorbed water** are evaporated from the soil.
- This temperature is high enough to remove pore water but **low enough to prevent**:
  - Decomposition of organic matter
  - Loss of structural water from clay minerals
  - Oxidation reactions in the soil
- After drying, the soil mass becomes **constant (steady mass)**, confirming complete water removal.

> [!NOTE]
> **Exception — Soils NOT suitable for oven drying:**
> - Soils containing **organic matter** (peat, organic clays) — organic matter oxidizes and loses mass
> - Soils containing **gypsum** (CaSO₄·2H₂O) — gypsum loses its water of crystallization at 105 °C
> - Soils with **volatile substances** or **high salt content**
> For such soils, methods like the **Torsion Balance** or **Pycnometer** are recommended.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $w = \dfrac{M_w}{M_s} \times 100$ | Definition of water content | % |
| 2 | $M_w = M_2 - M_3$ | Mass of water = (Wet soil + container) − (Dry soil + container) | g |
| 3 | $M_s = M_3 - M_1$ | Mass of solids = (Dry soil + container) − (Empty container) | g |
| 4 | $w = \dfrac{(M_2 - M_3)}{(M_3 - M_1)} \times 100$ | Combined working formula | % |
| 5 | $\gamma_d = \dfrac{\gamma_b}{1 + w}$ | Relationship between dry unit weight and bulk unit weight | kN/m³ |
| 6 | $w \cdot G \cdot s_e = S \cdot e$ | Phase relationship: $wG_s = Se$ | — |

Where:
- $M_1$ = Mass of empty moisture can (with lid)
- $M_2$ = Mass of moisture can + wet soil
- $M_3$ = Mass of moisture can + oven-dry soil
- $G_s$ = Specific gravity of soil solids
- $S$ = Degree of saturation
- $e$ = Void ratio
- $\gamma_d$ = Dry unit weight
- $\gamma_b$ = Bulk unit weight

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Construction Quality Control:** Verifying moisture content at site before compaction of embankments, subgrades, and fills.
- **Slope Stability Analysis:** Pore-water pressure and hence factor of safety depend on water content.
- **Foundation Design:** Settlement predictions require knowledge of in-situ water content.
- **Earthquake Engineering:** Liquefaction susceptibility is linked to water content and saturation.
- **Agricultural & Pavement Design:** Subgrade strength and CBR values correlate with water content.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory aid for KTU Exam:** Always remember the three weights: **W1, W2, W3**. Water = (W2 − W3); Solids = (W3 − W1). This sequence is the most common source of silly mistakes.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Working Formula

Starting from the fundamental definition:

$$w = \frac{M_w}{M_s} \times 100$$

From direct weighing observations:

$$M_w = M_2 - M_3$$

$$M_s = M_3 - M_1$$

Substituting these into the basic equation:

$$w = \frac{M_2 - M_3}{M_3 - M_1} \times 100$$

This is the **standard working formula** used in KTU lab record calculations.

## 3.2 Detailed Procedure with Analytical Reasoning

### **Step 1: Preparation of Moisture Can**

Clean the moisture can (a small metal container with a tight-fitting lid) and dry it thoroughly. Number it for identification.

- **Why?** A wet/dirty can introduces error into $M_1$ and $M_3$.

### **Step 2: Weighing the Empty Can ($M_1$)**

Weigh the empty, clean, dry moisture can (with its lid) on a balance of accuracy **0.01 g**.

$$M_1 = \text{Mass of empty, clean, dry container (g)}$$

### **Step 3: Wet Soil Sampling**

Take a representative wet soil sample (as per IS 2720 Part 1). Place it in the can, close the lid to prevent moisture loss, and weigh immediately.

$$M_2 = \text{Mass of container + wet soil (g)}$$

- **Why close the lid?** Prevents evaporation of water during weighing — exposure to air causes water loss, lowering $M_2$.

### **Step 4: Oven Drying**

Place the can (lid removed or placed underneath) in a thermostatically controlled **oven at 105 °C to 110 °C** for **16 to 24 hours**.

- **Why 105 °C?** Removes all unbound and capillary water.
- **Why not above 110 °C?** Risks loss of structural water and organic matter decomposition.
- **Why 16+ hours?** Ensures constant mass (steady weight).

### **Step 5: Cooling in Desiccator**

After drying, transfer the can (with lid on) to a **desiccator** containing a desiccant (anhydrous CaCl₂ or silica gel) and allow it to cool to room temperature.

- **Why use a desiccator?** Hot soil in open air re-absorbs moisture rapidly, leading to errors in $M_3$.

### **Step 6: Reweighing ($M_3$)**

After cooling, weigh the can with the oven-dry soil.

$$M_3 = \text{Mass of container + oven-dry soil (g)}$$

### **Step 7: Calculation**

Apply the working formula and report the result to **two decimal places**.

## 3.3 Worked-Out Numerical Example

### **Problem Statement (KTU-style)**

In a laboratory determination of water content by the oven drying method, the following data were recorded:

- Mass of empty moisture can, $M_1 = 25.50$ g
- Mass of can + wet soil, $M_2 = 85.40$ g
- Mass of can + oven-dry soil, $M_3 = 78.20$ g

**Calculate the water content of the soil.**

### **Step-by-Step Solution**

**Step (a):** Calculate the mass of water present.

$$M_w = M_2 - M_3 = 85.40 - 78.20 = 7.20 \text{ g}$$

**Step (b):** Calculate the mass of solids.

$$M_s = M_3 - M_1 = 78.20 - 25.50 = 52.70 \text{ g}$$

**Step (c):** Apply the water content formula.

$$w = \frac{M_w}{M_s} \times 100 = \frac{7.20}{52.70} \times 100$$

$$w = 0.13662 \times 100 = 13.66 \, \%$$

### **Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{w = 13.66 \, \%}$$

## 3.4 Sample Calculation — Mass to Volume Conversion

If the bulk unit weight of soil is given as $\gamma_b = 18.5$ kN/m³, find the dry unit weight.

$$\gamma_d = \frac{\gamma_b}{1 + w} = \frac{18.5}{1 + 0.1366} = \frac{18.5}{1.1366}$$

$$\gamma_d = 16.27 \, \text{kN/m}^3$$

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Common Variants in KTU Lab Records

| Variant | Modification | Formula Adjustment |
|---|---|---|
| Wet soil with high organic content | Use temperature-controlled desiccator + vacuum oven | $w$ calculated with correction factor |
| Loss on ignition method | Burn soil at 440 °C | Organic content removed → uses $M_3$ after ignition |
| Field rapid method | Use Speedy moisture meter | Empirical chart-based conversion |
| Gypsum-rich soil | Use 60 °C oven | Lower temp to preserve gypsum-bound water |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Pattern:** Most questions test (i) the working formula, (ii) the calculation, and (iii) identification of the three weights. Master these three and you will not lose marks.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow of the Oven Drying Method

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **complete procedural workflow** of the oven drying method, with each operational step explicitly labeled. This is the standard flow expected in KTU lab records and viva voce.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[START Clean and Dry Moisture Can]
    A2[Weigh Empty Can as M1 using 0.01g balance]
    A3[Collect Representative Wet Soil Sample]
    A4[Place Wet Soil in Can and Close Lid]
    A5[Weigh Can plus Wet Soil as M2]
    A6[Open Lid and Place Can in Oven]
    A7[Maintain Temperature 105 to 110 Degree C]
    A8[Dry for 16 to 24 Hours]
    A9[Transfer Can with Lid to Desiccator]
    A10[Cool to Room Temperature]
    A11[Reweigh Can plus Dry Soil as M3]
    A12[Apply Formula w equals M2 minus M3 divided by M3 minus M1 times 100]
    A13[Report Water Content in Percentage]

    A1 --> A2
    A2 --> A3
    A3 --> A4
    A4 --> A5
    A5 --> A6
    A6 --> A7
    A7 --> A8
    A8 --> A9
    A9 --> A10
    A10 --> A11
    A11 --> A12
    A12 --> A13
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Apparatus Interconnection

The following diagram maps the **interconnection between the apparatus components** used in the oven drying method. It represents how the soil sample is processed through different instruments sequentially.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B1[Soil Sample Source]
    B2[Moisture Can with Lid]
    B3[Electronic Balance Accuracy 0.01g]
    B4[Thermostatic Oven 105 to 110 Degree C]
    B5[Desiccator with CaCl2]
    B6[Calculation Block Working Formula]
    B7[Output Water Content w in Percentage]

    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> B3
    B2 --> B4
    B4 --> B5
    B5 --> B3
    B3 --> B6
    B6 --> B7
```

## 4.3 Three-Phase Soil System — Concept Map

This conceptual block diagram shows **how water content relates to the three phases** of a soil system (solids, water, air), which is fundamental for deriving all phase relationships.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C1[Soil Mass Three Phase System]
    C2[SOLID PHASE Mass Ms]
    C3[WATER PHASE Mass Mw]
    C4[AIR PHASE Mass Ma Negligible]
    C5[Total Mass Mt equals Ms plus Mw]
    C6[Volume Relations Vs Vw Va]

    C1 --> C2
    C1 --> C3
    C1 --> C4
    C2 --> C5
    C3 --> C5
    C1 --> C6
```

## 4.4 List of Apparatus (KTU Viva Voce Essentials)

| S.No | Apparatus | Purpose | Specification |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Moisture Can** | Container for soil sample | Non-corrosive, with tight-fitting lid |
| 2 | **Thermostatic Oven** | Drying soil at controlled temperature | Maintains **105 °C to 110 °C** |
| 3 | **Electronic Balance** | Mass measurement | Accuracy **0.01 g** for fine soils |
| 4 | **Desiccator** | Cooling dry soil without moisture re-absorption | Contains anhydrous CaCl₂ / silica gel |
| 5 | **Tongs / Gloves** | Handling hot moisture cans | Heat-resistant |
| 6 | **Spatula** | Mixing & transferring soil | Stainless steel |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU Lab Exam:** When asked "Name the apparatus," always list all six. The **desiccator** is the most-forgotten item — mentioning it will earn you bonus marks in viva.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define water content of soil. State the standard temperature range used in the oven drying method as per IS 2720 (Part 2).**

**Model Answer:**

> Water content ($w$) of a soil is defined as the ratio of the mass of water ($M_w$) to the mass of solid particles ($M_s$) in the soil, expressed as a percentage. It is calculated using the formula $w = (M_w / M_s) \times 100$.
>
> As per **IS 2720 (Part 2) - 1973**, the standard oven drying method uses a temperature range of **105 °C to 110 °C** for drying the soil sample, maintained for a duration of **16 to 24 hours**, until a constant mass is achieved.

*(3 marks: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for IS code specifications)*

---

### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is the role of a desiccator in the oven drying method? Why can't we weigh the dry soil immediately after removing it from the hot oven?**

**Model Answer:**

> A **desiccator** is a sealed glass chamber containing a desiccant (anhydrous CaCl₂ or silica gel) that absorbs any atmospheric moisture. Its role is to **cool the hot oven-dry soil to room temperature without allowing it to re-absorb moisture** from the surrounding air.
>
> The dry soil cannot be weighed immediately after oven removal because:
> (i) **Hot air rises and creates buoyancy errors** in the weighing balance.
> (ii) **The hot soil rapidly absorbs moisture** from the atmosphere, increasing its mass.
> (iii) Handling a hot can with bare hands may transfer heat and oils, affecting the reading.

*(3 marks: 1 mark for desiccator role, 2 marks for the reasoning behind cooling)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Mistake 1:** Students often divide $M_w$ by $M_2$ (total wet mass) instead of $M_s$ (dry mass). This gives a *wrong* value. The denominator must be the mass of **solids** only.
> - **Mistake 2:** Forgetting units of the empty container ($M_1$) — they assume $M_1 = 0$ if not explicitly given, leading to inflated water content.
> - **Mistake 3:** Reporting $w$ without the **% sign** — KTU examiners deduct 0.5 to 1 mark for missing units.
> - **Mistake 4:** Not stating the **IS Code reference** in the procedure — KTU 2024 scheme explicitly awards 1 mark for the IS code citation.

---

### **Question A (Option 1): [KTU University Exam – Module Internal Choice]**

**(a)** Describe the oven drying method for the determination of water content of soil in the laboratory, with a neat labelled diagram of the apparatus setup. State the relevant IS code. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. IS Code Reference:** IS 2720 (Part 2) - 1973.

**2. Apparatus Required:**
- Moisture cans with tight-fitting lids
- Thermostatic oven (105 °C to 110 °C)
- Electronic balance (accuracy 0.01 g)
- Desiccator with anhydrous CaCl₂
- Tongs and spatula

**3. Procedure:**
1. Clean and dry the moisture can. Weigh the empty can with its lid → $M_1$.
2. Take a representative wet soil sample and place it in the can. Close the lid immediately to prevent moisture loss. Weigh → $M_2$.
3. Remove the lid and place the can in the oven at 105–110 °C for 16–24 hours.
4. After drying, close the lid, transfer to the desiccator, and cool to room temperature.
5. Reweigh the can with dry soil → $M_3$.

**4. Calculation:**

$$w = \frac{M_2 - M_3}{M_3 - M_1} \times 100 \, (\%)$$

**5. Precautions:**
- Lid must be tight during weighing of wet soil.
- Cooling must be in desiccator only.
- Repeat drying for 1 hour to verify constant mass.

**6. Diagram:** (Refer to the procedural flow diagram in Section 4.1.)

*(Mark split: 1 mark IS code + 2 marks apparatus + 2 marks procedure + 1 mark formula + 1 mark precautions)*

---

**(b)** The following observations were recorded during a water content determination test using the oven drying method. Calculate the water content of the soil. **(7 marks)**

| Observation | Mass (g) |
|---|---|
| Empty moisture can ($M_1$) | 28.40 |
| Can + wet soil ($M_2$) | 92.60 |
| Can + oven-dry soil ($M_3$) | 84.50 |

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Mass of water

$$M_w = M_2 - M_3 = 92.60 - 84.50 = 8.10 \text{ g}$$

**Step 2:** Mass of solids

$$M_s = M_3 - M_1 = 84.50 - 28.40 = 56.10 \text{ g}$$

**Step 3:** Water content

$$w = \frac{M_w}{M_s} \times 100 = \frac{8.10}{56.10} \times 100 = 14.44 \, \%$$

**Final Answer:** $\boxed{w = 14.44 \, \%}$

*(Mark split: 2 marks each for water mass, solid mass, and final calculation; 1 mark for correct unit/answer)*

---

### **Question B (Option 2 — Internal Choice): [KTU University Exam – Module Internal Choice]**

**(a)** State the IS code specifications for the oven drying method. List the types of soils for which this method is **NOT** recommended, with justification. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. IS Code Specifications (IS 2720 Part 2 – 1973):**
- Oven temperature: **105 °C to 110 °C**
- Drying duration: **16 to 24 hours**
- Minimum sample mass: As per maximum particle size (e.g., 25 g for fine soils, 300 g for gravelly soils)
- Balance accuracy: **0.01 g** (fine soils), **0.1 g** (coarse soils)
- Repeat drying for 1 hour; mass difference should be **< 0.1%** for confirmation of constant mass.

**2. Soils NOT Suitable for Oven Drying:**

| Soil Type | Reason for Incompatibility |
|---|---|
| Organic soils (peat) | Organic matter oxidizes at 105 °C, losing mass |
| Gypsum-bearing soils | Gypsum loses water of crystallization at 105 °C |
| Soils with high salt content | Salt mass changes due to chemical reactions |
| Soils with volatile constituents | Volatiles evaporate, giving false water content |

**3. Alternative Methods for Such Soils:**
- Torsion balance method
- Pycnometer method
- Sand bath method
- Calcium carbide (Speedy) method

*(Mark split: 3 marks IS specs + 2 marks unsuitable soils + 1 mark each for alternative methods = 1 mark)*

---

**(b)** A wet soil sample has a water content of 18% and a bulk unit weight of 19.2 kN/m³. Calculate: **(i)** Dry unit weight, **(ii)** Void ratio (assume $G_s = 2.65$), and **(iii)** Degree of saturation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Water content: $w = 18\% = 0.18$
- Bulk unit weight: $\gamma_b = 19.2$ kN/m³
- Specific gravity: $G_s = 2.65$
- Unit weight of water: $\gamma_w = 9.81$ kN/m³ (use **9.81** or **10** as per KTU convention)

**Step (i): Dry unit weight**

$$\gamma_d = \frac{\gamma_b}{1 + w} = \frac{19.2}{1 + 0.18} = \frac{19.2}{1.18}$$

$$\boxed{\gamma_d = 16.27 \text{ kN/m}^3}$$

**Step (ii): Void ratio**

Using the relation $\gamma_d = \dfrac{G_s \cdot \gamma_w}{1 + e}$:

$$e = \frac{G_s \cdot \gamma_w}{\gamma_d} - 1 = \frac{2.65 \times 9.81}{16.27} - 1$$

$$e = \frac{25.9965}{16.27} - 1 = 1.598 - 1$$

$$\boxed{e = 0.598}$$

**Step (iii): Degree of saturation**

Using $S \cdot e = w \cdot G_s$:

$$S = \frac{w \cdot G_s}{e} = \frac{0.18 \times 2.65}{0.598} = \frac{0.477}{0.598}$$

$$\boxed{S = 0.798 \approx 79.8\%}$$

*(Mark split: 2 marks dry unit weight + 2 marks void ratio + 2 marks degree of saturation + 1 mark final answers with units)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

- **Definition:** Water content $w = (M_w / M_s) \times 100$ — always divide by **dry mass**, not total mass.
- **Three Weights:** $M_1$ (empty can), $M_2$ (can + wet soil), $M_3$ (can + dry soil).
- **Standard Formula:** $w = \dfrac{M_2 - M_3}{M_3 - M_1} \times 100$.
- **Oven Temperature:** Strictly **105 °C to 110 °C** — outside this range = wrong result.
- **Drying Time:** **16 to 24 hours**; verify constant mass by re-drying for 1 hour.
- **Desiccator:** Mandatory — prevents re-absorption of atmospheric moisture.
- **IS Code:** Always cite **IS 2720 (Part 2) - 1973** — worth 1 full mark in the exam.
- **Balance Accuracy:** 0.01 g for fine soils, 0.1 g for coarse/gravelly soils.
- **Unsuitable Soils:** Organic soils, gypsum-rich soils, soils with salts or volatiles.
- **Phase Relationship:** $S \cdot e = w \cdot G_s$ — most-missed formula in viva.
- **Dry Unit Weight:** $\gamma_d = \dfrac{\gamma_b}{1 + w}$ — frequent follow-up question.
- **Result Format:** Always report to **2 decimal places** with **% sign**.
- **Common Viva Questions:** "Why 105 °C and not 60 °C or 200 °C?" / "What if soil is heated above 110 °C?" / "Why cool in a desiccator and not in open air?"
- **Errors to Avoid:**
  - Dividing by total wet mass instead of dry mass
  - Forgetting to close the lid during wet soil weighing
  - Not stating IS code
  - Reporting water content as a decimal instead of a percentage
  - Confusing $M_2 - M_3$ with $M_2 - M_1$
- **KTU 2024 Tip:** Lab viva questions frequently link water content to **compaction (OMC)** and **Atterberg limits (LL & PL)** — be ready to explain the relationship qualitatively.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Swelling Test - Free Swell
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCEL507 - GEOTECHNICAL ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Swelling Test - Free Swell

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Free Swell Test** is a simple, rapid identification test used in geotechnical engineering to qualitatively classify the **swelling potential of fine-grained soils**, particularly **expansive clays** (e.g., black cotton soil, montmorillonite-rich soils). The test is standardized by the **Bureau of Indian Standards under IS 2720 (Part XL) – 1977**.

The test determines the **Free Swell Index (FSI)**, which is defined as the **percentage increase in volume of a soil specimen when it is freely allowed to swell in distilled water**, relative to its initial volume in a non-polar liquid (kerosene or carbon tetrachloride), where no swelling occurs.

Mathematically expressed as:

$$
\text{FSI (\%)} = \frac{V_w - V_k}{V_k} \times 100
$$

Where:
- $V_w$ = Final equilibrium volume of soil in **distilled water (mL)**
- $V_k$ = Final equilibrium volume of soil in **kerosene (mL)**

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a dry sponge dropped into water.** A regular kitchen sponge absorbs water and swells, but only a little. Now imagine a **special "super-sponge"** made of certain marine clay — drop it in water and it swells to **2–3 times its original size**, behaving almost like a living thing. This "super-sponge effect" is exactly what happens with expansive clays like **montmorillonite** because water molecules slip between their layered mineral sheets and push them apart.

**Why kerosene is used as the second liquid:**
Kerosene is a **non-polar liquid** that does not interact with the negatively charged clay particles. The clay cannot absorb it, so the volume in kerosene ($V_k$) represents the **"true" or unswollen initial volume** of the soil grains. This gives us a fair baseline to measure how much the soil actually swelled in water.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Standard test liquid 1:** Distilled water (polar, causes swelling)
- **Standard test liquid 2:** **Kerosene** or **Carbon tetrachloride** (non-polar, no swelling)
- **Soil mass used:** **10 g** of oven-dried soil
- **Sieve size for sample preparation:** **425 µm (IS sieve)**
- **Cylinder capacity:** **100 mL** graduated glass cylinder
- **Settling time:** **24 hours** (or until equilibrium)
- **Soil temperature during drying:** **105 °C – 110 °C** for 24 hours in oven
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Underlying Principle — The "Why" Behind the Test

Expansive soils contain clay minerals of the **smectite group** (especially **montmorillonite**). These minerals have a **2:1 layered lattice structure** (one octahedral sheet sandwiched between two tetrahedral sheets) that allows water molecules and exchangeable cations to enter the interlayer spaces. This causes the lattice to **expand dramatically**, producing macroscopic swelling.

The free swell test captures this behaviour by:
1. Allowing the soil **unrestricted access to water** in a measuring cylinder.
