## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*Define the term 'Liquid Limit' of a soil. State the IS code used for its determination by the Casagrande apparatus.*

**Model Answer:**

> **Liquid Limit (WL):** The liquid limit of a soil is the minimum water content (expressed as a percentage of the mass of oven-dry soil) at which the soil is on the boundary between the liquid state and the plastic state, i.e., the lowest water content at which the soil flows as a viscous liquid and does not retain its shape.

> By the Casagrande apparatus method, $W_L$ is defined as the water content at which a standard groove cut in a soil pat placed in the brass cup **closes over a length of 12.7 mm** in exactly **25 blows** when the cup is dropped from a height of **10 mm** at a rate of **2 revolutions per second**.

> **IS Code:** **IS 2720 (Part 5) – 1985** (Reaffirmed 2006) governs the determination of Liquid Limit and Plastic Limit of soils.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*What is meant by the term 'Shrinkage Limit'? Explain its physical significance.*

**Model Answer:**

> **Shrinkage Limit ($W_S$):** The shrinkage limit is the maximum water content at which a reduction in water content will **NOT cause any further reduction in the volume** of the soil mass. It is the boundary between the semi-solid state and the solid state.

> **Physical Significance:** Below the shrinkage limit, the soil particles have come into direct contact with one another, and any further loss of water simply replaces water with air in the voids (air-void formation). The total volume of the soil mass remains constant, though the dry mass continues to decrease. Above $W_S$, water loss causes proportional volume reduction.

> **Engineering Importance:** It governs the **cracking and shrinkage behavior** of clay soils during drying. In Kerala's Kuttanad and coastal alluvial clays, $W_S$ helps predict **desiccation crack patterns** in embankments and canal linings, and is used in designing **foundations on expansive soils**.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module II]**

The following data were obtained from a Casagrande Liquid Limit test on a soil sample:

| Trial No. | Number of Blows (N) | Mass of Container (g) | Mass of Container + Wet Soil (g) | Mass of Container + Dry Soil (g) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 15 | 22.5 | 48.0 | 41.7 |
| 2 | 22 | 21.8 | 46.5 | 40.1 |
| 3 | 30 | 23.2 | 47.0 | 41.3 |
| 4 | 45 | 22.0 | 45.8 | 41.0 |

The Plastic Limit of the soil, determined separately, is **24%**. The natural water content of the soil is **38%**.

**Compute:**
**(a)** The Liquid Limit, Plasticity Index, Flow Index, and Toughness Index of the soil.
**(b)** The Consistency Index and Liquidity Index. Comment on the in-situ state of the soil.

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1: Compute Water Content for Each Trial**

Using the formula $W = \dfrac{M_2 - M_3}{M_3 - M_1} \times 100$:

**Trial 1:** $M_1 = 22.5$ g, $M_2 = 48.0$ g, $M_3 = 41.7$ g
$$ W_1 = \dfrac{48.0 - 41.7}{41.7 - 22.5}\times 100 = \dfrac{6.3}{19.2}\times 100 = 32.81\% $$

**Trial 2:** $M_1 = 21.8$ g, $M_2 = 46.5$ g, $M_3 = 40.1$ g
$$ W_2 = \dfrac{46.5 - 40.1}{40.1 - 21.8}\times 100 = \dfrac{6.4}{18.3}\times 100 = 34.97\% $$

**Trial 3:** $M_1 = 23.2$ g, $M_2 = 47.0$ g, $M_3 = 41.3$ g
$$ W_3 = \dfrac{47.0 - 41.3}{41.3 - 23.2}\times 100 = \dfrac{5.7}{18.1}\times 100 = 31.49\% $$

**Trial 4:** $M_1 = 22.0$ g, $M_2 = 45.8$ g, $M_3 = 41.0$ g
$$ W_4 = \dfrac{45.8 - 41.0}{41.0 - 22.0}\times 100 = \dfrac{4.8}{19.0}\times 100 = 25.26\% $$

**Summary Table:**

| Trial | N | W (%) | $\log_{10}(N/25)$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 15 | 32.81 | $-0.2218$ |
| 2 | 22 | 34.97 | $-0.0554$ |
| 3 | 30 | 31.49 | $+0.0792$ |
| 4 | 45 | 25.26 | $+0.2553$ |

**Step 2: Compute Flow Index (Two-Point Method — Trial 1 and Trial 4)**

$$ I_f = \dfrac{W_1 - W_4}{\log_{10}(N_4/N_1)} = \dfrac{32.81 - 25.26}{\log_{10}(45/15)} = \dfrac{7.55}{\log_{10}(3.0)} = \dfrac{7.55}{0.4771} $$

$$ \boxed{I_f = 15.82\%} $$

**Step 3: Compute Liquid Limit**

Using the flow curve equation with Trial 1:
$$ W_L = W_1 + I_f \cdot \log_{10}(N_1/25) $$
$$ W_L = 32.81 + 15.82 \times (-0.2218) = 32.81 - 3.51 $$
$$ \boxed{W_L = 29.30\%} $$

Verify with Trial 4:
$$ W_L = 25.26 + 15.82 \times (0.2553) = 25.26 + 4.04 = 29.30\% \checkmark $$

**Step 4: Compute Plasticity Index and Toughness Index**

$$ I_P = W_L - W_P = 29.30 - 24.0 = \boxed{5.30\%} $$

$$ I_T = \dfrac{I_P}{I_f} = \dfrac{5.30}{15.82} = \boxed{0.335} $$

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 5: Consistency Index**

$$ I_C = \dfrac{W_L - W}{I_P} = \dfrac{29.30 - 38.0}{5.30} = \dfrac{-8.70}{5.30} $$

$$ \boxed{I_C = -1.64} $$

**Step 6: Liquidity Index**

$$ I_L = \dfrac{W - W_P}{I_P} = \dfrac{38.0 - 24.0}{5.30} = \dfrac{14.0}{5.30} $$

$$ \boxed{I_L = 2.64} $$

**Step 7: Comment on In-situ State**

> **Comment:** Since $I_L > 1$ (i.e., $I_L = 2.64$) and $I_C < 0$, the natural water content of the soil is **above the Liquid Limit**. This means the soil in its natural state behaves as a **viscous liquid** — a **sensitive clay** or **quick clay** condition. The soil is essentially in a state of suspension and would have very low shear strength in-situ. This is typical of **marine deposits**, **Kuttanad clays**, or **recently deposited alluvial clays**, and poses severe construction challenges requiring soil stabilization.

---

#### **Question B (Choice 2 — Internal Choice) — 14 Marks**

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module II]**

A shrinkage limit test on a soil sample gave the following data:
- Mass of empty shrinkage dish, $M_1 = 38.50$ g
- Mass of dish + wet soil (filled completely), $M_2 = 78.40$ g
- Mass of dish + oven-dry soil, $M_3 = 65.20$ g
- Volume of dish (initial volume of wet pat), $V_1 = 25.0$ cm³
- Volume of oven-dry soil pat (by mercury displacement), $V_d = 12.5$ cm³

**Determine:**
**(a)** The Initial Water Content $W_i$, the Shrinkage Limit $W_S$, the Shrinkage Ratio, and the Volumetric Shrinkage.
**(b)** Classify the soil using the Casagrande plasticity chart, given that the Liquid Limit = 65% and the Plastic Limit = 28%. Comment on the suitability of this soil as an embankment material.

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1: Mass of Oven-Dry Soil**
$$ M_s = M_3 - M_1 = 65.20 - 38.50 = 26.70 \text{ g} $$

**Step 2: Mass of Water (Initial)**
$$ M_w = M_2 - M_3 = 78.40 - 65.20 = 13.20 \text{ g} $$

**Step 3: Initial Water Content**
$$ W_i = \dfrac{M_w}{M_s}\times 100 = \dfrac{13.20}{26.70}\times 100 = \boxed{49.44\%} $$

**Step 4: Shrinkage Limit**

Using the derived formula:
$$ W_S = W_i - \left[\dfrac{(V_1 - V_d)\cdot \rho_w}{M_s}\right]\times 100 $$

$$ W_S = 49.44 - \left[\dfrac{(25.0 - 12.5)\times 1.0}{26.70}\right]\times 100 $$

$$ W_S = 49.44 - \left[\dfrac{12.5}{26.70}\right]\times 100 = 49.44 - 46.82 $$

$$ \boxed{W_S = 2.62\%} $$

**Step 5: Shrinkage Ratio**

$$ SR = \dfrac{M_s/V_d}{M_s/V_1} = \dfrac{V_1}{V_d} = \dfrac{25.0}{12.5} = \boxed{2.00} $$

(Also expressed as: $SR = \dfrac{M_s/V_d}{\rho_w} = \dfrac{26.70/12.5}{1.0} = 2.136$ — depending on definition used)

**Step 6: Volumetric Shrinkage**
$$ VS = \dfrac{V_1 - V_d}{V_1}\times 100 = \dfrac{25.0 - 12.5}{25.0}\times 100 = \boxed{50.0\%} $$

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 7: Plasticity Index and Plasticity Chart Classification**
$$ I_P = W_L - W_P = 65 - 28 = 37\% $$

**A-line value** at $W_L = 65$:
$$ I_{P,A-line} = 0.73\times(65 - 20) = 0.73\times 45 = 32.85\% $$

Since the actual $I_P = 37\% > 32.85\%$ (A-line value), the soil **plots above the A-line**.

Since $W_L = 65\% > 50\%$, it is in the **high-plasticity zone**.

> **Classification:** The soil is classified as **CH — Fat Clay (High Plasticity Clay)** as per **IS 1498** and the **Casagrande Plasticity Chart**.

**Step 8: Embankment Suitability Comment**

> **Comment:** As a **CH soil**, this material is **NOT suitable** for use as an embankment fill in its natural state because:
> 1. **High $W_L$ (65%)** indicates very high water retention; the soil will be in a plastic/liquid state at typical in-situ moisture contents.
> 2. **High $I_P$ (37%)** indicates very high compressibility and **potential for swelling/shrinkage** (expansive behavior).
> 3. **High $W_S$ is low (2.62%)** but the very high **Volumetric Shrinkage (50%)** indicates the soil undergoes significant volume change on drying, leading to **deep desiccation cracks** in embankment slopes during Kerala's dry season.
> 4. The very high **Shrinkage Ratio (2.0)** confirms the soil will undergo large volume change per unit water loss.
>
> **Recommendation:** If this CH soil must be used, it should be **stabilized with lime (3–5%)** or **cement (5–8%)** to reduce plasticity, or **replaced** with better-quality lateritic or alluvial fill available locally. Typical KTU project contexts include: **Kerala State Highway embankments, KSEB reservoir bunds, coastal road embankments**.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Atterberg Limits Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Casagrande and Cone Penetrometer Definitions** — In the Casagrande method, $W_L$ is at **25 blows**; in the Indian Cone method, it is at **17 mm penetration** (NOT 25 mm, which is the European/British standard). Mixing these is a guaranteed 1-mark loss.
>
> 2. **Sign Convention in $I_C$ and $I_L$** — If you get a **negative $I_C$** or **$I_L > 1$**, do NOT assume a calculation error. These are valid physical states indicating the soil is in the **liquid zone in-situ** (e.g., sensitive clays, quick clays). KTU examiners specifically test this.
>
> 3. **Units in $I_P$ and $I_f$** — $I_P$ is in **percent (%)** but $I_f$ is **also in percent** (per log cycle). When computing Toughness Index $I_T = I_P/I_f$, the result is **dimensionless** — show this clearly.
>
> 4. **Plotting the Flow Curve** — Always use a **semi-log plot** with $W$ on arithmetic Y-axis and $N$ on **log-scale X-axis**. Reversing the axes is a common mistake that gives a curved line instead of straight.
>
> 5. **Forgetting to Subtract Dish Mass** — In all water content calculations: $W = \dfrac{M_2 - M_3}{M_3 - M_1}\times 100$. Forgetting $M_1$ in the denominator yields water contents 2–3 times too low.
>
> 6. **In the Shrinkage Limit derivation** — The term $(V_1 - V_d)\cdot \rho_w$ represents the mass of water in the void spaces at shrinkage limit. Students often incorrectly identify it as the mass of water lost. **Loses 2 marks easily.**

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:**

- ☐ **Three Atterberg Limits:** $W_L$ (Liquid), $W_P$ (Plastic), $W_S$ (Shrinkage) — each marks a **state transition**.
- ☐ **Liquid Limit Definition:** Water content at which Casagrande groove closes **12.7 mm in 25 blows** (cup drop **10 mm**, rate **2 rev/sec**).
- ☐ **Cone Method:** $W_L$ = water content at **17 mm penetration** with **80 g, 30° apex cone** (Indian Standard).
- ☐ **Plastic Limit Definition:** Lowest water content at which soil can be rolled into **3 mm threads** without crumbling.
- ☐ **Shrinkage Limit:** Maximum water content at which further drying causes **NO volume reduction** — boundary of semi-solid to solid state.
- ☐ **Plasticity Index:** $I_P = W_L - W_P$ — key index for soil classification.
- ☐ **Shrinkage Index:** $I_S = W_P - W_S$.
- ☐ **Consistency Index:** $I_C = (W_L - W)/I_P$ — in-situ state indicator.
- ☐ **Liquidity Index:** $I_L = (W - W_P)/I_P$ — and $I_C + I_L = 1$ **always**.
- ☐ **Flow Index:** $I_f = (W_1 - W_2)/\log_{10}(N_2/N_1)$ — slope of flow curve.
- ☐ **Toughness Index:** $I_T = I_P/I_f$ — measures workability/soil consistency.
- ☐ **A-line equation:** $I_P = 0.73(W_L - 20)$ — **Casagrande Plasticity Chart reference.**
- ☐ **U-line equation:** $I_P = 0.9(W_L - 8)$ — **upper bound**, no soil exists above.
- ☐ **IS Codes:** Part 5 (LL & PL), Part 6 (SL), Part 15 (Cone method).
- ☐ **Volumetric Shrinkage:** $VS = (V_1 - V_d)/V_1 \times 100$ % — used in KSPCB / KSEB embankment design.
- ☐ **Linear Shrinkage:** $LS = \left(1 - \sqrt[3]{1 - VS/100}\right)\times 100$ %.
- ☐ **CH classification:** Above A-line AND $W_L \geq 50\%$ — high-plasticity clay; **not suitable** for embankments without stabilization.
- ☐ **Non-Plastic Soils (NP):** Silts, sands — cannot be rolled at any water content.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Field Density Test - Core Cutter and Sand Replacement Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCEL507 - GEOTECHNICAL ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Field Density Test - Core Cutter and Sand Replacement Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Field Density Test (FDT)** is a fundamental *in-situ* geotechnical investigation used to determine the **bulk (wet) density** and subsequently the **dry density** of compacted or natural soil in the field. These two parameters are the primary acceptance criteria for earthwork compaction in highways, embankments, dams, and airfield pavements.

The two standard methods specified in the KTU 2024 Geotechnical Engineering Laboratory syllabus (aligned with the Bureau of Indian Standards) are:

1. **Core Cutter Method** — Governed by **IS 2720 (Part XXIX) — 1975**. Suitable for fine-grained, cohesive, and soft soils where the cutter can be driven into the ground.
2. **Sand Replacement Method** — Governed by **IS 2720 (Part XXVIII) — 1974 (Reaffirmed 2010)**. Suitable for coarse-grained, granular, and rocky soils (and stiff clays) where a cylindrical cutter cannot penetrate easily.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The in-situ dry density $\rho_d$ obtained is compared with the **Maximum Dry Density (MDD)** obtained from the Proctor's Compaction Test (IS 2720 Part VII/VIII) to compute the **Relative Compaction (RC%)**:
> $$RC\% = \frac{\rho_{d,\text{field}}}{\rho_{d,\text{max}}} \times 100$$
> For most KTU field projects, a minimum **RC = 95%** (heavy compaction) or **RC = 90%** (light compaction) is mandated.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 1 — The "Ice-Cream Scoop" Idea (Core Cutter)**
> Imagine you have a tub of tightly packed *kulfi* (Indian frozen dessert) in a deep tray. To find exactly how dense it is packed, you press a sharp, hollow metal cylinder straight down — just like an ice-cream scoop. You lift it out, and the *kulfi* inside is a perfect, undisturbed cylindrical sample. You simply weigh it and divide by the cylinder's known volume. The "Core Cutter Method" works exactly like this: a steel cylinder is hammered into the soil, the soil inside is extracted, weighed, and its density calculated.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 2 — The "Sand-Filling a Hole" Idea (Sand Replacement)**
> Now imagine the dessert is *gulab jamun* (irregular, hard, crumbly) — you cannot scoop it neatly. So you instead remove a lump of irregular shape from the tray, leaving a hole of unknown volume. To measure that volume, you pour a stream of dry, uniform sand (calibrated) into the hole. Since each grain of sand has a known packing density, the **mass of sand that fills the hole tells you its exact volume**. That is precisely what the Sand Replacement Method does for coarse, stony, or unyielding soils.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Apparatus

| Component | Standard Specification |
|---|---|
| **Core Cutter** | Height = **130 mm**, Internal Diameter = **100 mm**, Wall Thickness = **3 mm** (typical) |
| **Cutter Volume** | ~ **1000 cm³ (1 litre)** |
| **Steel Dolly** | Diameter slightly larger than cutter; placed on top to prevent cutter damage during hammering |
| **Calibrated Sand** | **Ennore Sand** or **Leighton Buzzard Sand**, passing **600 µm** and retained on **300 µm** IS Sieve |
| **Sand Pouring Cylinder (SPC)** | Capacity ~ **3 litres**, calibrated for a specific sand pour |
| **Standard Hammer (for cutter)** | **2.5 kg** rammer with **250 mm** drop |

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — The Core Cutter Method (IS 2720 Part XXIX)

**Principle:** A steel cylinder of known mass and volume is driven into the soil. The soil trapped inside the cutter is extracted, weighed, and its in-situ bulk density computed. Water content is then determined on a representative sub-sample to find the dry density.

**Step-by-Step Operational Logic:**

1. **Site Preparation:** Expose and level a small area of soil (~ 300 mm × 300 mm). Trim loose material.
2. **Weighing Empty Cutter:** Record mass of empty core cutter ($M_1$) and cutter with dolly ($M_1 + M_d$).
3. **Driving the Cutter:** Place cutter on the prepared surface, place the steel dolly on top, and drive the cutter into the soil using a hammer until the dolly seats fully (cutter penetrates to its full height).
4. **Excavation:** Carefully excavate around the cutter, lift it out, and trim the protruding soil flush with both ends using a straightedge.
5. **Weighing Filled Cutter:** Weigh the cutter + wet soil + dolly = $M_2$ (or $M_2'$ without dolly, as per record).
6. **Water Content:** Take a representative sub-sample (~ 200–300 g) from the cut ends for oven-drying at **105 °C – 110 °C** for 24 hours to find water content $w$.
7. **Calculation:** Compute $\rho_{\text{bulk}}$, then $\rho_{\text{dry}}$.

**Why the Core Cutter Method Works:**
- The steel cylinder is thin-walled and sharp-edged, so it shears through the soil with minimum disturbance.
- The **bulking factor** (sample expansion) is minimal because the cutter fits snugly and the sample is unconfined vertically during driving.
- The method gives a *direct* volume measurement, eliminating the need for calibration against a secondary standard (unlike sand replacement).

### 2.2 Operational Logic — The Sand Replacement Method (IS 2720 Part XXVIII)

**Principle:** A small pit is excavated in the ground, the excavated soil is weighed, and the volume of the pit is determined indirectly by filling it with sand of known bulk density, poured from a calibrated container.

**Step-by-Step Operational Logic:**

1. **Calibration of Sand Density ($\rho_{\text{sand}}$):** First, the pouring cylinder is calibrated by filling it over a flat plate. The mass of sand that fills the entire cylinder ($M_3$) is noted. This mass corresponds to the cylinder's known volume. Hence:
   $$\rho_{\text{sand}} = \frac{M_3}{V_{\text{cyl}}}$$
   where $V_{\text{cyl}}$ is the internal volume of the SPC. The sand is calibrated for a *standard free-fall height of 15 cm* between the SPC nozzle and the ground.

2. **Site Preparation:** A small flat area is prepared, and the **base plate** (with a central hole of the same diameter as the SPC nozzle) is placed and seated.

3. **Excavating the Test Pit:** Soil is excavated through the central hole of the base plate to a depth of about **150 mm**. All excavated soil is carefully collected and weighed ($M_{\text{soil, wet}}$).

4. **Sand Filling the Pit:** The SPC is filled with calibrated sand, weighed, and placed over the central hole. Sand is allowed to flow freely until it fills the pit and the cone of the SPC nozzle. The cylinder is re-weighed ($M_4$). The mass of sand filling the pit alone is:
   $$M_{\text{sand, pit}} = (M_{\text{cylinder, full}} - M_4) - M_{\text{sand, cone}}$$
   where $M_{\text{sand, cone}}$ is determined by separate cone-calibration.

5. **Volume of Pit:**
   $$V_{\text{pit}} = \frac{M_{\text{sand, pit}}}{\rho_{\text{sand}}}$$

6. **Density Calculation:**
   $$\rho_{\text{bulk}} = \frac{M_{\text{soil, wet}}}{V_{\text{pit}}}, \quad \rho_{\text{dry}} = \frac{\rho_{\text{bulk}}}{1 + w}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the Sand Replacement Method Works:**
> - The sand is **uniform, dry, free-flowing, and non-cohesive** — it packs to a *constant bulk density* regardless of the shape of the cavity.
> - This converts an irregular 3-D void volume into a measurable 2-D mass of granular material.
> - The method is **non-destructive to the surrounding soil matrix** but *destructive to the test point*.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Symbol Legend | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Bulk Density (Core Cutter) | $\rho_b = \dfrac{M_2 - M_1}{V_c}$ | $M_1$ = mass empty cutter; $M_2$ = mass cutter + wet soil; $V_c$ = cutter volume | $\text{g/cc}$ or $\text{kN/m}^3$ |
| 2 | Bulk Density (Sand Replacement) | $\rho_b = \dfrac{M_s}{V_{\text{pit}}}$ | $M_s$ = mass of wet soil excavated; $V_{\text{pit}}$ = pit volume from sand | $\text{g/cc}$ or $\text{kN/m}^3$ |
| 3 | Volume of Pit | $V_{\text{pit}} = \dfrac{M_{\text{sand, pit}}}{\rho_{\text{sand}}}$ | Mass of sand in pit ÷ density of sand | $\text{cm}^3$ or $\text{m}^3$ |
| 4 | Density of Calibrated Sand | $\rho_{\text{sand}} = \dfrac{M_3 - M_2}{V_{\text{cyl}}}$ | Mass of sand filling SPC ÷ SPC volume | $\text{g/cc}$ |
| 5 | Dry Density | $\rho_d = \dfrac{\rho_b}{1 + w}$ | $w$ = water content (decimal) | $\text{g/cc}$ or $\text{kN/m}^3$ |
| 6 | Water Content | $w = \dfrac{M_w - M_d}{M_d} \times 100$ | $M_w$ = wet mass; $M_d$ = oven-dry mass | % |
| 7 | Relative Compaction | $RC\% = \dfrac{\rho_{d,\text{field}}}{\rho_{d,\text{max}}} \times 100$ | Compared to Proctor MDD | % |
| 8 | Void Ratio (from density) | $e = \dfrac{G_s \cdot \rho_w}{\rho_d} - 1$ | $G_s$ = specific gravity; $\rho_w$ = 1 g/cc | Dimensionless |
| 9 | Degree of Saturation | $S = \dfrac{w \cdot G_s}{e}$ | Using $w$ in decimal | % |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Unit Conversion Tip for KTU Exam:** $1 \text{ g/cc} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3 = 9.81 \text{ kN/m}^3$ (since $1 \text{ kgf} = 9.81 \text{ N}$). Always quote the answer with units matching the question.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Why FDT is Critical |
|---|---|
| **Highway Subgrade Construction** | Ensures each compaction layer meets IRC/MoRTH specifications (typically 97% MDD for subgrade). |
| **Earthen Dam Construction** | Compaction uniformity prevents seepage failure and settlement cracks. |
| **Railway Embankments** | RDSO specifies FDT at every 500 m² for ballast/sub-ballast layers. |
| **Airport Runways** | FAA/ICAO mandates high relative compaction to bear aircraft landing loads. |
| **Building Foundations** | Backfill around footings must meet 95% MDD to avoid differential settlement. |
| **Landfill Liners** | Clay liners compacted to 95% MDD with low permeability to prevent leachate migration. |

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Core Cutter Bulk Density Equation

Let us derive the primary working equation from first principles.

Let:
- $M_c$ = mass of empty core cutter (in grams)
- $M_{cs}$ = mass of core cutter + wet soil (in grams)
- $V_c$ = internal volume of the core cutter (in cm³ or cc)
- $M_w$ = mass of wet sub-sample for water content (g)
- $M_d$ = mass of oven-dry sub-sample (g)

**Step 1:** Mass of wet soil filling the cutter is:
$$M_{\text{soil, wet}} = M_{cs} - M_c$$

**Step 2:** Bulk (wet) density is mass per unit volume:
$$\rho_b = \frac{M_{\text{soil, wet}}}{V_c} = \frac{M_{cs} - M_c}{V_c}$$

**Step 3:** Water content from the sub-sample:
$$w = \frac{M_w - M_d}{M_d}$$

**Step 4:** Dry density (substituting $\rho_b$):
$$\rho_d = \frac{\rho_b}{1 + w} = \frac{1}{1+w} \cdot \frac{M_{cs} - M_c}{V_c}$$

### 3.2 Worked-Out Numerical Example — Core Cutter Method (KTU 2024 Pattern)

**Given Data (typical KTU lab observation sheet):**

| Observation | Value |
|---|---|
| Mass of empty core cutter, $M_1$ | **2100 g** |
| Mass of core cutter + wet soil, $M_2$ | **3675 g** |
| Internal volume of cutter, $V_c$ | **1000 cm³** |
| Mass of empty moisture container | **25 g** |
| Mass of container + wet soil | **125 g** |
| Mass of container + dry soil | **109 g** |

**Solution — Step by Step:**

**Step A:** Mass of wet soil in the cutter:
$$\Delta M = M_2 - M_1 = 3675 - 2100 = 1575 \text{ g}$$

**Step B:** Bulk density:
$$\rho_b = \frac{\Delta M}{V_c} = \frac{1575 \text{ g}}{1000 \text{ cm}^3} = 1.575 \text{ g/cc}$$

**Step C:** Water content:
$$w = \frac{125 - 109}{109 - 25} = \frac{16}{84} = 0.1905 = 19.05\%$$

**Step D:** Dry density:
$$\rho_d = \frac{\rho_b}{1 + w} = \frac{1.575}{1 + 0.1905} = \frac{1.575}{1.1905} = 1.323 \text{ g/cc}$$

**Step E (KTU add-on):** Converting to SI units:
$$\rho_d = 1.323 \times 9.81 = 12.98 \text{ kN/m}^3$$

**Step F (Compaction check):** If Proctor's MDD = 1.65 g/cc, then:
$$RC = \frac{1.323}{1.65} \times 100 = 80.18\%$$
This would **fail** the typical 95% requirement — indicating need for re-compaction.

### 3.3 Worked-Out Numerical Example — Sand Replacement Method (KTU 2024 Pattern)

**Given Data:**

| Observation | Value |
|---|---|
| Mass of SPC + sand (before pouring) | **14.20 kg** |
| Mass of SPC + sand (after pouring) | **8.65 kg** |
| Mass of sand in cone (calibration) | **0.45 kg** |
| Density of calibrated sand, $\rho_{\text{sand}}$ | **1.55 g/cc** |
| Mass of wet soil excavated from pit, $M_s$ | **3.85 kg** |
| Mass of empty container | **0.20 kg** |
| Mass of container + wet soil | **0.95 kg** |
| Mass of container + dry soil | **0.86 kg** |

**Solution — Step by Step:**

**Step A:** Mass of sand that actually filled the test pit:
$$M_{\text{sand, pit}} = (\text{Initial} - \text{Final}) - M_{\text{cone}}$$
$$M_{\text{sand, pit}} = (14.20 - 8.65) - 0.45 = 5.55 - 0.45 = 5.10 \text{ kg}$$

**Step B:** Volume of the pit:
$$V_{\text{pit}} = \frac{M_{\text{sand, pit}}}{\rho_{\text{sand}}} = \frac{5.10 \text{ kg}}{1.55 \text{ g/cc}}$$

To convert kg to g: $5.10 \text{ kg} = 5100 \text{ g}$
$$V_{\text{pit}} = \frac{5100}{1.55} = 3290.32 \text{ cm}^3 = 3.290 \text{ litres}$$

**Step C:** Bulk density of in-situ soil:
$$\rho_b = \frac{M_s}{V_{\text{pit}}} = \frac{3850 \text{ g}}{3290.32 \text{ cm}^3} = 1.170 \text{ g/cc}$$

**Step D:** Water content:
$$w = \frac{0.95 - 0.86}{0.86 - 0.20} = \frac{0.09}{0.66} = 0.1364 = 13.64\%$$

**Step E:** Dry density:
$$\rho_d = \frac{1.170}{1 + 0.1364} = \frac{1.170}{1.1364} = 1.0295 \text{ g/cc}$$

**Step F:** Conversion to kN/m³:
$$\rho_d = 1.0295 \times 9.81 = 10.10 \text{ kN/m}^3$$

**Step G:** Relative compaction (if MDD = 1.85 g/cc):
$$RC = \frac{1.0295}{1.85} \times 100 = 55.65\%$$
This is unacceptably low — the contractor must re-compact the layer.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Methodology

| Parameter | Core Cutter Method | Sand Replacement Method |
|---|---|---|
| **Applicable soil** | Soft to medium cohesive soils | Granular, stony, or hard cohesive soils |
| **IS Code** | IS 2720 Part XXIX | IS 2720 Part XXVIII |
| **Principle** | Direct volume measurement | Indirect volume via sand density |
| **Apparatus cost** | Low | Moderate (SPC + calibrated sand) |
| **Speed** | Faster | Slower (calibration + cone test) |
| **Field disturbance** | Moderate (driving the cutter) | High (excavation) |
| **Accuracy in cohesive soils** | High | Moderate |
| **Accuracy in granular soils** | Not applicable (cutter bends) | High |
| **Sample recovery** | Yes (for further tests) | No (soil is disturbed) |
| **Sources of error** | Slight soil compression during driving; cutter may bend on stones | Sand compaction variation; cone calibration error; vibration disturbance |
| **Volume range** | ~ 1000 cm³ (fixed) | 1500 – 3500 cm³ (flexible) |
| **Depth of test** | Surface layers (~ 130 mm) | Up to ~ 200 mm typical |

### 3.5 Systematic Error & Pitfall Analysis

**Common KTU lab mistakes (must be reported in the observation sheet):**

1. **Sand density drift** — Sand gets damp or fine particles break off, changing $\rho_{\text{sand}}$. Always re-calibrate if sand is reused.
2. **Cone-test omission** — Students often forget to subtract $M_{\text{sand, cone}}$, inflating $V_{\text{pit}}$ and depressing $\rho_b$.
3. **Cutter not driven fully** — Leads to under-estimation of volume and over-estimation of density.
4. **Water content from wrong location** — Always take sub-sample from the *trimmings* of the cutter, not from the excavated soil of an adjacent pit.
5. **Mass of dolly ignored** — If the cutter is weighed *with* the dolly, add the dolly mass; if *without*, ensure the dolly is removed before weighing.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**
*State the IS code governing the Core Cutter Method and the Sand Replacement Method for determining field density. List the suitability conditions for each method.*

**Model Answer:**

| Method | IS Code | Suitable Soil Type |
|---|---|---|
| Core Cutter | **IS 2720 (Part XXIX) – 1975** | Soft to medium stiff cohesive soils (clay, silt) where a steel cylinder can be driven without damage |
| Sand Replacement | **IS 2720 (Part XXVIII) – 1974** | Granular soils (sand, gravel), stony soils, and hard cohesive soils where driving a cutter is impractical |

**Key distinguishing point:** The core cutter measures volume *directly*; the sand replacement measures volume *indirectly* via the bulk density of calibrated sand.

---

**Q2.** **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Supplementary Exam]**
*Define "Relative Compaction" as used in field quality control. Why is it preferred over absolute dry density as a field acceptance criterion?*

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Relative Compaction (RC) is the *percentage ratio* of the in-situ dry density achieved in the field to the maximum dry density (MDD) obtained from a standard Proctor test in the lab.

$$RC\% = \frac{\rho_{d,\text{field}}}{\rho_{d,\text{max}}} \times 100$$

**Why preferred:**
- Soil type and gradation vary across the site — absolute $\rho_d$ is *not universal*.
- MDD normalises the result, allowing **uniform specification** across all soil types.
- Directly tied to engineering performance (shear strength, permeability, compressibility).
- Acts as a single quality benchmark (e.g., 95% RC) irrespective of project location.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** and must be answered in approximately **6–7 pages** of A4. Sub-parts (a) and (b) typically carry **7 marks each**.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Core Cutter Method

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Describe with a neat sketch the procedure for determining the in-situ density of a compacted cohesive soil layer using the **Core Cutter Method** as per IS 2720 (Part XXIX). List the apparatus required. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** In a field test using a core cutter of **100 mm diameter** and **130 mm height**, the following data was recorded:
- Mass of empty cutter = **2120 g**
- Mass of cutter + wet soil = **3760 g**
- Mass of empty moisture box = **30 g**
- Mass of box + wet soil = **120 g**
- Mass of box + oven-dry soil = **105 g**

Compute the **bulk density**, **dry density**, and **degree of saturation** (take $G_s = 2.70$). **[7 Marks]**

---

**Model Solution to Question A:**

**(a) Procedure & Apparatus [7 Marks]:**

**Apparatus Required:**
1. Core cutter (steel cylinder, 100 mm ID × 130 mm height, 3 mm wall thickness)
2. Steel dolly (slightly larger diameter)
3. Driving hammer (2.5 kg, 250 mm drop)
4. Straightedge (for trimming)
5. Weighing balance (0.1 g sensitivity)
6. Moisture content containers
7. Oven (105 °C – 110 °C)
8. Spade, knife, brush

**Procedure:**
1. **Site Prep:** Clean a small area of about 300 mm square. Trim loose, dried, or disturbed soil to expose a flat, level surface.
2. **Initial Weighing:** Weigh the empty core cutter (with both end caps or open ends noted) — record $M_1$.
3. **Positioning:** Place the cutter vertically on the prepared surface, with the steel dolly placed on top to protect the cutter edge.
4. **Driving:** Using the 2.5 kg hammer with 250 mm drop, drive the cutter *gently but firmly* into the soil until the **dolly rests fully on the soil surface** (cutter penetrates to its full 130 mm depth).
5. **Excavation:** Carefully dig around the cutter with a spade, taking care not to disturb the soil *inside* the cutter. Lift the cutter out, holding the soil firmly at the bottom with a palm or a cap.
6. **Trimming:** With a straightedge, trim the protruding soil flush with both the top and bottom rims of the cutter.
7. **Weighing:** Weigh the cutter + wet soil = $M_2$.
8. **Water Content:** Take a representative soil sample (200–300 g) from the trimmings into a pre-weighed moisture box, weigh, and oven-dry at **105 °C – 110 °C for 24 hours** to determine $w$.

**Neat Sketch Description (to be drawn in exam):**
- Top view: Circular cutter, central axis, soil plug inside
- Side view: Cylinder with dolly on top, hammer striking the dolly, soil plug visible inside the cylinder

---

**(b) Numerical Solution [7 Marks]:**

**Given:** $D = 100$ mm = 10 cm; $H = 130$ mm = 13 cm; $M_1 = 2120$ g; $M_2 = 3760$ g; $M_{\text{box}} = 30$ g; $M_{\text{w+box}} = 120$ g; $M_{\text{d+box}} = 105$ g; $G_s = 2.70$.

**Step 1: Volume of the cutter**
$$V_c = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot D^2 \cdot H = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot 10^2 \cdot 13 = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot 1300 = 1021.02 \text{ cm}^3$$

**Step 2: Mass of wet soil**
$$M_{\text{wet}} = M_2 - M_1 = 3760 - 2120 = 1640 \text{ g}$$

**Step 3: Bulk density**
$$\rho_b = \frac{M_{\text{wet}}}{V_c} = \frac{1640}{1021.02} = 1.606 \text{ g/cc}$$

**Step 4: Water content**
$$w = \frac{(M_{\text{w+box}} - M_{\text{d+box}})}{(M_{\text{d+box}} - M_{\text{box}})} = \frac{120 - 105}{105 - 30} = \frac{15}{75} = 0.20 = 20\%$$

**Step 5: Dry density**
$$\rho_d = \frac{\rho_b}{1 + w} = \frac{1.606}{1.20} = 1.338 \text{ g/cc}$$

**Step 6: Void ratio**
$$\rho_d = \frac{G_s \cdot \rho_w}{1 + e} \implies 1.338 = \frac{2.70 \times 1}{1 + e}$$
$$1 + e = \frac{2.70}{1.338} = 2.0180 \implies e = 1.0180$$

**Step 7: Degree of saturation**
$$S = \frac{w \cdot G_s}{e} = \frac{0.20 \times 2.70}{1.0180} = \frac{0.540}{1.0180} = 0.5304 = 53.04\%$$

**Final Answer Box:**
$$\boxed{\rho_b = 1.606 \text{ g/cc}, \quad \rho_d = 1.338 \text{ g/cc}, \quad S = 53.04\%}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Sand Replacement Method (Internal Choice)

**[KTU University Exam — June 2023]**

**(a)** Explain with a flowchart the **Sand Replacement Method** for determining in-situ density of a compacted granular subgrade as per IS 2720 (Part XXVIII). Discuss the role of **calibrated sand** and the necessity of the **cone test correction**. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** The following observations were recorded during a sand replacement test:
- Mass of SPC + sand (initial) = **15.40 kg**
- Mass of SPC + sand (after pouring into pit) = **9.20 kg**
- Mass of sand in cone (from cone calibration) = **0.50 kg**
- Density of calibrated sand, $\rho_{\text{sand}} = 1.52 \text{ g/cc}$
- Mass of wet soil excavated = **4.10 kg**
- Water content of soil = **12%**

Compute the **field dry density** and the **relative compaction** if the Proctor MDD is **1.78 g/cc**. **[7 Marks]**

---

**Model Solution to Question B:**

**(a) Theory & Cone Test [7 Marks]:**

**Principle:** The volume of an irregular pit excavated in the ground is determined by measuring the mass of dry, free-flowing, uniform sand that fills the pit, knowing the *bulk density* of that sand under standard pouring conditions.

**Why Calibrated Sand:**
- Must be **uniform, clean, dry, non-cohesive**, and free-flowing.
- Sieve passing **600 µm** and retained on **300 µm** (IS Sieve) — typically Ennore sand.
- Uniformity ensures **constant bulk density** upon pouring from a fixed height (15 cm).
- Coarse enough to not flow out of pit; fine enough to fill narrow crevices.

**Cone Test Correction (mandatory step):**
- When the SPC is placed over the pit hole, the sand fills *both* the pit *and* the *annular cone recess* at the bottom of the SPC nozzle.
- To get the mass of sand *only in the pit*, the mass of sand that would have filled just the cone (without the pit) must be **subtracted**.
- **Procedure for cone test:** Place the SPC on a flat glass plate (representing an empty pit), open the valve, and weigh the sand that fills the cone alone.
- Omitting this correction leads to **over-estimation of pit volume** → **under-estimation of density** → false sense of *poor compaction*.

**Flowchart Description (to be drawn in exam):**
1. Calibrate SPC (with sand of known density)
2. Determine cone correction
3. Excavate pit, collect & weigh soil
4. Fill pit via SPC
5. Reweigh SPC
6. Subtract cone mass → sand in pit
7. Divide by sand density → pit volume
8. Compute $\rho_b$, then $\rho_d$

---

**(b) Numerical Solution [7 Marks]:**

**Given:** $M_{\text{init}} = 15.40$ kg; $M_{\text{final}} = 9.20$ kg; $M_{\text{cone}} = 0.50$ kg; $\rho_{\text{sand}} = 1.52$ g/cc; $M_s = 4.10$ kg; $w = 12\% = 0.12$; $\text{MDD} = 1.78$ g/cc.

**Step 1: Mass of sand poured into the pit (net of cone)**
$$M_{\text{sand, pit}} = (M_{\text{init}} - M_{\text{final}}) - M_{\text{cone}}$$
$$M_{\text{sand, pit}} = (15.40 - 9.20) - 0.50 = 6.20 - 0.50 = 5.70 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 2: Volume of the pit**
Convert: $5.70 \text{ kg} = 5700 \text{ g}$
$$V_{\text{pit}} = \frac{M_{\text{sand, pit}}}{\rho_{\text{sand}}} = \frac{5700}{1.52} = 3750 \text{ cm}^3$$

**Step 3: Bulk density of in-situ soil**
Convert: $4.10 \text{ kg} = 4100 \text{ g}$
$$\rho_b = \frac{M_s}{V_{\text{pit}}} = \frac{4100}{3750} = 1.0933 \text{ g/cc}$$

**Step 4: Dry density**
$$\rho_d = \frac{\rho_b}{1 + w} = \frac{1.0933}{1.12} = 0.9762 \text{ g/cc}$$

**Step 5: Relative compaction**
$$RC = \frac{\rho_d}{\text{MDD}} \times 100 = \frac{0.9762}{1.78} \times 100 = 54.84\%$$

**Final Answer Box:**
$$\boxed{\rho_d = 0.9762 \text{ g/cc}, \quad RC = 54.84\%}$$

**Verdict:** This compaction is **grossly insufficient** (need 95% RC). Site must be reworked and re-tested.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps (Avoid These for Full Marks!):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract the cone mass** in sand replacement → entire 7-mark numerical becomes wrong. Always include the line "$(M_{\text{init}} - M_{\text{final}}) - M_{\text{cone}}$".
> 2. **Mixing up water content formula** → Use *dry mass* in the denominator, not the *wet mass*. $w = (M_w - M_d)/M_d$, NOT $(M_w - M_d)/M_w$.
> 3. **Quoting $\rho_d$ in g/cc when the question asks in kN/m³** → Multiply by **9.81** or by **$\gamma_w$**.
> 4. **Ignoring units of $V_c$** → For $D$ in cm and $H$ in cm, $V$ comes in cm³. Then 1 g/cm³ = 1 g/cc. No need for further conversion unless SI units are required.
> 5. **Not mentioning the IS Code reference** in long answers → Examiners award 0.5–1 mark specifically for citing IS 2720 Part XXIX / XXVIII.
> 6. **Cone test correction confusion** → Many students calibrate the *full* SPC against a pit of *known* volume (e.g., a metal container) to *avoid* doing the cone test — this is also acceptable per IS 2720, but the cone test is the *standard prescribed method*.
> 7. **Forgetting to label the diagram** → In 7-mark procedure questions, a *labelled* sketch (with dimensions) carries 1–2 marks.
> 8. **Wrong formula for relative compaction** → It is $\rho_{d,\text{field}} / \rho_{d,\text{max}}$, not the wet density.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Field Density Test**
>
> - [x] **FDT** determines *in-situ* dry density of soil in the field.
> - [x] **Core Cutter** is for **soft, cohesive** soils (IS 2720 Part XXIX).
> - [x] **Sand Replacement** is for **granular, stony, hard** soils (IS 2720 Part XXVIII).
> - [x] Standard cutter: **100 mm dia × 130 mm height**, ~ 1000 cm³.
> - [x] Hammer: **2.5 kg, 250 mm drop**.
> - [x] Calibrated sand: passing **600 µm**, retained on **300 µm**.
> - [x] Standard free-fall height of sand: **15 cm** above the surface.
> - [x] Cone test correction is **mandatory** in sand replacement.
> - [x] Bulk density: $\rho_b = (M_2 - M_1) / V_c$ (core cutter) or $M_s / V_{\text{pit}}$ (sand replacement).
> - [x] Dry density: $\rho_d = \rho_b / (1 + w)$.
> - [x] Water content: $w = (M_w - M_d) / M_d \times 100\%$.
> - [x] Relative Compaction (RC) = $(\rho_{d,\text{field}} / \rho_{d,\text{max}}) \times 100$.
> - [x] Typical field acceptance: **RC = 95%** (heavy), **RC = 90%** (light).
> - [x] 1 g/cc = 9.81 kN/m³ (unit conversion for SI answers).
> - [x] Void ratio: $e = (G_s \cdot \rho_w / \rho_d) - 1$.
> - [x] Degree of saturation: $S = (w \cdot G_s) / e$.
> - [x] Sub-sample for $w$ must be taken from the **trimmings of the same cutter/pit**.
> - [x] Oven-dry temperature: **105 °C – 110 °C** for 24 hours.
> - [x] Cutter must be **driven fully** to ensure correct $V_c$.
> - [x] Pit depth for sand replacement: **~ 150 mm**.
> - [x] Sand must be **re-calibrated** if reused (to counter moisture/gradation change).
> - [x] The two methods are **not interchangeable** — selection depends on soil type.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Light Compaction Test (Standard Proctor Test)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCEL507 - GEOTECHNICAL ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Light Compaction Test (Standard Proctor Test)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (IS 2720: Part 7 – 1980, Reaffirmed 2010):**
> The **Light Compaction Test** (also called the **Standard Proctor Test**) is a laboratory compaction test used to determine the relationship between **dry density** and **moisture content** of a soil for a specific (standard) compactive effort, thereby establishing the **Maximum Dry Density (MDD)** and the corresponding **Optimum Moisture Content (OMC)**.

The test was originally developed by **R. R. Proctor (1933)** and later standardized by the Indian Standards. The word "**Light**" distinguishes it from the "**Heavy**" (Modified Proctor) test. The "light" compactive effort corresponds to a moderate amount of tamping energy.

### 1.1 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world analogy — "Packing a lunchbox with rice" 🍚**
> Imagine you are packing a lunchbox (the mould) with rice (soil). If the rice is completely dry, the grains do not stick to each other, and you are left with a lot of empty air pockets — the rice pack is "loose." As you slowly sprinkle a little water onto the rice, the grains begin to bind together with a thin film of water acting like glue, and you can pack the rice more tightly. However, if you pour *too much* water, the rice starts to float, and the box cannot be packed tightly anymore. The *sweet spot* — where the rice is packed the *tightest* — corresponds to the **OMC**, and the weight of rice in the box is the **MDD**. The compaction test simply replicates this "packing experiment" with controlled energy.

### 1.2 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- Mass of rammer: **$2.6 \ \text{kg}$** (light rammer)
- Drop height of rammer: **$310 \ \text{mm}$**
- Internal diameter of mould: **$100 \ \text{mm}$**
- Internal height of mould: **$127.3 \ \text{mm}$**
- Volume of mould: **$1000 \ \text{cm}^3$** (1 litre)
- Number of layers: **3**
- Number of blows per layer: **25**
- Compactive energy: **$595.6 \ \text{kJ/m}^3$** (≈ $606 \ \text{kJ/m}^3$)
- Sieve used: soil passing **$4.75 \ \text{mm}$** IS sieve (only fines tested)
- Standard reference: **IS 2720 (Part 7) – 1980 (Reaffirmed 2010)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> Compaction tests form the foundation of earthwork quality control in highway subgrades, earthen dams, embankments, and railway formations. Students are expected to know both the *numerical* answer (MDD/OMC) and the *engineering significance* (which compactive effort suits which field project).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The 'Why' Behind the Test

Compaction is the process of **densifying soil by mechanical means** (rolling, tamping, vibrating) to:
- Increase **shear strength**
- Reduce **compressibility** (settlement)
- Decrease **permeability** (seepage loss)
- Improve **bearing capacity**

Different field compactors deliver different amounts of energy. The Lab test standardizes this energy so engineers can:
1. Predict **field compaction behavior**.
2. Specify **field moisture content** for contractors.
3. **Control quality** during construction.

### 2.2 The 'How' — Operational Logic Steps

1. **Sample preparation:** About **$3$ to $4 \ \text{kg}$** of air-dried soil passing the $4.75 \ \text{mm}$ IS sieve is taken. Initial water content is measured.
2. **Water addition:** A known quantity of water is added and mixed thoroughly. The soil is sealed in polythene and kept for **maturation (≈ 24 hours)** so moisture becomes uniform.
3. **Compaction:** The soil is placed in the mould in **3 equal layers**. Each layer is compacted with **25 blows** of the $2.6 \ \text{kg}$ rammer dropped from $310 \ \text{mm}$. The blows must be distributed evenly over the entire surface.
4. **Trimming & weighing:** The collar is removed. The top surface is trimmed flush with the mould using a straightedge. The mould + compacted wet soil is weighed ($M_2$). The mass of the empty mould ($M_1$) is known.
5. **Moisture sampling:** A representative sample (~ $30\text{–}50 \ \text{g}$) is taken from the middle of the compacted soil to determine the moisture content ($w$).
6. **Repetition:** Steps 2–5 are repeated for **at least 4 to 5 trials**, with the water content **progressively increased by about 2 %** each time. The mould and base plate need *not* be dried between trials.
7. **Plotting:** A graph of **Dry density ($\rho_d$) on Y-axis vs Moisture content ($w$) on X-axis** is drawn. The peak of the smooth curve gives **MDD** and the corresponding abscissa gives **OMC**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|----------|---------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Bulk density | $\rho \ =\ \dfrac{M_2 - M_1}{V}$ | $\text{g/cc}$ or $\text{Mg/m}^3$ | $V = 1000 \ \text{cc}$ for standard mould |
| 2 | Dry density | $\rho_d \ =\ \dfrac{\rho}{1 + w}$ | $\text{g/cc}$ | $w$ is decimal moisture content |
| 3 | Zero Air Voids (Saturation) line | $\rho_{d,\text{sat}} \ =\ \dfrac{G \cdot \rho_w}{1 + wG}$ | $\text{g/cc}$ | $G$ = specific gravity; $\rho_w = 1 \ \text{g/cc}$ |
| 4 | Compactive energy (SI) | $E \ =\ \dfrac{n_b \cdot N \cdot W \cdot h}{V}$ | $\text{kJ/m}^3$ | $n_b$ = blows/layer, $N$ = layers, $W$ = rammer, $h$ = drop |
| 5 | Air voids content | $a_v \ =\ \dfrac{\rho_w \cdot G - \rho_d}{\rho_w \cdot G} \cdot 100$ | % | Useful for interpreting saturation |
| 6 | Degree of saturation | $S \ =\ \dfrac{w \cdot G}{a_v \ \text{(in decimals)}}$ | fraction | $0 \le S \le 1$ |
| 7 | Oversize correction (USBR) | $\rho_{d,\text{corr}} \ =\ \rho_{d,\text{lab}} \cdot \dfrac{100}{100 + P_o}$ | $\text{g/cc}$ | $P_o$ = % retained on $4.75 \ \text{mm}$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Exam Pitfall:** In every table cell above, the symbol "$/$" denotes **division**, **not** the markdown table pipe symbol. Do not paste the formula with vertical bars; KTU scripts use horizontal fraction bars as shown.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility of the Test

- **Highway engineering:** Subgrade compaction specifications are tied to *relative compaction* = $\dfrac{\rho_{d,\text{field}}}{\rho_{d,\text{max,lab}}} \times 100 \ (\%)$.
- **Earthen dams:** Optimum moisture ensures minimum permeability of the clay core.
- **Railway formations:** Modern high-speed rail embankments require $\ge 97\ \%$ relative compaction per RDSO specifications.
- **Landfill liners:** Compacted clay liners are designed at $w \ge \text{OMC} - 2\ \%$ to achieve low hydraulic conductivity.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Zero Air Voids (ZAV) / Theoretical Maximum Dry Density

The ZAV line represents the locus of dry densities when the soil is **$100\ \%$ saturated** ($S = 1$) with **zero air voids** ($a_v = 0$).

**Phase relationship definitions:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Volume of solids} \quad V_s \ &=\ \dfrac{M_s}{G \cdot \rho_w} \\[4pt]
\text{Volume of water} \quad V_w \ &=\ \dfrac{M_w}{\rho_w} \ =\ \dfrac{w \cdot M_s}{\rho_w} \\[4pt]
\text{Total volume} \quad V \ &=\ V_s + V_w \quad \text{(since } a_v = 0 \text{)} \\[4pt]
\text{Dry density} \quad \rho_d \ &=\ \dfrac{M_s}{V} \ =\ \dfrac{M_s}{V_s + V_w}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Substituting the volumes:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho_d \ &=\ \dfrac{M_s}{\dfrac{M_s}{G \cdot \rho_w} + \dfrac{w \cdot M_s}{\rho_w}} \\[6pt]
\rho_d \ &=\ \dfrac{M_s}{\dfrac{M_s}{\rho_w}\left(\dfrac{1}{G} + w\right)} \\[6pt]
\rho_d \ &=\ \dfrac{\rho_w}{\dfrac{1}{G} + w} \ =\ \dfrac{G \cdot \rho_w}{1 + w \cdot G}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **Zero Air Voids (ZAV) equation**, valid only when air voids $a_v = 0$.

$$
\boxed{\ \rho_{d,\text{ZAV}} \ =\ \dfrac{G \cdot \rho_w}{1 + w \cdot G}\ }
$$

### 3.2 Derivation of Compactive Effort $E$ for Light Compaction

$$
\begin{aligned}
E \ &=\ \dfrac{n_b \cdot N \cdot W \cdot h}{V} \\[4pt]
   \ &=\ \dfrac{25 \cdot 3 \cdot 2.6 \cdot 0.310 \ (\text{m})}{1000 \times 10^{-6} \ \text{m}^3} \\[4pt]
   \ &=\ \dfrac{25 \cdot 3 \cdot 0.806}{0.001} \ =\ \dfrac{60.45}{0.001} \\[4pt]
   \ &=\ 60\,450 \ \text{J/m}^3 \ \approx \ 604.5 \ \text{kJ/m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

(For air-dry bulk, the value reported by IS is $595.6 \ \text{kJ/m}^3$ because of small rounding differences; either is accepted in KTU scripts.)

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Typical 14-Mark Problem)

**Data:**

| Trial | Mass of wet soil in mould (g) | Moisture content (%) |
|:-----:|:-----------------------------:|:--------------------:|
| 1 | 1992 | 8.2 |
| 2 | 2060 | 10.5 |
| 3 | 2108 | 12.6 |
| 4 | 2125 | 14.7 |
| 5 | 2108 | 16.8 |

Mass of empty mould $M_1 = 1192 \ \text{g}$, Volume $V = 1000 \ \text{cc}$, $G = 2.70$, $\rho_w = 1 \ \text{g/cc}$.

**Step 1 — Bulk density $\rho$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho_1 \ &=\ \dfrac{1992 - 1192}{1000} \ =\ 0.800 \ \text{g/cc} \\
\rho_2 \ &=\ \dfrac{2060 - 1192}{1000} \ =\ 0.868 \ \text{g/cc} \\
\rho_3 \ &=\ \dfrac{2108 - 1192}{1000} \ =\ 0.916 \ \text{g/cc} \\
\rho_4 \ &=\ \dfrac{2125 - 1192}{1000} \ =\ 0.933 \ \text{g/cc} \\
\rho_5 \ &=\ \dfrac{2108 - 1192}{1000} \ =\ 0.916 \ \text{g/cc}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Dry density $\rho_d = \dfrac{\rho}{1 + w}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho_{d,1} \ &=\ \dfrac{0.800}{1 + 0.082} \ =\ 0.739 \ \text{g/cc} \\
\rho_{d,2} \ &=\ \dfrac{0.868}{1 + 0.105} \ =\ 0.785 \ \text{g/cc} \\
\rho_{d,3} \ &=\ \dfrac{0.916}{1 + 0.126} \ =\ 0.814 \ \text{g/cc} \\
\rho_{d,4} \ &=\ \dfrac{0.933}{1 + 0.147} \ =\ 0.813 \ \text{g/cc} \\
\rho_{d,5} \ &=\ \dfrac{0.916}{1 + 0.168} \ =\ 0.784 \ \text{g/cc}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — ZAV line calculation (for plotting) at various $w$:**

Using $\rho_{d,\text{ZAV}} = \dfrac{2.70 \times 1}{1 + 2.70 \cdot w}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{At } w = 8\%: \quad & \rho_d = \dfrac{2.70}{1 + 0.216} \ =\ 2.221 \ \text{g/cc} \\
\text{At } w = 10\%: \quad & \rho_d = \dfrac{2.70}{1 + 0.270} \ =\ 2.126 \ \text{g/cc} \\
\text{At } w = 12\%: \quad & \rho_d = \dfrac{2.70}{1 + 0.324} \ =\ 2.040 \ \text{g/cc} \\
\text{At } w = 14\%: \quad & \rho_d = \dfrac{2.70}{1 + 0.378} \ =\ 1.960 \ \text{g/cc} \\
\text{At } w = 16\%: \quad & \rho_d = \dfrac{2.70}{1 + 0.432} \ =\ 1.886 \ \text{g/cc}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Result from the compaction curve:**

$$
\boxed{\ \text{MDD} \ \approx \ 0.815 \ \text{g/cc} \quad ; \quad \text{OMC} \ \approx \ 13\ \%\ }
$$

(At peak, $w \approx 12.6$ % gives $\rho_d = 0.814$, while the smooth-curve peak lies at ~ $13.0$ % with $\rho_d = 0.815 \ \text{g/cc}$.)

**Step 5 — Verification (saturation check at OMC):**

$$
\rho_{d,\text{ZAV at } 13\%} \ =\ \dfrac{2.70}{1 + 2.70 \times 0.13} \ =\ \dfrac{2.70}{1.351} \ =\ 1.998 \ \text{g/cc}
$$

The MDD ($0.815 \ \text{g/cc}$) is far below the ZAV ($1.998 \ \text{g/cc}$), confirming that $100\ \%$ saturation was *not* reached (which is impossible for fine-grained soils even at OMC).

### 3.4 Effect of Compactive Effort — Analytical Insight

For the *same* soil, when energy $E$ is increased (e.g., Heavy Compaction), the curve shifts **upward and to the left**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{d \rho_d}{d E} &> \ 0 \quad \text{(higher energy} \rightarrow \text{higher MDD)} \\
\dfrac{d w_{\text{opt}}}{d E} &< \ 0 \quad \text{(higher energy} \rightarrow \text{lower OMC)}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is because a greater compactive effort can expel more air and more water from the soil matrix, achieving a denser state at a smaller moisture content.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mermaid Compilation Note:** All node IDs below are alphanumeric (e.g., `n1`, `stepA`, `compY`). All labels contain only raw uppercase text and symbols — no markdown bold/italic tags, no HTML tables, and no reserved keywords.

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Apparatus of Light Compaction Test

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[RAMMER 2.6 KG DROP 310 MM] --> B[STANDARD MOULD 1000 CC]
    B --> C[BASE PLATE]
    B --> D[COLLAR 60 MM HEIGHT]
    D --> E[COMPACTED SOIL 3 LAYERS X 25 BLOWS]
    E --> F[STRAIGHT EDGE FOR TRIMMING]
    F --> G[OVEN 105 TO 110 C FOR MOISTURE CONTENT]
    H[SOIL PASSING 4.75 MM SIEVE] --> B
    I[GRADUATED CYLINDER FOR WATER] --> B
```

### 4.2 Functional Process Flow — Conducting the Test

```mermaid
graph TD
    stepA[STEP 1 AIR DRY SOIL PASSING 4.75 MM] --> stepB[STEP 2 ADD WATER MIX UNIFORMLY]
    stepB --> stepC[STEP 3 FILL MOULD IN 3 LAYERS]
    stepC --> stepD[STEP 4 RAM 25 BLOWS PER LAYER]
    stepD --> stepE[STEP 5 REMOVE COLLAR TRIM TOP]
    stepE --> stepF[STEP 6 WEIGH MOULD PLUS WET SOIL]
    stepF --> stepG[STEP 7 TAKE SAMPLE FOR MOISTURE CONTENT]
    stepG --> stepH[STEP 8 INCREASE WATER BY 2 PERCENT]
    stepH --> stepI{FOUR TO FIVE TRIALS DONE}
    stepI -->|NO| stepB
    stepI -->|YES| stepJ[STEP 9 PLOT DRY DENSITY VERSUS WATER CONTENT]
    stepJ --> stepK[STEP 10 READ MDD AND OMC AT PEAK]
    stepK --> stepL[STEP 11 PLOT ZAV LINE FOR VERIFICATION]
```

### 4.3 Compaction Curve Schematic (Cartesian Plot Description)

A typical Standard Proctor curve has:

- **X-axis:** Water content $w$ in % (range typically $4$ % to $20$ %).
- **Y-axis (Left):** Dry density $\rho_d$ in $\text{g/cc}$ (range typically $1.5$ to $2.1$).
- **Y-axis (Right):** Degree of saturation $S$ in % (rising curve, sigmoid).
- **Main curve:** Bell-shaped with a single peak — the **compaction curve**.
- **ZAV line:** A hyperbolic curve drawn to the *right* of the bell; the compaction curve never touches the ZAV line.
- **Peak coordinates:** $\text{OMC}$ (x) and $\text{MDD}$ (y).
- **Dry side (left of peak):** Soil is brittle, has high air voids, low cohesion.
- **Wet side (right of peak):** Soil is plastic, low air voids, higher saturation.

```
  ρd (g/cc)                      Compaction Curve
    ▲                                 ___
2.0 |                               _/   \_    ZAV line
    |                              /        \_
    |                            _/           \_
1.8 |                          _/               \_
    |                        _/                   \_
1.6 |                      _/                       \_
    |                    _/  ← PEAK at (OMC, MDD)    \_
1.4 |                  _/                               \_
    |                _/                                   
1.2 |              _/
    |           __/
    |        __/
1.0 |______/______________________________►  w (%)
        0   4   8   12  16  20  24
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Tip:** When asked to draw a "neat sketch", always include the ZAV line, label the OMC and MDD clearly, and shade the dry side and wet side with arrows.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define **(a) Optimum Moisture Content (OMC)** and **(b) Maximum Dry Density (MDD)**. State the Indian Standard code for the Light Compaction Test.

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **(a) OMC (1 mark):** Optimum Moisture Content is the moisture content at which a soil attains its **maximum dry density** for a given compactive effort.
> **(b) MDD (1 mark):** Maximum Dry Density is the **highest dry density** achievable for a soil under a specific compactive effort, corresponding to its OMC.
> **(c) IS Code (1 mark):** **IS 2720 (Part 7) – 1980** (Reaffirmed 2010).

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** What is the **Zero Air Voids (ZAV) line**? State its significance in compaction.

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **Definition (1.5 marks):** The ZAV line is the theoretical curve representing dry density when the soil is **$100\ \%$ saturated** with **zero air content**; it is plotted using $\rho_{d,\text{ZAV}} = \dfrac{G \cdot \rho_w}{1 + wG}$.
> **Significance (1.5 marks):**
> 1. It represents the **upper limit** of dry density for a given moisture content — actual compaction curves always lie *below* it.
> 2. The **gap** between the compaction curve and the ZAV line indicates the **air voids remaining** in the compacted soil.
> 3. It helps verify if the laboratory compaction is **realistic** (curves approaching the ZAV line indicate near-saturation, which is typical of fine-grained soils at high compaction energies).

---

### Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023]** 
**(a)** Describe with a neat sketch the procedure for conducting the **Light Compaction Test (Standard Proctor Test)** as per IS 2720 (Part 7). List the apparatus required. (7 marks)
**(b)** The following observations were recorded during a Standard Proctor Test. Plot the compaction curve, determine **MDD and OMC**, and verify the result by drawing the **ZAV line**. Take $G = 2.70$, $\rho_w = 1 \ \text{g/cc}$. (7 marks)

| Trial | Mass of compacted soil in mould (g) | Moisture content (%) |
|:-----:|:-----------------------------------:|:--------------------:|
| 1 | 1700 | 5.0 |
| 2 | 1810 | 7.5 |
| 3 | 1890 | 10.0 |
| 4 | 1920 | 12.5 |
| 5 | 1900 | 15.0 |

Volume of mould = $1000 \ \text{cc}$.

> **Model Solution:**
> **(a) Procedure (7 marks):** *[Full procedural write-up as detailed in Section 2.2 of these notes — cover sample preparation (1), water addition and maturation (1), 3-layer compaction with 25 blows (2), trimming and weighing (1), moisture determination (1), repetition and plotting (1).]*
> **(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**
> **Step 1 — Bulk density** $\rho = \dfrac{M}{V}$:
> $\rho_1 = 1.700, \ \rho_2 = 1.810, \ \rho_3 = 1.890, \ \rho_4 = 1.920, \ \rho_5 = 1.900 \ \text{g/cc}$.
> **Step 2 — Dry density** $\rho_d = \dfrac{\rho}{1+w}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho_{d,1} \ &=\ \dfrac{1.700}{1.05} \ =\ 1.619 \ \text{g/cc} \\
\rho_{d,2} \ &=\ \dfrac{1.810}{1.075} \ =\ 1.684 \ \text{g/cc} \\
\rho_{d,3} \ &=\ \dfrac{1.890}{1.10} \ =\ 1.718 \ \text{g/cc} \\
\rho_{d,4} \ &=\ \dfrac{1.920}{1.125} \ =\ 1.707 \ \text{g/cc} \\
\rho_{d,5} \ &=\ \dfrac{1.900}{1.15} \ =\ 1.652 \ \text{g/cc}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Step 3 — From the smooth compaction curve:**
> $$\boxed{\ \text{MDD} \ \approx \ 1.72 \ \text{g/cc} \quad ; \quad \text{OMC} \ \approx \ 10.5\ \%\ }$$
> **Step 4 — ZAV line verification at $w = 10.5\ \%$:**

$$
\rho_{d,\text{ZAV}} \ =\ \dfrac{2.70 \times 1}{1 + 2.70 \times 0.105} \ =\ \dfrac{2.70}{1.2835} \ =\ 2.104 \ \text{g/cc}
$$

> Since $1.72 \ll 2.10$, the result is realistic and saturation is not achieved at OMC. (Final 1 mark for comment.)

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**(a)** Enumerate the **factors affecting compaction** of soils. With suitable diagrams, explain how **compactive effort** and **soil type** influence the shape and position of the compaction curve. (7 marks)
**(b)** Define **Relative Compaction** and **Relative Compactive Effort**. A field subgrade is compacted at a dry density of $1.65 \ \text{g/cc}$ with an in-situ water content of $11\ \%$. The lab Proctor test gave MDD = $1.75 \ \text{g/cc}$ at OMC = $12\ \%$. Comment on the field compaction with respect to (i) IS Light Compaction specification, and (ii) the air-voids condition. Assume $G = 2.70$. (7 marks)

> **Model Solution:**
> **(a) Factors affecting compaction (7 marks):**
> 1. **Water content (2 marks):** Initially, $\rho_d$ rises with $w$ as water lubricates grains; beyond OMC, water displaces solids and $\rho_d$ falls.
> 2. **Compactive effort (2 marks):** Higher energy $\rightarrow$ higher MDD and lower OMC; curve shifts upward-left.
> 3. **Soil type (1.5 marks):** Cohesive soils have lower MDD and higher OMC; granular soils have higher MDD and lower OMC.
> 4. **Method of compaction** (static, kneading, vibratory) — affects fabric. (1 mark)
> 5. **Admixtures, lift thickness, number of passes** (0.5 mark).
> **(b) Relative compaction & analysis (7 marks):**
> **Definition (1 mark):** $\text{Relative Compaction} = \dfrac{\rho_{d,\text{field}}}{\rho_{d,\text{max,lab}}} \times 100\ (\%)$.
> **Definition (1 mark):** Relative Compactive Effort = $\dfrac{E_{\text{field}}}{E_{\text{lab}}} \times 100\ (\%)$ — ratio of field energy to lab energy.
> **Step 1 — Relative compaction (1 mark):**

$$
\dfrac{1.65}{1.75} \times 100 \ =\ 94.3\ \%
$$

> **Step 2 — Comment on IS specification (2 marks):** As per IS 2720 (Part 7) and MoRTH specifications for embankments, relative compaction $\ge 95\ \%$ (for subgrade) is desired. The field value of $94.3\ \%$ is **slightly below the spec — RE-COMPACTION RECOMMENDED**, OR an additional pass should be given.
> **Step 3 — Air-voids check (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho_{d,\text{ZAV at } 11\%} \ &=\ \dfrac{2.70 \times 1}{1 + 2.70 \times 0.11} \ =\ \dfrac{2.70}{1.297} \ =\ 2.082 \ \text{g/cc} \\
\text{Air voids } a_v \ &=\ 1 \ -\ \dfrac{\rho_d}{G \cdot \rho_w} \ =\ 1 \ -\ \dfrac{1.65}{2.70} \ =\ 0.389 \ \approx \ 38.9\ \%
\end{aligned}
$$

> Air voids of $\sim 39\ \%$ are **excessively high** for a finished subgrade (typical target $a_v \le 10\ \%$). The compaction is unsatisfactory on both counts — **more compactive effort is required** before the next lift is placed. (Final conclusion 1 mark.)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common mark-loss point 1:** Students forget to convert moisture content from **percent to decimal** in the formula $\rho_d = \dfrac{\rho}{1+w}$. Always use $w = 12\ \% = 0.12$.
> 2. **Common mark-loss point 2:** Many students plot *bulk* density instead of *dry* density. The peak of the bulk-density curve is **NOT** the MDD — it must be plotted as dry density.
> 3. **Common mark-loss point 3:** Forgetting to mention **IS 2720 (Part 7) – 1980 (Reaffirmed 2010)** in the procedure — losing 1 full mark.
> 4. **Common mark-loss point 4:** Not mentioning the **maturation period** (24 hours curing) after adding water — losing 1 mark.
> 5. **Common mark-loss point 5:** In the 14-mark question, students often miss the **ZAV verification** — losing 2 marks.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Test name:** Light Compaction Test / Standard Proctor Test.
- **IS code:** **IS 2720 (Part 7) – 1980 (Reaffirmed 2010)**.
- **Mould:** Volume = **$1000 \ \text{cc}$**, diameter = $100 \ \text{mm}$, height = $127.3 \ \text{mm}$.
- **Rammer:** Mass = **$2.6 \ \text{kg}$**, drop = **$310 \ \text{mm}$**.
- **Compaction:** **3 layers × 25 blows** = $604.5 \ \text{kJ/m}^3$ energy.
- **Sieve:** Soil passing **$4.75 \ \text{mm}$** IS sieve.
- **Key outputs:** **MDD** (peak of dry-density curve) and **OMC** (corresponding water content).
- **ZAV line:** $\rho_{d,\text{ZAV}} = \dfrac{G \cdot \rho_w}{1 + wG}$ — always lies *above* the compaction curve.
- **Bulk density:** $\rho = \dfrac{M_2 - M_1}{V}$.
- **Dry density:** $\rho_d = \dfrac{\rho}{1+w}$ (use $w$ in decimal).
- **Curve shape:** Bell-shaped; dry side (left of peak) is brittle, wet side (right of peak) is plastic.
- **Heavy Compaction comparison:** Modified Proctor (IS 2720 Part 8) uses a $4.9 \ \text{kg}$ rammer, $450 \ \text{mm}$ drop, 5 layers × 25 blows — yields higher MDD and lower OMC.
- **Sample mass required:** 3 to 4 kg per trial; 4 to 5 trials minimum.
- **Maturation:** 24-hour sealed curing after water addition to homogenize moisture.
- **Oversize correction:** When field soil has > 5 % particles retained on $4.75 \ \text{mm}$ sieve, correct MDD and OMC using the standard USBR equations.
- **Practical use:** Field specifications are stated as **Relative Compaction = $95\ \text{to}\ 100\ \%$** of MDD (depending on project).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Direct Shear Test
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCEL507 - GEOTECHNICAL ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Direct Shear Test

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE EXPERIMENT 2 — DIRECT SHEAR TEST

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Direct Shear Test** (also called the **Shear Box Test**) is a laboratory test performed on a soil specimen to determine its **shear strength parameters** — namely the **cohesion (c)** and the **angle of internal friction (φ)** — under a specified rate of straining and drainage conditions. The test is governed by **IS 2720 (Part 13): 1986 (Reaffirmed 2021)** and is the most fundamental and widely used method in KTU undergraduate geotechnical laboratories.

In this test, a soil specimen is placed in a rigid metal **shear box** that is split horizontally into two halves. A controlled **normal force (N)** is applied vertically, and an increasing **horizontal shear force (P)** is applied to the upper half of the box until the specimen fails along a pre-defined horizontal plane (the **shear plane** or **failure plane**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Direct Shear Test is the **second mandatory experiment** under Module 2 of PCCEL507 — Geotechnical Engineering Lab. It is consistently assessed in the **Continuous Evaluation (CE)** component, viva voce, and the **End Semester Practical Examination (ESE)**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Deck of Cards" Intuition

Imagine holding a **deck of playing cards** firmly between your palms. Your palms push the deck from the top and bottom (this is the **normal force**, holding the cards together). Now, if you slowly slide your upper palm forward while keeping your lower palm fixed, the cards will start to **slide relative to each other**, one over the other. The deck resists your sliding effort, and eventually, all the cards slip at once — this is **shear failure**.

The shear strength of the deck comes from the **friction** between adjacent card surfaces. This is *exactly* how a Direct Shear Test works:

| Real-World Analogy | Direct Shear Test Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Upper palm pressing down | Normal load (N) on specimen |
| Lower palm fixed | Lower half of shear box (stationary) |
| Sliding of upper palm | Horizontal shear load (P) on upper half |
| Friction between cards | Soil's internal friction (φ) |
| Stickiness/glue between cards | Soil's cohesion (c) |
| Sudden slip of all cards | Shear failure of specimen |

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Summary:** The Direct Shear Test literally **forces the soil to slide along a horizontal plane** while measuring how much shear resistance the soil offers. The relationship between the normal stress and the maximum shear stress gives us the **Mohr-Coulomb failure envelope**, from which c and φ are obtained.

### 1.3 Physical Constants, Standard Dimensions & IS Code Reference

- **Standard Specimen Size:** **60 mm × 60 mm × 25 mm** (length × width × height) for fine-grained soils; **100 mm × 100 mm × 50 mm** for coarse-grained soils.
- **IS Code:** **IS 2720 (Part 13): 1986**
- **Standard Shear Box Material:** Mild steel, with two halves rigidly held by guide pins and locking screws.
- **Loading Rate (Fine-grained soils):** **1.25 mm/min**
- **Loading Rate (Coarse-grained soils):** **0.5 mm/min**
- **Proving Ring Constant:** Reported on the proving ring (typically in N/division).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Mohr-Coulomb Failure Criterion — The "Why" Behind the Test

The Direct Shear Test is built upon the **Mohr-Coulomb Failure Criterion**, which states that a soil fails when the **shear stress (τ)** on any plane reaches a critical value given by:

$$\tau_f = c + \sigma_n \tan(\phi)$$

Where:
- $\tau_f$ = Shear strength of the soil at failure (kN/m² or kPa)
- $c$ = Cohesion of the soil (kPa) — the "sticky" component
- $\sigma_n$ = Normal stress on the failure plane (kPa)
- $\phi$ = Angle of internal friction (degrees) — the "frictional" component

The Direct Shear Test forces failure along a **single horizontal plane**, so the orientation of the failure plane is **pre-defined** (unlike the Triaxial Test where the failure plane develops naturally).

### 2.2 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step (How the Test Works)

1. **Specimen Preparation:** Soil is compacted or remolded into the lower half of the shear box at the desired dry density and moisture content. The upper half is placed and locked.
2. **Porous Stones & Filter Paper:** Placed at top and bottom to allow drainage (for consolidated-drained tests).
3. **Normal Loading:** A vertical load is applied through a loading cap using dead weights on a loading yoke. This creates the normal stress $\sigma_n$.
4. **Locking Pins Removal:** The locking pins (which hold the two halves together) are removed, allowing relative movement.
5. **Shear Loading:** A horizontal force is applied at a constant rate using a motorized gear system. The force is measured by a **proving ring**, and the horizontal displacement by a **dial gauge**.
6. **Failure Detection:** Failure is identified when the proving ring dial reaches its **maximum reading** (peak shear) or when **continuous displacement** occurs without further increase in load (for dense sands and overconsolidated clays).
7. **Repeat:** The test is repeated on **three identical specimens** with three different normal loads (e.g., 50 N, 100 N, 150 N).

### 2.3 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why three specimens?** We need at least **three points** to plot a straight line $\tau_f$ vs. $\sigma_n$ and extract c and φ.
- **Why constant rate?** It ensures **drained conditions** (excess pore pressure dissipates as fast as it builds up), which is critical for valid c and φ values.
- **Why remove locking pins?** To allow the upper box to slide; otherwise the test measures the **box's** resistance, not the soil's.
- **Why horizontal failure plane only?** Simplicity — it's the easiest plane to enforce. The drawback is that the failure plane orientation is **forced**, not the natural one.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|----------|---------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Normal Stress | $\sigma_n = \dfrac{N}{A}$ | kN/m² or kPa | N = applied normal force, A = corrected area |
| 2 | Shear Stress | $\tau = \dfrac{P}{A}$ | kPa | P = horizontal shear force at failure |
| 3 | Shear Force from Proving Ring | $P = L \times C_r$ | N | L = proving ring reading (divisions), C_r = proving ring constant (N/div) |
| 4 | Corrected Area (during shearing) | $A_c = A_0 \left(1 - \dfrac{\Delta}{H}\right)$ | mm² or m² | $A_0$ = initial area, Δ = horizontal displacement, H = initial specimen height |
| 5 | Mohr-Coulomb Equation | $\tau_f = c + \sigma_n \tan(\phi)$ | kPa | Failure criterion |
| 6 | Cohesion (y-intercept) | $c$ = y-intercept of $\tau_f$ vs $\sigma_n$ plot | kPa | Obtained graphically |
| 7 | Angle of Internal Friction | $\phi = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{\Delta \tau_f}{\Delta \sigma_n}\right)$ | degrees | Slope of the failure envelope line |
| 8 | Initial Area (60×60 box) | $A_0 = 3600$ | mm² | For 60 mm × 60 mm box |
| 9 | Initial Area (100×100 box) | $A_0 = 10000$ | mm² | For 100 mm × 100 mm box |
| 10 | Dry Density of Specimen | $\rho_d = \dfrac{W_s}{V}$ | g/cc or kN/m³ | W_s = weight of solids, V = volume of box |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Insight:** Always use the **corrected area** ($A_c$), not the initial area, when calculating shear stress for displacements beyond the peak. The area reduces as the upper half slides over the lower half, increasing the actual stress on the failure plane.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The c and φ values obtained from this test are used directly in:

- **Bearing Capacity Calculations** for shallow foundations (Terzaghi, Meyerhof, IS Code equations).
- **Earth Pressure Computations** (Rankine's and Coulomb's theory) for retaining walls.
- **Stability Analysis** of slopes, embankments, and earth dams.
- **Pile Capacity** estimation (skin friction component).
- **Pavement Subgrade** strength assessment (CBR correlations with c and φ).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Corrected Area During Shearing

### Step 1: Geometric Setup

The shear box has an initial length $L_0$, width $B_0$, and specimen height $H$. Initially, the upper half sits exactly above the lower half, so the contact area is:

$$A_0 = L_0 \times B_0$$

### Step 2: After Displacement

As the upper half slides by a horizontal displacement $\Delta$ relative to the lower half, the contact (overlap) area between the two halves is **reduced**. The effective length of contact becomes $(L_0 - \Delta)$, while the width remains $B_0$ (the box is constrained laterally).

$$A_c = (L_0 - \Delta) \times B_0$$

### Step 3: Express in Terms of Initial Area

Factoring out $L_0 \times B_0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_c &= L_0 \cdot B_0 \left(1 - \dfrac{\Delta}{L_0}\right) \\
A_c &= A_0 \left(1 - \dfrac{\Delta}{L_0}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Note on the Term $\Delta / H$ vs $\Delta / L_0$

In many KTU textbook references, the formula is written as $A_c = A_0 \left(1 - \dfrac{\Delta}{H}\right)$. The strict geometric derivation gives $L_0$ in the denominator, but for a **square box** ($L_0 = B_0$) and the **small-displacement approximation**, $H \approx L_0$ is sometimes loosely applied. **The KTU-accepted convention is to use $\Delta / H$** (where H is the specimen height in the box), following the IS code simplification. This is what students should use in exams.

$$
\boxed{A_c = A_0 \left(1 - \dfrac{\Delta}{H}\right)}
$$

**Conversion logic:** As Δ → 0, A_c → A_0 (intuitive, no displacement means no area reduction). As Δ → H, A_c → 0 (the box has completely slid out, contact lost). This makes physical sense.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Shear Stress at Failure

The horizontal force P is measured by a proving ring:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= L_{peak} \times C_r \\
\tau &= \dfrac{P}{A_c} = \dfrac{L_{peak} \times C_r}{A_0 \left(1 - \dfrac{\Delta_{peak}}{H}\right)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- $L_{peak}$ = Proving ring reading at failure (in divisions)
- $C_r$ = Proving ring constant (N per division)
- $A_0$ = Initial area of the shear box
- $\Delta_{peak}$ = Horizontal displacement corresponding to peak load
- $H$ = Initial specimen height (typically 25 mm for 60×60 box)

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Extracting c and φ from the Failure Envelope

Once three (or more) specimens are tested with normal loads $N_1, N_2, N_3$, the corresponding failure shear stresses $\tau_1, \tau_2, \tau_3$ are computed.

Plot $\tau_f$ (y-axis) against $\sigma_n$ (x-axis). The best-fit straight line through the three points gives:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_f &= c + \sigma_n \tan(\phi) \\
\text{slope} &= \tan(\phi) = \dfrac{\tau_2 - \tau_1}{\sigma_{n2} - \sigma_{n1}} \\
\text{y-intercept} &= c
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\phi = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{\tau_2 - \tau_1}{\sigma_{n2} - \sigma_{n1}}\right) \quad ; \quad c = \tau_f \text{ when } \sigma_n = 0}
$$

## 3.4 Worked-Out KTU-Style Sample Calculation

**Given Data (typical KTU lab problem):**

- Shear box size: 60 mm × 60 mm × 25 mm → $A_0 = 3600 \text{ mm}^2 = 0.0036 \text{ m}^2$
- Proving ring constant, $C_r = 0.01 \text{ kN/div}$
- Three tests with normal loads: 0.10 kN, 0.20 kN, 0.30 kN
- Peak proving ring readings: 28 div, 48 div, 68 div
- Horizontal displacement at peak (assume negligible area correction, Δ/H ≈ 0 for simplicity in this illustration).

**Solution:**

### Step 1: Normal Stress for Each Test

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{n1} &= \dfrac{0.10}{0.0036} = 27.78 \text{ kPa} \\
\sigma_{n2} &= \dfrac{0.20}{0.0036} = 55.56 \text{ kPa} \\
\sigma_{n3} &= \dfrac{0.30}{0.0036} = 83.33 \text{ kPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Shear Force at Failure for Each Test

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_1 &= 28 \times 0.01 = 0.28 \text{ kN} \\
P_2 &= 48 \times 0.01 = 0.48 \text{ kN} \\
P_3 &= 68 \times 0.01 = 0.68 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Shear Stress at Failure

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_1 &= \dfrac{0.28}{0.0036} = 77.78 \text{ kPa} \\
\tau_2 &= \dfrac{0.48}{0.0036} = 133.33 \text{ kPa} \\
\tau_3 &= \dfrac{0.68}{0.0036} = 188.89 \text{ kPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Plotting and Extracting c and φ

Tabulated data:

| Test | $\sigma_n$ (kPa) | $\tau_f$ (kPa) |
|------|------------------|----------------|
| 1    | 27.78            | 77.78          |
| 2    | 55.56            | 133.33         |
| 3    | 83.33            | 188.89         |

From the linear fit (or using the slope formula):

$$
\tan(\phi) = \dfrac{188.89 - 77.78}{83.33 - 27.78} = \dfrac{111.11}{55.55} = 2.000
$$

$$
\phi = \tan^{-1}(2.000) = 63.43^\circ
$$

For c, substitute into the Mohr-Coulomb equation using test 1:

$$
\begin{aligned}
c &= \tau_1 - \sigma_{n1} \tan(\phi) \\
c &= 77.78 - 27.78 \times 2.000 \\
c &= 77.78 - 55.56 = 22.22 \text{ kPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** $c = 22.22 \text{ kPa}, \quad \phi = 63.43^\circ$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Note:** The above result has unusually high φ for a soil because it is illustrative. Real soils typically have $\phi$ between **0° and 45°**. Always sanity-check your answers!

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — Direct Shear Apparatus

```mermaid
graph TD
    LOAD_YOKE["Loading Yoke With Dead Weights"]
    UPPER_BOX["Upper Half of Shear Box - Movable"]
    SPECIMEN["Soil Specimen 60x60x25 mm"]
    LOWER_BOX["Lower Half of Shear Box - Stationary"]
    POROUS["Porous Stones Top and Bottom"]
    SHEAR_MOTOR["Motorized Gear System Horizontal Drive"]
    PROVING["Proving Ring Force Transducer"]
    H_DIAL["Horizontal Dial Gauge Displacement"]
    V_DIAL["Vertical Dial Gauge Dilation"]
    N_LOCK["Locking Pins - To Be Removed Before Shear"]

    LOAD_YOKE -- applies vertical N --> UPPER_BOX
    UPPER_BOX -- contains upper half of --> SPECIMEN
    SPECIMEN -- rests on --> LOWER_BOX
    POROUS -- at top and bottom of --> SPECIMEN
    LOWER_BOX -- fixed to base plate --> SHEAR_MOTOR
    SHEAR_MOTOR -- pushes upper half via --> PROVING
    UPPER_BOX -- is connected to --> PROVING
    PROVING -- reads shear force P --> H_DIAL
    SPECIMEN -- dilates or contracts --> V_DIAL
```
