```mermaid
    N_LOCK -- locks halves initially --> UPPER_BOX
```

## 4.2 Block Flow Diagram — Test Procedure Sequence

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1["Step 1: Clean and assemble shear box halves"]
    S2["Step 2: Place porous stone and filter paper in lower half"]
    S3["Step 3: Compact soil specimen to required density"]
    S4["Step 4: Place upper porous stone and loading cap"]
    S5["Step 5: Apply normal load N on loading yoke"]
    S6["Step 6: Set proving ring and dial gauges to zero"]
    S7["Step 7: Remove locking pins carefully"]
    S8["Step 8: Start motor at specified rate 1.25 mm/min"]
    S9["Step 9: Record P and horizontal displacement at intervals"]
    S10["Step 10: Note peak proving ring reading and continue till failure"]
    S11["Step 11: Repeat with two more normal loads"]
    S12["Step 12: Plot tau vs sigma and obtain c and phi"]

    S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5 --> S6 --> S7 --> S8 --> S9 --> S10 --> S11 --> S12
```

## 4.3 Typical Plot — Failure Envelope (Conceptual)

```mermaid
graph LR
    ORIGIN["Origin 0,0"]
    POINT1["Test 1: sigma_n1, tau_1"]
    POINT2["Test 2: sigma_n2, tau_2"]
    POINT3["Test 3: sigma_n3, tau_3"]
    LINE["Best Fit Straight Line Slope = tan phi"]
    Y_INT["Y Intercept = Cohesion c"]

    ORIGIN --> POINT1
    POINT1 --> POINT2
    POINT2 --> POINT3
    POINT1 -. lies on .-> LINE
    POINT2 -. lies on .-> LINE
    POINT3 -. lies on .-> LINE
    LINE -. intercepts y axis at .-> Y_INT
```

## 4.4 Apparatus Component Description (Tabular Reference)

| Component | Function | Key Specification |
|-----------|----------|-------------------|
| Shear Box | Holds specimen, splits horizontally | 60×60×25 mm or 100×100×50 mm |
| Proving Ring | Measures shear force P | Calibrated constant in N/div |
| Loading Yoke | Transmits normal load to specimen | Dead weight hanger |
| Dial Gauges | Measure horizontal and vertical displacement | Least count 0.01 mm |
| Motorized Gear | Applies constant-rate horizontal displacement | 1.25 mm/min or 0.5 mm/min |
| Porous Stones | Allow drainage from top and bottom | Sintered brass or bronze |
| Locking Pins | Hold two halves together initially | Removed before shearing |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question 1: Define the Direct Shear Test. State its IS code reference. `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

The Direct Shear Test is a laboratory test conducted to determine the **shear strength parameters** of a soil, namely the **cohesion (c)** and the **angle of internal friction (φ)**. A soil specimen confined in a split-box is subjected to a vertical normal load and a horizontal shear force until failure occurs along a pre-defined horizontal plane. The test is conducted as per **IS 2720 (Part 13): 1986** (Reaffirmed 2021).

The normal stress and corresponding maximum shear stress from a minimum of **three tests** are plotted to obtain the failure envelope, from which c and φ are derived using the **Mohr-Coulomb** equation: $\tau_f = c + \sigma_n \tan(\phi)$.

---

### Question 2: List the advantages and disadvantages of the Direct Shear Test. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer:**

**Advantages:**
1. Test apparatus is **simple, robust, and inexpensive** to operate.
2. **Thick specimens** can be tested, especially for granular soils and gravels.
3. The **rate of shearing** can be precisely controlled, allowing drained tests on low-permeability soils.
4. **Quick** to perform compared to triaxial testing.
5. **Drainage** is easy to achieve via porous stones.

**Disadvantages:**
1. The **failure plane is forced** to be horizontal — not the weakest plane of the soil.
2. **Stress distribution** along the failure plane is non-uniform, especially near the edges.
3. The **area of contact decreases** as shearing progresses (requires area correction).
4. **Pore water pressure** cannot be measured during the test.
5. The **principal stresses** cannot be directly determined or controlled.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**Q. A (a)** A Direct Shear Test is conducted on a soil specimen of size 60 mm × 60 mm × 25 mm. The proving ring constant is 0.012 kN/division. Three tests are performed with normal loads of 0.15 kN, 0.30 kN, and 0.45 kN. The corresponding peak proving ring readings are 22, 42, and 62 divisions. Determine the cohesion and angle of internal friction of the soil. (7 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute the initial area of the specimen**

$$A_0 = 60 \times 60 = 3600 \text{ mm}^2 = 0.0036 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2: Compute normal stresses**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{n1} &= \dfrac{0.15}{0.0036} = 41.67 \text{ kPa} \\
\sigma_{n2} &= \dfrac{0.30}{0.0036} = 83.33 \text{ kPa} \\
\sigma_{n3} &= \dfrac{0.45}{0.0036} = 125.00 \text{ kPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute shear forces at peak using the proving ring**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_1 &= 22 \times 0.012 = 0.264 \text{ kN} \\
P_2 &= 42 \times 0.012 = 0.504 \text{ kN} \\
P_3 &= 62 \times 0.012 = 0.744 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Compute shear stresses (using initial area, ignoring small area correction)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_1 &= \dfrac{0.264}{0.0036} = 73.33 \text{ kPa} \\
\tau_2 &= \dfrac{0.504}{0.0036} = 140.00 \text{ kPa} \\
\tau_3 &= \dfrac{0.744}{0.0036} = 206.67 \text{ kPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Tabulate the results**

| Test | Normal Stress $\sigma_n$ (kPa) | Shear Stress $\tau_f$ (kPa) |
|------|--------------------------------|------------------------------|
| 1    | 41.67                          | 73.33                        |
| 2    | 83.33                          | 140.00                       |
| 3    | 125.00                         | 206.67                       |

**Step 6: Plot $\tau_f$ vs $\sigma_n$ and fit a straight line** (or use two-point formula)

$$
\tan(\phi) = \dfrac{206.67 - 73.33}{125.00 - 41.67} = \dfrac{133.34}{83.33} = 1.600
$$

$$
\phi = \tan^{-1}(1.600) = 57.99^\circ \approx 58.0^\circ
$$

**Step 7: Calculate cohesion c using any test (using test 1)**

$$
c = \tau_1 - \sigma_{n1} \tan(\phi) = 73.33 - 41.67 \times 1.600 = 73.33 - 66.67 = 6.66 \text{ kPa}
$$

**Final Answer:**

$$
\boxed{c = 6.66 \text{ kPa} \quad ; \quad \phi = 58.0^\circ}
$$

---

**Q. A (b)** Explain with a neat sketch the working of the Direct Shear Test apparatus. List the IS code and the standard specimen size. (7 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**Apparatus Components (refer to Section 4 diagrams):**

The Direct Shear Box apparatus consists of:

1. **Shear Box:** A square box split horizontally into two halves, made of brass or mild steel. The lower half is fixed to the base, and the upper half is free to slide horizontally. Standard sizes are 60×60×25 mm (fine soils) and 100×100×50 mm (coarse soils).

2. **Loading Yoke and Dead Weights:** A vertical loading system that applies a **constant normal load (N)** to the top of the specimen through a loading cap.

3. **Proving Ring:** A calibrated **annular spring ring** mounted on the upper box. The horizontal force P is transmitted through this ring, and the dial gauge on the ring measures the force in divisions.

4. **Dial Gauges:** One for **horizontal displacement** and another for **vertical displacement** (to capture dilatancy or contraction).

5. **Motorized Gear System:** A constant-rate horizontal loading device (1.25 mm/min for fine soils, 0.5 mm/min for coarse soils).

6. **Porous Stones and Filter Papers:** Placed at top and bottom of the specimen to facilitate drainage.

**Working Principle:**

The soil specimen is placed in the shear box and consolidated under the applied normal load N. The locking pins are removed, and a horizontal shear force P is applied at a constant rate. The shear force is measured via the proving ring, and the horizontal displacement is recorded via the dial gauge. Failure is identified at the peak proving ring reading.

**IS Code:** IS 2720 (Part 13): 1986
**Standard Specimen Size:** 60 mm × 60 mm × 25 mm

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks)

**Q. B (a)** What are the different drainage conditions under which a Direct Shear Test can be performed? Describe each briefly. (7 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

The Direct Shear Test can be performed under three drainage conditions depending on the field situation being simulated:

1. **Unconsolidated Undrained (UU) Test:**
   - Specimen is sheared **immediately** after applying the normal load, without allowing drainage or consolidation.
   - Pore water pressure builds up and is **not measured**.
   - Simulates rapid loading in the field (e.g., during an earthquake or sudden embankment construction).
   - Used to determine the **undrained shear strength (c_u)**.

2. **Consolidated Undrained (CU) Test:**
   - Specimen is allowed to **consolidate fully** under the normal load (drainage is permitted) before shearing.
   - During shearing, drainage valves are **closed** — pore pressure builds up but is generally not measured in the standard direct shear apparatus.
   - Simulates the field condition where the soil has consolidated under long-term loading but is subjected to a rapid additional load.

3. **Consolidated Drained (CD) Test:**
   - Specimen is consolidated fully, and then sheared **very slowly** to ensure **no excess pore pressure** develops during shearing.
   - Rate of shearing is low enough to allow full drainage (1.25 mm/min for fine-grained soils).
   - Used to determine the **drained shear strength parameters c' and φ'** (effective stress parameters).
   - Simulates the field condition where the soil is loaded slowly (e.g., a building constructed over many months).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** For most undergraduate lab practicals in Kerala, the **CD (Consolidated Drained)** test is the default, since the apparatus does not measure pore water pressure.

---

**Q. B (b)** Describe the test procedure for performing a Direct Shear Test in the laboratory. State the precautions to be taken. (7 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Test Procedure:**

1. **Clean and assemble the shear box.** Place the lower half in position and ensure it is properly seated on the base.
2. **Place a porous stone and filter paper** in the lower half of the box.
3. **Compact the soil specimen** to the required dry density and moisture content inside the lower half.
4. **Place the upper half of the box**, ensuring proper alignment. Add a filter paper and porous stone on top of the soil.
5. **Position the loading cap** and place the entire assembly under the loading frame.
6. **Apply the desired normal load (N)** on the loading yoke using dead weights.
7. **Set the proving ring dial gauge and the horizontal displacement dial gauge to zero.**
8. **Carefully remove the locking pins** that hold the two halves of the box together.
9. **Start the motor** at the specified rate (1.25 mm/min for fine-grained, 0.5 mm/min for coarse-grained).
10. **Record readings** of the proving ring and horizontal dial gauge at regular intervals of displacement (e.g., every 0.5 mm).
11. **Continue the test** until the proving ring reading reaches a peak and then drops, or until 15–20% horizontal strain is achieved.
12. **Reverse the motor** to unload, remove the specimen, and repeat with a fresh specimen at a different normal load.

**Precautions:**

1. The **locking pins must be removed** before applying the shear force; otherwise, the box's resistance (not the soil's) will be measured.
2. The **rate of shearing** must be carefully controlled to ensure drained conditions for fine soils.
3. The **proving ring** should be calibrated and its constant verified before the test.
4. The **specimen** must be uniformly compacted to avoid density variations.
5. **Filter papers** should be placed correctly to prevent soil particles from clogging the porous stones.
6. **Dial gauges** should be properly zeroed and checked for sensitivity.
7. The **top porous stone must be saturated** to prevent drying of the specimen during the test.
8. The **shear box must be free from rust** and properly cleaned to ensure smooth sliding.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in KTU Practical Exams (PCCEL507):**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units:** Proving ring constant is often given in N/div, while area is in mm² or m². Convert to a **consistent unit system** (SI: N and m² → Pa) before computing stress. **Marks lost: 1–2 per error.**
> 2. **Using wrong area:** Students often use the initial area $A_0$ throughout, ignoring the **area correction** $A_c = A_0(1 - \Delta/H)$. KTU expects this correction for **displacements beyond 10–15% strain**.
> 3. **Wrong identification of "peak" load:** Failure is at the **maximum** proving ring reading, not the **final** reading. For loose sands and normally consolidated clays, the peak may not be obvious — students must carefully observe the dial.
> 4. **Inverting the axes:** In the $\tau_f$ vs $\sigma_n$ plot, $\sigma_n$ is on the **x-axis** and $\tau_f$ is on the **y-axis**. Reversing them gives an incorrect slope and wrong φ.
> 5. **Reporting c as a positive number for cohesionless soils:** For clean sand, c should be **≈ 0**; reporting c = 50 kPa for sand is a glaring error. Always sanity-check.
> 6. **Not stating the IS code:** Examiners often give 1 mark just for citing **IS 2720 (Part 13): 1986** explicitly.
> 7. **Ignoring units in the final answer:** Always write **c in kPa** and **φ in degrees**, never as a decimal or unitless.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### Rapid-Revision Checklist

- [ ] **Test Name:** Direct Shear Test (Shear Box Test)
- [ ] **IS Code:** IS 2720 (Part 13): 1986
- [ ] **Standard Specimen Size:** 60 mm × 60 mm × 25 mm (fine), 100 mm × 100 mm × 50 mm (coarse)
- [ ] **Failure Plane:** Horizontal (forced)
- [ ] **Number of Tests Required:** Minimum **3** with different normal loads
- [ ] **Loading Rate:** **1.25 mm/min** (fine-grained), **0.5 mm/min** (coarse-grained)
- [ ] **Core Formulas:** $\sigma_n = N/A$, $\tau = P/A$, $P = L \cdot C_r$, $A_c = A_0(1 - \Delta/H)$
- [ ] **Mohr-Coulomb Equation:** $\tau_f = c + \sigma_n \tan(\phi)$
- [ ] **c (cohesion)** = y-intercept of $\tau_f$ vs $\sigma_n$ plot (in kPa)
- [ ] **φ (angle of internal friction)** = $\tan^{-1}$ of slope of the failure envelope (in degrees)
- [ ] **Locking pins must be removed** before shearing — common mistake
- [ ] **Drainage is provided** via porous stones — test is typically drained (CD)
- [ ] **Pore water pressure** is **not measured** in this test
- [ ] **Area correction** must be applied for large horizontal displacements
- [ ] **Typical φ range:** 0° (pure clay) to 45° (dense sand/gravel)
- [ ] **Typical c range:** 0 kPa (clean sand) to 100+ kPa (stiff clay)
- [ ] **Plotting rule:** $\sigma_n$ on x-axis, $\tau_f$ on y-axis, draw best-fit straight line through at least 3 points
- [ ] **Failure indicator:** Peak proving ring reading, OR continuous displacement with no further load increase
- [ ] **Real-world use:** Bearing capacity, earth pressure, slope stability, pavement design

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic — "SHEAR BOX":**
> **S** — Split into two halves
> **H** — Horizontal failure plane
> **E** — Effective parameters obtained (c' and φ')
> **A** — Area correction needed
> **R** — Rate of shearing is critical
> **B** — Best-fit line through 3 points
> **O** — One horizontal plane (forced)
> **X** — eXamine the peak reading on the proving ring
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Unconfined Compression Test
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCEL507 - GEOTECHNICAL ENGINEERING LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Unconfined Compression Test ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!NOTE] > **Unconfined Compression Test (UCT)** is a rapid, economical, and widely used laboratory test performed on **cohesive (fine-grained) soils** to determine the **Unconfined Compressive Strength (qu)** of the soil, from which the **Undrained Shear Strength (cu)** of the soil is indirectly calculated. The test is conducted as per **IS 2720 (Part 10): 1991** – *Determination of Unconfined Compressive Strength of Soil*. It is essentially a special case of the **Triaxial Compression Test** in which the **confining pressure (σ₃) is reduced to zero**. The cylindrical soil specimen is subjected to an **axial compressive load** at a constant rate of strain until failure occurs or a specified strain is reached. **Key Conceptual Terms:** - **Unconfined Compressive Strength (qu):** The maximum axial compressive stress (force per unit area) that a soil specimen can withstand under zero lateral confinement, expressed in **kN/m²** or **kPa**. - **Undrained Shear Strength (cu):** The maximum resistance of a saturated cohesive soil to shearing under undrained conditions. - **Sensitivity (St):** The ratio of the undisturbed shear strength to the remoulded shear strength, indicating the loss of strength upon remoulding. - **Strain-Controlled Loading:** In this test, the specimen is loaded at a **constant rate of strain** (typically 0.5% to 2% per minute), not at a constant rate of stress. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The UCT is strictly applicable only to **cohesive soils** (silts and clays) that possess sufficient cohesion to stand unsupported as a cylindrical specimen. **Granular (cohesionless) soils cannot be tested** as they would collapse without lateral confinement. --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine squeezing a **soft toffey or a piece of wet clay** between your thumb and index finger, but only from the top — there is no side support or wrapping around it. The maximum force the toffey can resist before it crumbles or shears is analogous to its **Unconfined Compressive Strength (qu)**. - **Why zero confinement?** Because in real field conditions, many natural clay deposits are often saturated and tested immediately (undrained), and we want a **quick estimate** of how strong the clay is in shear without the complexity of a triaxial test. - **Why is it fast?** There is no need to apply cell pressure, saturate the specimen with back pressure, or wait for pore pressure equalization — you simply push the specimen down and record the load. > [!TIP] > **Real-World Analogy for Students:** Think of the UCT as a "**one-sided push**" test — you push down on a column of clay that has **no walls around it**, and you see how much load it can take before it bulges and shears. The "bulge and shear" pattern on the failure surface is what gives us the soil's shear strength. ### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics - **Standard Strain Rate:** **0.5% to 2.0% per minute** (as per IS 2720 Part 10). Most labs use **1.2% per minute**. - **Standard Specimen Dimensions:** Length-to-diameter ratio (L/D) of approximately **2.0 to 2.5**. Typical diameter: **38 mm to 100 mm**. Minimum specimen diameter must be at least **10 times the largest particle size**. - **Unit Weight of Water (γw):** **9.81 kN/m³** (often rounded to **10 kN/m³** for academic problems in KTU exams). - **Failure Strain:** Typically reached between **5% to 20% axial strain**, depending on soil type (soft clays fail at higher strains, stiff clays at lower strains). ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation The Unconfined Compression Test is based on the **Mohr-Coulomb Failure Criterion** under the specific condition of **zero lateral confinement**. **Mohr-Coulomb Equation (General Form):** $$\tau_f = c + \sigma \tan\phi$$ **For a Saturated Cohesive Soil under Undrained Conditions (φᵤ = 0):** - The angle of internal friction under undrained conditions is **assumed to be zero** (φᵤ = 0) for **saturated clays**. - Therefore, the shear strength becomes independent of normal stress and equals the cohesion: $$\tau_f = c_u = \frac{q_u}{2}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Why φᵤ = 0?** For **saturated clays tested under undrained (rapid) loading**, the pore water pressure increases and the effective stress remains constant. Since shear strength depends on effective stress (τ = c' + σ' tan φ'), and the effective stress does not change during the test, the soil behaves as if it has **no frictional resistance (φᵤ = 0)**. The entire shear strength comes from cohesion, called the **apparent cohesion (cᵤ)**. ### 2.2 Step-by-Step Theoretical Logic **Step 1: Application of Axial Load** An axial compressive load (P) is applied to the cylindrical soil specimen at a constant rate of strain, with **zero lateral confinement (σ₃ = 0)**. **Step 2: Calculation of Axial Stress** The axial stress at any given strain (ε) is calculated as: $$\sigma = \frac{P}{A_c}$$ where $A_c$ is the corrected cross-sectional area accounting for the bulging of the specimen during compression. **Step 3: Area Correction (Volumetric Constancy)** As the specimen shortens, it bulges outward. Assuming the volume remains constant: $$A_c = \frac{A_0}{1 - \varepsilon}$$ where $A_0$ is the original cross-sectional area and $\varepsilon$ is the axial strain (as a decimal). **Step 4: Determination of qu** The maximum axial stress recorded during the test, or the stress at 20% strain (whichever occurs first), is reported as the **Unconfined Compressive Strength (qu)**. **Step 5: Determination of cu** For a saturated clay under undrained loading (φᵤ = 0), the **Mohr circle** is a circle of radius **qu/2** that is tangent to a horizontal line. The shear strength is: $$c_u = \frac{q_u}{2}$$ **Step 6: Sensitivity Calculation** $$S_t = \frac{q_u \, (\text{undisturbed})}{q_r \, (\text{remoulded})}$$ ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Formula Table for KTU Board Exams** | # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Remarks | |---|-----------|---------|-------|---------| | 1 | Axial Strain | $\varepsilon = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0}$ | Dimensionless (or %) | $\Delta L$ = change in length | | 2 | Corrected Area | $A_c = \dfrac{A_0}{1 - \varepsilon}$ | mm² or cm² | Volume constancy assumption | | 3 | Axial Stress | $\sigma = \dfrac{P}{A_c}$ | kN/m² or kPa | At any given strain | | 4 | Unconfined Compressive Strength | $q_u = \max(\sigma)$ | kPa | Peak stress or 20% strain | | 5 | Undrained Shear Strength | $c_u = \dfrac{q_u}{2}$ | kPa | For saturated clays (φᵤ = 0) | | 6 | Sensitivity | $S_t = \dfrac{q_u}{q_r}$ | Dimensionless | Disturbed/Undisturbed | | 7 | Original Area | $A_0 = \dfrac{\pi D_0^2}{4}$ | mm² or cm² | $D_0$ = initial diameter | | 8 | Volume of Specimen | $V_0 = A_0 \cdot L_0$ | cm³ or mm³ | Initial volume | | 9 | Strain Rate | $\dot{\varepsilon} = \dfrac{\Delta L / L_0}{\Delta t} \times 100$ | %/min | IS Code: 0.5%–2%/min | ### 2.4 Classification of Soils Based on Sensitivity | Sensitivity (Sₜ) | Classification | |:----------------:|:--------------:| | 1 | Insensitive | | 1 – 2 | Low sensitivity | | 2 – 4 | Medium sensitivity | | 4 – 8 | High sensitivity | | 8 – 16 | Extra sensitive | | > 16 | Quick clays | ### 2.5 Typical Values of qu and cu (For Reference) | Soil Type | qu (kPa) | cu (kPa) | |-----------|----------|----------| | Very soft clay | < 25 | < 12.5 | | Soft clay | 25 – 50 | 12.5 – 25 | | Medium clay | 50 – 100 | 25 – 50 | | Stiff clay | 100 – 200 | 50 – 100 | | Very stiff clay | 200 – 400 | 100 – 200 | | Hard clay | > 400 | > 200 | ### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Foundation Design:** The value of cᵤ obtained from the UCT is directly used in calculating the **bearing capacity** of foundations on cohesive soils using **Terzaghi's or Skempton's equations**. - **Slope Stability Analysis:** Used in determining the Factor of Safety (FOS) of embankments, natural slopes, and excavations. - **Earth Pressure Calculations:** Used to compute **active and passive earth pressures** behind retaining walls (Rankine's or Coulomb's theory). - **Quality Control:** Quick estimation of soil strength at construction sites. - **Lime/Cement Stabilization:** Used to evaluate the effectiveness of soil stabilization by comparing qu before and after treatment. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Area Correction Formula **Starting Point:** We assume the soil specimen maintains **constant volume** during the test (incompressible behaviour of saturated soil under undrained conditions). **Step 1:** Initial volume of the cylindrical specimen: $$V_0 = A_0 \cdot L_0$$ **Step 2:** When the specimen is compressed by an axial load, it shortens axially and bulges laterally. By the constant volume assumption: $$V_0 = A_c \cdot L_c$$ **Step 3:** The compressed length is: $$L_c = L_0 - \Delta L$$ **Step 4:** By definition of axial strain: $$\varepsilon = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \Delta L = \varepsilon \cdot L_0$$ **Step 5:** Substituting: $$L_c = L_0 - \varepsilon \cdot L_0 = L_0 (1 - \varepsilon)$$ **Step 6:** Equating volumes: $$A_0 \cdot L_0 = A_c \cdot L_0 (1 - \varepsilon)$$ **Step 7:** Solving for $A_c$: $$A_c = \frac{A_0 \cdot L_0}{L_0 (1 - \varepsilon)} = \frac{A_0}{1 - \varepsilon}$$ **Hence, the corrected area is:** $$\boxed{A_c = \frac{A_0}{1 - \varepsilon}}$$ --- ### 3.2 Derivation of cu = qu/2 from Mohr's Circle **Step 1:** In the UCT, the confining pressure is zero: $$\sigma_3 = 0$$ **Step 2:** The major principal stress at failure equals the unconfined compressive strength: $$\sigma_1 = q_u$$ **Step 3:** For a saturated clay tested under undrained conditions, **φᵤ = 0** (Total Stress Analysis). The Mohr-Coulomb failure envelope is a **horizontal line**. **Step 4:** The Mohr circle at failure has: - Center at: $\sigma_c = \dfrac{\sigma_1 + \sigma_3}{2} = \dfrac{q_u + 0}{2} = \dfrac{q_u}{2}$ - Radius: $R = \dfrac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2} = \dfrac{q_u - 0}{2} = \dfrac{q_u}{2}$ **Step 5:** Since the failure envelope is horizontal (φᵤ = 0), the **shear strength equals the radius of the Mohr circle**: $$c_u = R = \frac{q_u}{2}$$ **Hence, the undrained shear strength is:** $$\boxed{c_u = \frac{q_u}{2}}$$ --- ### 3.3 Worked-Out Numerical Example (KTU Board Exam Style) **Problem Statement:** A cylindrical clay specimen was tested in the Unconfined Compression Test. The following data was recorded: - Initial length of specimen, $L_0 = 80$ mm - Initial diameter, $D_0 = 38$ mm - Load at failure, $P_f = 215$ N - Axial deformation at failure, $\Delta L_f = 8.5$ mm **Calculate:** 1. The axial strain at failure (in %) 2. The corrected cross-sectional area at failure 3. The Unconfined Compressive Strength (qu) 4. The Undrained Shear Strength (cᵤ) 5. The sensitivity, if the remoulded strength is 38 kPa --- **Solution:** **Part 1: Axial Strain at Failure** $$\varepsilon = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0} = \frac{8.5}{80} = 0.10625 = 10.625\%$$ **Part 2: Original Cross-Sectional Area** $$A_0 = \frac{\pi D_0^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 38^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 1444}{4} = 1133.54 \text{ mm}^2$$ **Part 3: Corrected Cross-Sectional Area at Failure** $$A_c = \frac{A_0}{1 - \varepsilon} = \frac{1133.54}{1 - 0.10625} = \frac{1133.54}{0.89375}$$ $$A_c = 1268.30 \text{ mm}^2$$ **Part 4: Unconfined Compressive Strength (qu)** $$q_u = \frac{P_f}{A_c} = \frac{215 \text{ N}}{1268.30 \text{ mm}^2} = 0.1695 \text{ N/mm}^2$$ $$q_u = 169.5 \text{ kN/m}^2 \text{ (or kPa)}$$ **Part 5: Undrained Shear Strength (cᵤ)** $$c_u = \frac{q_u}{2} = \frac{169.5}{2} = 84.75 \text{ kPa}$$ **Part 6: Sensitivity** $$S_t = \frac{q_u}{q_r} = \frac{169.5}{38} = 4.46$$ > [!NOTE] > **Classification:** Since $S_t$ is between 4 and 8, the clay is classified as **"High Sensitivity"**. --- ### 3.4 Sample Stress-Strain Data Reduction Table | Reading No. | Dial Gauge (mm) | Axial Strain $\varepsilon$ | Load P (N) | Corrected Area $A_c$ (mm²) | Stress $\sigma$ (kPa) | |:-----------:|:---------------:|:--------------------------:|:----------:|:--------------------------:|:---------------------:| | 1 | 0.0 | 0.000 | 0 | 1133.54 | 0.00 | | 2 | 1.0 | 0.0125 | 45 | 1147.85 | 39.21 | | 3 | 2.0 | 0.0250 | 88 | 1162.60 | 75.70 | | 4 | 4.0 | 0.0500 | 165 | 1193.20 | 138.29 | | 5 | 6.0 | 0.0750 | 210 | 1225.40 | 171.38 | | 6 | 8.0 | 0.1000 | 235 | 1259.49 | 186.59 | | 7 | 10.0 | 0.1250 | 220 | 1295.48 | 169.82 | | 8 | 12.0 | 0.1500 | 200 | 1333.58 | 149.97 | > [!NOTE] > The peak stress is observed at **Reading 6** ($\sigma = 186.59$ kPa). This is the **Unconfined Compressive Strength (qu)**. Beyond this point, the stress decreases, indicating **strain-softening** behaviour typical of **sensitive clays**. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow of the Unconfined Compression Test ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Soil Specimen Preparation
L by D ratio 2.0 to 2.5] --> B[Measurement of Initial
Dimensions L0 and D0] B --> C[Placement in Loading
Apparatus] C --> D[Application of Axial Load
at Constant Strain Rate
0.5 to 2 percent per min] D --> E[Recording of Load and
Deformation at Intervals] E --> F{Strain Exceeds
20 percent?} F -- No --> G[Continue Loading Until
Failure or 20 percent Strain] F -- Yes --> H[Stop Test] G --> I[Calculation of
Axial Strain epsilon] I --> J[Calculation of
Corrected Area Ac] J --> K[Calculation of
Axial Stress sigma] K --> L[Plotting of
Stress-Strain Curve] L --> M[Identification of Peak
Stress as qu] M --> N[Calculation of
cu equals qu by 2] N --> O[Calculation of
Sensitivity St] H --> I ``` ### 4.2 Block Diagram of the Testing Apparatus ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph A1[Loading Frame Assembly] A[Crosshead] --> B[Loading Platen] B --> C[Soil Specimen] C --> D[Base Platen] end subgraph A2[Measurement Systems] E[Proving Ring
or Load Cell] --> F[Deformation
Dial Gauge] end subgraph A3[Data Acquisition] F --> G[Manual Reading
at Intervals] G --> H[Tabulation and
Graph Plotting] end A1 <--> A2 A2 --> A3 ``` ### 4.3 Mohr Circle Representation for UCT (Zero Confinement) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph MohrCircle[Failure Mohr Circle for UCT] O[Origin] --> P[Point sigma3 equals 0] P --> Q[Point sigma1 equals qu] O --> R[Center at qu by 2] R --> S[Radius equals qu by 2] S --> T[cu equals qu by 2 on Tau axis] end ``` ### 4.4 Typical Stress-Strain Curve Features ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Origin] --> B[Initial Linear
Elastic Region] B --> C[Yield Point] C --> D{Soil Behaviour} D -- Stiff Clay --> E[Brittle Failure
Sharp Peak at low strain] D -- Soft Clay --> F[Ductile Failure
Gradual Peak at high strain] D -- Sensitive Clay --> G[Strain Softening
Peak then drop] E --> H[qu identified at peak] F --> H G --> H H --> I[If no peak, qu at 20 percent strain] ``` ### 4.5 List of Apparatus (KTU Lab Manual Standard) | Sl. No. | Apparatus | Function | |:-------:|-----------|----------| | 1 | Unconfined Compression Test Apparatus | Main loading frame with base and top platen | | 2 | Proving Ring (or Load Cell) | Measures the applied load accurately | | 3 | Dial Gauge (0.01 mm least count) | Measures axial deformation | | 4 | Specimen Trimming Tools | Wire saw, knife, soil lathe for sample preparation | | 5 | Vernier Calliper | Measures initial length and diameter | | 6 | Balance (0.01 g accuracy) | Measures mass for moisture content and bulk density | | 7 | Moisture Content Containers | For oven-drying | | 8 | Stopwatch | Records time for strain rate calculation | | 9 | Loading Platen (spherically seated) | Ensures uniform axial load application | | 10 | Membrane or Split Mould | For undisturbed sample extraction (if used) | ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *Define the Unconfined Compressive Strength (qu) of a soil. How is it related to the undrained shear strength (cᵤ) of a saturated clay?* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** - **Definition (1.5 Marks):** The Unconfined Compressive Strength (qu) is defined as the maximum axial compressive stress per unit area that a cylindrical specimen of cohesive soil can withstand when subjected to an axial load without any lateral confinement (i.e., σ₃ = 0). It is determined in the Unconfined Compression Test as per IS 2720 (Part 10): 1991. - **Relation to cᵤ (1.5 Marks):** For a saturated clay tested under undrained conditions, the angle of internal friction φᵤ is taken as zero (Total Stress Analysis). Under this assumption, the Mohr circle at failure has its center at qu/2 and radius qu/2. The horizontal failure envelope is tangent to the circle, giving: $c_u = \dfrac{q_u}{2}$. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *List any six apparatus required to perform the Unconfined Compression Test in the laboratory.* **Model Answer (3 Marks — 0.5 marks each):** 1. Unconfined Compression Test loading apparatus (loading frame with platens) 2. Proving ring (capacity 1 kN to 10 kN depending on soil) 3. Dial gauge (0.01 mm least count) 4. Vernier calliper 5. Specimen trimming tools (wire saw, knife, soil lathe) 6. Balance (0.01 g accuracy) --- ### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1** **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]** (a) *Explain the procedure for performing the Unconfined Compression Test on a cohesive soil specimen as per IS 2720 (Part 10). Mention the standard strain rate and specimen dimensions.* **(7 Marks)** (b) *The following data was obtained from a UCT performed on a clay sample. Plot the stress-strain curve and determine the unconfined compressive strength and the undrained shear strength.* | Strain (%) | Load (N) | |:----------:|:--------:| | 0 | 0 | | 2 | 65 | | 4 | 115 | | 6 | 155 | | 8 | 185 | | 10 | 200 | | 12 | 195 | | 14 | 180 | | 16 | 160 | *Initial diameter = 38 mm, Initial length = 76 mm.* **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution for (a):** **Procedure (5 Marks) — Step by Step:** 1. **Specimen Preparation:** An undisturbed cylindrical soil specimen is carefully trimmed from a large soil sample using a soil lathe and wire saw. The standard specimen should have a **length-to-diameter ratio (L/D) of 2.0 to 2.5** and a minimum diameter of **38 mm** (or 10 times the largest particle size). 2. **Measurement of Initial Dimensions:** The initial length (L₀) and diameter (D₀) of the specimen are measured using a vernier calliper. The initial cross-sectional area $A_0 = \dfrac{\pi D_0^2}{4}$ is calculated. 3. **Placement in Apparatus:** The specimen is placed centrally on the bottom platen of the loading apparatus. A spherically seated top platen is placed on the specimen to ensure uniform load distribution. 4. **Instrumentation:** The proving ring (or load cell) and the dial gauge for measuring axial deformation are properly aligned and set to zero. 5. **Application of Load:** Axial compressive load is applied at a **constant rate of strain of 0.5% to 2% per minute** (typically 1.2%/min) until the specimen fails or reaches **20% axial strain**, whichever occurs first. 6. **Recording of Data:** Load readings and corresponding deformation readings are taken at regular intervals (typically every 30 seconds, or at every 0.5% strain increment). 7. **After Test:** The failed specimen is removed, its failure pattern is observed, and a representative portion is taken for **moisture content determination** by oven-drying at 105°C to 110°C for 24 hours. **Standard Specifications (2 Marks):** - **IS Code:** IS 2720 (Part 10): 1991 - **Strain Rate:** 0.5% to 2% per minute - **L/D Ratio:** 2.0 to 2.5 - **Minimum Diameter:** 38 mm or 10 times the largest particle size --- **Model Solution for (b):** **Step 1: Calculate Initial Area** $$A_0 = \frac{\pi D_0^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 38^2}{4} = 1133.54 \text{ mm}^2$$ **Step 2: Calculate Strain and Corrected Area for Each Reading** For each reading: $$\varepsilon = \frac{\text{Strain \%}}{100}, \quad A_c = \frac{A_0}{1 - \varepsilon}, \quad \sigma = \frac{P}{A_c}$$ | Strain (%) | $\varepsilon$ | $A_c$ (mm²) | Load (N) | Stress $\sigma$ (kPa) | |:----------:|:-------------:|:-----------:|:--------:|:---------------------:| | 0 | 0.00 | 1133.54 | 0 | 0.00 | | 2 | 0.02 | 1156.67 | 65 | 56.20 | | 4 | 0.04 | 1180.77 | 115 | 97.40 | | 6 | 0.06 | 1205.89 | 155 | 128.55 | | 8 | 0.08 | 1232.11 | 185 | 150.15 | | 10 | 0.10 | 1259.49 | 200 | 158.80 | | 12 | 0.12 | 1288.11 | 195 | 151.38 | | 14 | 0.14 | 1318.07 | 180 | 136.56 | | 16 | 0.16 | 1349.45 | 160 | 118.57 | **Step 3: Identify the Peak Stress** From the stress-strain curve, the **peak stress** is observed at **10% strain** with: $$q_u = 158.80 \text{ kPa}$$ **Step 4: Calculate Undrained Shear Strength** $$c_u = \frac{q_u}{2} = \frac{158.80}{2} = 79.40 \text{ kPa}$$ **Final Answer:** $q_u = 158.80$ kPa, $c_u = 79.40$ kPa > [!NOTE] > **Observation from the curve:** The stress increases up to 10% strain and then decreases — this is the **strain-softening behaviour** typical of **medium to stiff sensitive clays**. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** (a) *What is the sensitivity of a clay soil? Classify clays based on sensitivity (Skempton's classification). A clay has an undisturbed unconfined compressive strength of 180 kPa and a remoulded strength of 45 kPa. Comment on its sensitivity.* **(7 Marks)** (b) *Explain the advantages and limitations of the Unconfined Compression Test.* **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution for (a):** **Definition of Sensitivity (2 Marks):** Sensitivity ($S_t$) of a clay is defined as the **ratio of the undisturbed (or natural) shear strength to the remoulded shear strength**, with the moisture content held constant. It is a measure of the **loss of strength** of a clay upon remoulding at the same water content. $$S_t = \frac{q_u \, (\text{undisturbed})}{q_r \, (\text{remoulded})} = \frac{c_u \, (\text{undisturbed})}{c_u \, (\text{remoulded})}$$ **Skempton's Classification (3 Marks):** | Sensitivity (Sₜ) | Classification | |:----------------:|:--------------:| | ≈ 1 | Insensitive (or Normal) | | 1 – 2 | Low sensitivity | | 2 – 4 | Medium sensitivity | | 4 – 8 | High sensitivity | | 8 – 16 | Extra sensitive | | > 16 | Quick clays (e.g., marine clays of Norway, Canada) | **Calculation (2 Marks):** $$S_t = \frac{180}{45} = 4.0$$ > [!NOTE] > **Conclusion:** Since $S_t = 4.0$, the clay falls in the range **4 to 8** and is classified as a **"Highly Sensitive"** clay. Such clays should be handled with care during construction, as remoulding (e.g., by pile driving, excavation) can cause a **75% loss of strength**. **Model Solution for (b):** **Advantages (4 Marks):** 1. **Simple and Quick:** The test is straightforward, requires minimal time, and no complicated setup as compared to triaxial tests. 2. **Economical:** No need for a triaxial cell, confining pressure system, pore water pressure measurement, or membrane — making it inexpensive. 3. **Useful for Cohesive Soils:** Provides a quick and reasonably accurate estimate of cᵤ for saturated clays for routine foundation design. 4. **No Need for Pore Pressure Measurement:** Since the test is rapid and undrained, the results are interpreted using total stress analysis without measuring pore pressure. 5. **Good for Field Quality Control:** Easily adaptable for site use (e.g., with a portable vane shear or pocket penetrometer for verification). **Limitations (3 Marks):** 1. **Restricted to Cohesive Soils Only:** Cannot be performed on **cohesionless (sandy or gravelly) soils** or stiff fissured clays, as they crumble without confinement. 2. **No Confinement:** The actual field condition is never truly unconfined. Soils in the field exist under some lateral stress (K₀ condition), which the test fails to simulate. 3. **No Pore Pressure Measurement:** Cannot compute effective stress parameters (c', φ') directly. Only gives cᵤ (undrained strength). 4. **Assumes φᵤ = 0:** The test assumes zero friction angle for saturated clays under undrained loading, which may not be strictly valid for all soil types (e.g., partially saturated clays or stiff fissured clays may exhibit some frictional resistance). 5. **Sample Disturbance:** Results are highly sensitive to sample disturbance during trimming and handling. 6. **Strain Rate Effects:** The undrained strength depends on the strain rate; non-standard strain rates can lead to erroneous cᵤ values. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting Area Correction (–2 marks):** Using the original area $A_0$ instead of the corrected area $A_c$ for all calculations after the first reading. This is the **single most common mistake** in KTU board exams. > 2. **Using φᵤ = 0 for ALL soils (–1 mark):** The assumption φᵤ = 0 is valid only for **saturated cohesive soils under undrained loading**. For dry or partially saturated clays, this is a gross simplification. > 3. **Confusing qu with cᵤ (–2 marks):** Some students directly report cᵤ = qu without halving. Remember: $c_u = \dfrac{q_u}{2}$, NOT $c_u = q_u$. > 4. **Not reporting qu at 20% strain for ductile soils (–1 mark):** For soft clays that do not show a peak, qu must be reported at **20% axial strain** (as per IS code). > 5. **Unit Errors (–1 mark):** Forgetting to convert N/mm² to kN/m² (1 N/mm² = 1000 kN/m² = 1 MPa). This is heavily penalized by KTU examiners. > 6. **Skipping the stress-strain plot (–1 mark):** KTU examiners specifically check for the **plot**, not just the table. Always plot the curve and clearly mark the peak. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Unconfined Compression Test** - **IS Code:** IS 2720 (Part 10): 1991 - **Applicable to:** Saturated cohesive soils (clays and silts) only - **Special Case of:** Triaxial Test with **σ₃ = 0** - **Strain Rate:** 0.5% – 2% per minute (typically 1.2%/min) - **L/D Ratio:** 2.0 – 2.5 (minimum diameter ≥ 10× largest particle size) - **Key Assumption:** φᵤ = 0 for saturated clays under undrained loading - **Core Formula 1:** $A_c = \dfrac{A_0}{1 - \varepsilon}$ (volume constancy) - **Core Formula 2:** $\sigma = \dfrac{P}{A_c}$ (axial stress) - **Core Formula 3:** $c_u = \dfrac{q_u}{2}$ (undrained shear strength) - **Core Formula 4:** $S_t = \dfrac{q_u}{q_r}$ (sensitivity) - **Failure Criterion:** Maximum stress OR stress at 20% strain (whichever is earlier) - **Volume Assumption:** Soil is **incompressible** (volume remains constant) - **Type of Test:** Strain-controlled (not stress-controlled) - **Advantages:** Quick, simple, economical, no need for cell pressure or pore pressure measurement - **Limitations:** Only for cohesive soils, no confinement, no effective stress parameters - **Skempton's Sensitivity Classes:** 1, 1–2, 2–4, 4–8, 8–16, > 16 (Insensitive → Quick) - **Typical cᵤ Range:** Soft clay ~ 12.5 kPa; Stiff clay ~ 50–100 kPa; Hard clay > 200 kPa - **Don't forget:** Peak stress is **qu**; Shear strength is **cu = qu/2**; Always use the **corrected area** $A_c$ in calculations. - **Exam Tip:** Draw the **stress-strain curve** carefully, mark the peak, and label both axes with proper units (kPa on Y-axis, % on X-axis).

topics.title: Consolidation Test
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCEL507 - GEOTECHNICAL ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Consolidation Test

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Consolidation Test (Oedometer Test)** is a standardized laboratory test (IS 2720 Part 15) performed on an undisturbed saturated cohesive soil specimen to determine the **rate** and **magnitude** of one-dimensional (1-D) settlement under applied axial load, while lateral deformation is prevented by a rigid confining ring.

The test yields the following key engineering parameters:

| Parameter | Symbol | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Coefficient of Consolidation | $C_v$ | Rate at which pore water pressure dissipates |
| Compression Index | $C_c$ | Slope of virgin (normally consolidated) portion of $e \text{ vs } \log \sigma'$ curve |
| Recompression Index | $C_r$ | Slope of recompression (unloading–reloading) branch |
| Swelling Index | $C_s$ | Slope of unloading (swelling) branch |
| Preconsolidation Pressure | $P_c$ | Maximum past effective stress experienced by the soil |
| Coefficient of Volume Compressibility | $m_v$ | Volumetric strain per unit increase in effective stress |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Sponge in a Tube" 🧽

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuition:** Imagine a water-soaked sponge tightly packed inside a rigid, airtight vertical tube with drainage allowed only at the top. If you place a weight on the sponge, it cannot expand sideways (the rigid tube), so the only way to compress is for water to slowly squeeze out through the top. The sponge's volume decreases as water escapes — **this is exactly what happens in a clay layer sandwiched between two permeable sand layers under a building load**.

- The **sponge** = clay (compressible soil skeleton)
- The **water** = pore water under excess pressure
- The **rigid tube** = oedometer ring (prevents lateral strain)
- The **drainage at top** = porous stones
- The **applied weight** = structural load
- **Time taken for water to escape** = primary consolidation settlement

The time it takes depends on how "tight" the sponge is — a dense sponge (low permeability clay) takes much longer than a loose one (silt or sand).

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Test Conditions (IS 2720 Part 15):**
> - Standard Load Increment Ratio (LIR) = **1.0** (i.e., each load is double the previous)
> - Load sequence: **10, 20, 40, 80, 160, 320, 640 kPa** (typical) → Unload → Reload
> - Each load increment held for **24 hours** (or until primary consolidation is complete)
> - Time intervals for dial gauge readings: **0, 0.25, 1, 2.25, 4, 9, 16, 25, 36, 49, 64, 81, 100, 200, 400, 1440 min**
> - Specimen diameter: **60 mm** (standard), height: **20 mm**
> - Acceleration due to gravity: $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

**Typical $C_v$ Values for Field Reference:**

| Soil Type | $C_v$ (m²/s) | Drainage Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| Dense sand | $10^{-3} \text{ to } 10^{-1}$ | Practically instant |
| Soft clay | $10^{-8} \text{ to } 10^{-7}$ | Very slow (years) |
| Stiff clay | $10^{-7} \text{ to } 10^{-6}$ | Slow (months) |
| Peat | $10^{-8} \text{ to } 10^{-7}$ | Extremely slow |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Terzaghi's Theory of 1-D Consolidation — The Underlying Physics

The test is governed by **Terzaghi's One-Dimensional Consolidation Theory** (1925), built on the following assumptions:

1. The soil is **homogeneous**, **saturated**, and **isotropic**.
2. The soil grains and pore water are **incompressible**.
3. **Darcy's Law** governs pore water flow.
4. **Lateral strain = 0** (oedometer condition → $K_0$ loading).
5. The coefficient of permeability $k$ and coefficient of volume compressibility $m_v$ remain **constant** during the load increment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Logic in 4 Steps — "Why and How":**
>
> **Step 1 — Apply Load** $\sigma$: When a load is applied instantaneously, it is initially carried entirely by the **pore water** as excess pore water pressure $\Delta u$.
>
> **Why?** Water is incompressible, but the soil skeleton wants to compress; the water must first escape before the soil skeleton can take up the load.
>
> **Step 2 — Pore Pressure Dissipation**: As water flows out (through the nearest drainage boundary), $\Delta u$ decreases with time, and the effective stress $\sigma' = \sigma - u$ increases.
>
> **How?** Governed by the diffusion equation: $\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial t} = C_v \cdot \dfrac{\partial^2 u}{\partial z^2}$
>
> **Step 3 — Volume Change**: The soil skeleton compresses as $\sigma'$ increases, expelling water equal in volume to the change in void space.
>
> **Step 4 — End of Primary Consolidation (EOP)**: When $\Delta u \to 0$, all applied load is carried by the soil skeleton. Secondary compression (creep) may then follow.

## 2.2 The Three Stages of Consolidation

| Stage | Description | Governing Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| **Initial (Instantaneous)** | Very small, immediate compression due to entrapped air & soil structure adjustment | Elastic/structural distortion |
| **Primary Consolidation** | Major volume change due to expulsion of pore water; $u_e$ dissipates to zero | Hydrodynamic (Darcy flow) |
| **Secondary Consolidation** | Slow volume change at constant effective stress; rearrangement of soil particles | Viscous creep of soil skeleton |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Table 2.3 — Essential Consolidation Equations**

| S.No. | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Coefficient of Consolidation | $C_v = \dfrac{T_v \cdot H_d^2}{t}$ | m²/s | $H_d$ = drainage path length |
| 2 | Drainage Path (double) | $H_d = \dfrac{H}{2}$ | m | Top + bottom drainage |
| 3 | Drainage Path (single) | $H_d = H$ | m | Only one drainage face |
| 4 | Time Factor at $U = 50\%$ | $T_v = 0.197$ | — | For $U \le 60\%$; valid for any drainage |
| 5 | Time Factor at $U = 90\%$ | $T_v = 0.848$ | — | For $U > 60\%$; valid for any drainage |
| 6 | Coefficient of Volume Compressibility | $m_v = \dfrac{\Delta e}{\Delta \sigma' (1+e_0)}$ | m²/kN | Ratio of volumetric strain to stress |
| 7 | Coefficient of Compressibility | $a_v = \dfrac{\Delta e}{\Delta \sigma'}$ | m²/kN | Slope of $e \text{ vs } \sigma'$ curve |
| 8 | Compression Index | $C_c = \dfrac{\Delta e}{\Delta \log_{10} \sigma'}$ | — | Slope of virgin (NC) part of $e\text{-}\log\sigma'$ |
| 9 | Recompression Index | $C_r = \dfrac{\Delta e}{\Delta \log_{10} \sigma'}$ | — | Slope of recompression branch |
| 10 | Swelling Index | $C_s \approx C_r$ | — | Slope of unloading (swelling) branch |
| 11 | Degree of Consolidation (Taylor) | $U = \dfrac{R_0 - R_t}{R_0 - R_{100}}$ | — | Using $\sqrt{t}$ plot dial readings |
| 12 | Void Ratio at any load | $e = e_0 - \dfrac{\Delta H (1+e_0)}{H_0}$ | — | Where $\Delta H$ is the change in specimen height |
| 13 | Settlement (general) | $S = m_v \cdot \Delta \sigma' \cdot H$ | m | For a soil layer of thickness $H$ |
| 14 | Settlement (using $C_c$) | $S = \dfrac{C_c \cdot H}{1+e_0} \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{\sigma_0' + \Delta \sigma'}{\sigma_0'}\right)$ | m | For NC clay ($C_c$ method) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Distinction:** Always check the **drainage condition** in the field. In an oedometer, both top and bottom are drained, so $H_d = H/2$. In a field clay layer between two sand layers, **double drainage** applies. If the clay rests on impervious rock, use **single drainage** ($H_d = H$).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Design of foundations on clay** to estimate long-term settlement of buildings, embankments, and tanks.
- **Predicting post-construction settlement** of highway and railway subgrades.
- **Stability analysis of earth dams** (consolidation of clay cores).
- **Land reclamation projects** (pre-consolidation using surcharge and vertical drains).
- **Lime/cement stabilization** evaluation by comparing pre- and post-treatment $C_c$ values.
- **Numerical modeling** in FEM software (PLAXIS, GeoStudio) requires $C_v$, $C_c$, and OCR as input.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of Coefficient of Volume Compressibility $m_v$

**Given:** A soil specimen of initial height $H_0$ and initial void ratio $e_0$ is subjected to an effective stress increment $\Delta \sigma'$. The corresponding change in height is $\Delta H$.

**Step 1** — Express volume of voids change in terms of height change (since lateral strain is zero, volume change is 1-D):

$$\Delta V_v = A \cdot \Delta H$$

**Step 2** — The volumetric strain (change in total volume per unit initial volume) equals:

$$\epsilon_v = \dfrac{\Delta H}{H_0}$$

**Step 3** — The coefficient of volume compressibility is defined as volumetric strain per unit effective stress:

$$m_v = \dfrac{\epsilon_v}{\Delta \sigma'} = \dfrac{\Delta H / H_0}{\Delta \sigma'}$$

**Step 4** — To express in terms of void ratio: For a saturated soil, the change in void ratio $\Delta e$ relates to the change in height as (since volume of solids $V_s$ is constant):

$$\dfrac{\Delta H}{H_0} = \dfrac{\Delta e}{1 + e_0}$$

**Substituting** this relation into Step 3:

$$m_v = \dfrac{1}{1 + e_0} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta e}{\Delta \sigma'}$$

**Final Compact Form:**

$$m_v = \dfrac{\Delta e}{\Delta \sigma' \cdot (1 + e_0)}$$

> **Logic:** The factor $1/(1+e_0)$ converts the void ratio change (per unit volume of solids) into a volumetric strain (per unit total volume). This is **why** $m_v$ has units of m²/kN, not dimensionless.

## 3.2 Derivation of Settlement using $C_c$ for Normally Consolidated Clay

**Step 1** — Define initial and final effective stresses:
- Initial effective stress: $\sigma_0'$
- Final effective stress after loading: $\sigma_f' = \sigma_0' + \Delta \sigma'$

**Step 2** — From the definition of compression index on the virgin compression line:

$$C_c = -\dfrac{\Delta e}{\Delta \log_{10} \sigma'} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \Delta e = -C_c \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{\sigma_f'}{\sigma_0'}\right)$$

**Step 3** — Settlement $S$ equals the height change $\Delta H$ for a layer of total thickness $H$:

$$S = \Delta H = \dfrac{\Delta e \cdot H}{1 + e_0}$$

**Step 4** — Substitute $\Delta e$ from Step 2 into Step 3:

$$S = \dfrac{H \cdot C_c}{1 + e_0} \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{\sigma_f'}{\sigma_0'}\right)$$

**Final Form:**

$$S = \dfrac{C_c \cdot H}{1 + e_0} \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{\sigma_0' + \Delta \sigma'}{\sigma_0'}\right)$$

## 3.3 Derivation of Time Factor Equation

**Starting Point** — Terzaghi's 1-D consolidation equation:

$$\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial t} = C_v \cdot \dfrac{\partial^2 u}{\partial z^2}$$

**Step 1** — Non-dimensionalize using drainage path $H_d$ and time $t$. Let $Z = z/H_d$ and $T_v = C_v \cdot t / H_d^2$:

$$\dfrac{\partial U}{\partial T_v} = \dfrac{\partial^2 U}{\partial Z^2}$$

**Step 2** — Solve this partial differential equation analytically using a Fourier series for the boundary conditions:
- At $z = 0$ and $z = 2H_d$ (drainage faces), $u = 0$ (double drainage)
- At $t = 0$ (initial), $u = u_i$ (uniform initial excess pore pressure)

The solution is:

$$U = 1 - \sum_{m=0}^{\infty} \dfrac{2}{M^2} \cdot e^{-M^2 T_v}$$

where $M = \dfrac{\pi}{2}(2m+1)$ and $U$ is the average degree of consolidation.

**Step 3** — For practical use, $T_v$ is expressed in terms of $U$ using approximate empirical formulas:

**For $U \le 60\%$** (the most commonly used range in Casagrande's method):

$$T_v = \dfrac{\pi}{4} U^2 = 0.785 U^2$$

**At $U = 50\%$,** $T_{v50} = 0.785 \times 0.25 = 0.196$ → rounded to **$T_{v50} = 0.197$**.

**For $U > 60\%$** (used in Taylor's method):

$$T_v = -0.933 \log_{10}(1 - U) - 0.085$$

**At $U = 90\%$,** $T_{v90} = -0.933 \log_{10}(0.1) - 0.085 = 0.933 - 0.085 = 0.848$.

**Final Working Equations:**

$$C_v = \dfrac{0.197 \cdot H_d^2}{t_{50}} \quad \text{(Casagrande's Log-Time Method)}$$

$$C_v = \dfrac{0.848 \cdot H_d^2}{t_{90}} \quad \text{(Taylor's Square-Root Time Method)}$$

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example — $C_v$ Calculation

> [!NOTE]
> **Example Problem:** A 20 mm thick saturated clay sample is tested in an oedometer with double drainage. From the dial gauge readings of a particular load increment, the time for 50% primary consolidation ($t_{50}$) is found to be **9 minutes** and the time for 90% primary consolidation ($t_{90}$) is found to be **36 minutes**. Calculate the coefficient of consolidation using both methods.

**Solution:**

**Step 1** — Identify drainage condition. Since both top and bottom porous stones are present:

$$H_d = \dfrac{H}{2} = \dfrac{20 \text{ mm}}{2} = 10 \text{ mm} = 0.01 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2** — Using **Casagrande's Log-Time Method** with $t_{50} = 9 \text{ min} = 540 \text{ s}$:

$$C_v = \dfrac{0.197 \cdot H_d^2}{t_{50}} = \dfrac{0.197 \times (0.01)^2}{540}$$

$$C_v = \dfrac{0.197 \times 10^{-4}}{540} = \dfrac{1.97 \times 10^{-5}}{540}$$

$$C_v = 3.65 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$$

**Step 3** — Using **Taylor's Square-Root Time Method** with $t_{90} = 36 \text{ min} = 2160 \text{ s}$:

$$C_v = \dfrac{0.848 \cdot H_d^2}{t_{90}} = \dfrac{0.848 \times (0.01)^2}{2160}$$

$$C_v = \dfrac{0.848 \times 10^{-4}}{2160} = \dfrac{8.48 \times 10^{-5}}{2160}$$

$$C_v = 3.93 \times 10^{-8} \{m^2/s}$$

**Step 4** — Interpretation: Both methods give similar orders of magnitude ($10^{-8} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$), confirming the soil behaves as a **soft to medium clay** with very slow consolidation. ✓

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Methods Summary

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table 3.5 — Casagrande vs. Taylor's Method Comparison**

| Aspect | Casagrande's Log-Time Method | Taylor's Square-Root Time Method |
|---|---|---|
| Plot type | Dial Reading vs $\log_{10} t$ | Dial Reading vs $\sqrt{t}$ |
| Reference line | Initial horizontal (theoretical) tangent | Initial straight portion of curve |
| Reference point | $t_{50}$ (at 50% primary consolidation) | $t_{90}$ (at 90% primary consolidation) |
| Time Factor used | $T_{v50} = 0.197$ | $T_{v90} = 0.848$ |
| Precision required | High — relies on accurate point of max curvature | High — relies on extending initial tangent to x-axis |
| Best for | All stages of consolidation; standard academic method | Quick estimation; better for the early part of curve |
| Sourced from | A. Casagrande (1938) | D.W. Taylor (1948) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Consolidometer Apparatus

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Loading Frame Top Plate] --> B[Loading Cap with Spherical Seat]
    B --> C[Upper Porous Stone]
    C --> D[Soil Specimen in Metal Ring]
    D --> E[Lower Porous Stone]
    E --> F[Base Plate with Water Channel]
    F --> G[Water Reservoir]
    H[Dial Gauge Stand] --> I[Vertical Dial Gauge LVDT]
    I --> A
    J[Reaction Frame] --> A
    J --> K[Bottom Plate Anchored to Bench]
    style A fill:#E0F2F1,stroke:#00695C
    style D fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100
    style G fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#0D47A1
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Load is applied from the top frame $\to$ transmitted through the spherical seat $\to$ cap $\to$ upper porous stone $\to$ soil sample $\to$ lower porous stone $\to$ base plate. Pore water escapes **downward** into the reservoir. The dial gauge (LVDT) measures vertical settlement continuously.

## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — Test Procedure & Calculation Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Receive Undisturbed Sample] --> A1[Trim Sample to Fit Oedometer Ring]
    A1 --> A2[Weigh Sample; Record Initial Moisture Content]
    A2 --> A3[Assemble in Consolidometer; Saturate]
    A3 --> A4[Apply Seating Load and Set Dial to Zero]
    A4 --> A5[Apply First Load e.g. 10 kPa]
    A5 --> A6[Record Dial Readings at Specified Time Intervals]
    A6 --> A7{24 Hours Elapsed?}
    A7 -->|No| A6
    A7 -->|Yes| A8[Apply Next Load Double Previous]
    A8 --> A9{Load Sequence Complete?}
    A9 -->|No| A5
    A9 -->|Yes| A10[Unload in Stages for Rebound]
    A10 --> A11[Plot Dial R vs log t for Each Increment]
    A10 --> A12[Plot Dial R vs sqrt t for Each Increment]
    A11 --> A13[Find t50 using Casagrande Construction]
    A12 --> A14[Find t90 using Taylor Construction]
    A13 --> A15[Compute Cv using Tv50 equals 0.197]
    A14 --> A16[Compute Cv using Tv90 equals 0.848]
    A15 --> A17[Plot e vs log sigma for the Test]
    A16 --> A17
    A17 --> A18[Find Preconsolidation Pressure Pc]
    A17 --> A19[Compute Compression Index Cc]
    A17 --> A20[Compute Recompression Index Cr]
    A18 --> A21[Report Results with Graphical Plots]
    A19 --> A21
    A20 --> A21
    style A17 fill:#FFF59D,stroke:#F57F17
    style A21 fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Block Architecture — Consolidation Mechanics

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[Applied Stress sigma] --> L2[Excess Pore Pressure ue]
    L2 --> L3{Pore Water Escapes via Drainage Faces}
    L3 -->|Yes| L4[Effective Stress sigma prime Increases]
    L3 -->|No| L5[Secondary Compression Creep]
    L4 --> L6[Soil Skeleton Compresses]
    L6 --> L7[Volume Change equals Expelled Water Volume]
    L7 --> L8[Settlement Delta H Recorded by Dial Gauge]
    L5 --> L8
    style L2 fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C
    style L4 fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20
    style L8 fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#0D47A1
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The applied stress is split into two parts — **excess pore water pressure** (which dissipates with time) and **effective stress** (which causes the soil skeleton to compress and produce settlement). Secondary compression occurs *after* primary consolidation is essentially complete.
