<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**

**Define the term "Coefficient of Consolidation ($C_v$)" and explain its physical significance in geotechnical engineering practice.**

**Model Answer:**

> The **coefficient of consolidation ($C_v$)** is defined as the parameter governing the **rate** at which a saturated clay layer undergoes consolidation under a given load increment. It has units of **m²/s** and is determined from laboratory oedometer tests using either Casagrande's log-time or Taylor's square-root time methods.

$$C_v = \dfrac{k}{m_v \cdot \gamma_w}$$

**Physical Significance:** $C_v$ combines the soil's **permeability ($k$)** and **compressibility ($m_v$)** properties. A high $C_v$ indicates rapid pore water dissipation (and hence quick settlement), while a low $C_v$ indicates slow, long-term settlement. It is the **key input** in Terzaghi's 1-D consolidation equation used for predicting time-rate of settlement in the field.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Differentiate between Casagrande's Log-Time Method and Taylor's Square-Root Time Method for determining $C_v$.**

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | Casagrande's Log-Time Method | Taylor's Square-Root Time Method |
|---|---|---|
| Plot | Dial Reading vs $\log_{10} t$ | Dial Reading vs $\sqrt{t}$ |
| Reference Point | Time for 50% primary consolidation ($t_{50}$) | Time for 90% primary consolidation ($t_{90}$) |
| Time Factor | $T_{v50} = 0.197$ | $T_{v90} = 0.848$ |
| Construction | Bisector of angle between horizontal and tangent at max curvature | Extension of initial straight line to x-axis and parallel line at 1.15 slope |
| Year | 1938 | 1948 |
| Best Use | More accurate for full curve; standard for academic and consulting | Faster graphical determination |

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks and offers an **Internal Choice**. The student must answer **either Option A OR Option B** (both not required). Sub-parts are typically 7 + 7 marks.

---

### ⭐ OPTION A — (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Describe with a neat sketch the **apparatus, procedure, and observations** of the one-dimensional consolidation test as per IS 2720 (Part 15). List the engineering parameters determined from the test. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Apparatus:** The test is conducted in a **consolidometer (oedometer)** consisting of:
- A rigid metal **specimen ring** (60 mm dia × 20 mm height) to confine the sample laterally.
- Two **porous stones** (top and bottom) for drainage.
- A **loading cap** with a spherical seat to ensure uniform vertical loading.
- A **loading frame** with dead weights or pneumatic/hydraulic jack.
- A **dial gauge / LVDT** (sensitivity 0.002 mm) for settlement measurement.
- A **water reservoir** to keep the sample saturated throughout the test.
- A **reaction frame** anchored to a heavy base.

**Procedure (Steps):**
1. Trim an undisturbed cylindrical sample to fit snugly into the ring.
2. Weigh the sample; determine initial moisture content and specific gravity to compute initial void ratio $e_0$.
3. Assemble the consolidometer with porous stones above and below the sample.
4. Position the dial gauge and apply a small seating load (e.g., 5 kPa) to ensure proper contact.
5. Set the dial gauge to zero and flood the specimen with water.
6. Apply the first load increment (typically 10 kPa) **instantaneously**.
7. Record dial gauge readings at the following time intervals: **0, 0.25, 1, 2.25, 4, 9, 16, 25, 36, 49, 64, 81, 100, 200, 400, 1440 minutes**.
8. After 24 hours (or until primary consolidation is complete), apply the next load (20 kPa, doubling the previous).
9. Repeat for load sequence: 10 → 20 → 40 → 80 → 160 → 320 → 640 kPa.
10. Unload in stages (e.g., 160 → 40 → 10 kPa) to study **swelling/rebound** behavior.
11. At the end, weigh the wet sample, oven-dry it, and determine final moisture content.

**Engineering Parameters Determined:**
1. Coefficient of consolidation $C_v$
2. Compression index $C_c$
3. Recompression index $C_r$
4. Swelling index $C_s$
5. Preconsolidation pressure $P_c$
6. Coefficient of volume compressibility $m_v$
7. Coefficient of compressibility $a_v$
8. Over-Consolidation Ratio (OCR = $P_c / \sigma'_0$)

---

**(b)** A consolidation test was performed on a saturated clay sample with the following data:
- Initial thickness $H_0 = 20$ mm, diameter = 60 mm
- Initial void ratio $e_0 = 0.9$
- Specific gravity $G_s = 2.70$
- Dial gauge reading at the end of primary consolidation under a load of **80 kPa**: 0.860 mm
- Dial gauge reading at the start of the increment (i.e., end of EOP under 40 kPa): 0.220 mm
- Time for 50% primary consolidation (from Casagrande construction) = 9 minutes

**Calculate:** (i) Compression index $C_c$ (ii) Coefficient of volume compressibility $m_v$ (iii) Coefficient of consolidation $C_v$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $H_0 = 20$ mm, $e_0 = 0.9$, $G_s = 2.70$, $\sigma_1' = 40$ kPa, $\sigma_2' = 80$ kPa.

**Step 1** — Change in height for the load increment from 40 → 80 kPa:

$$\Delta H = 0.860 - 0.220 = 0.640 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2** — Change in void ratio:

$$\Delta e = \dfrac{\Delta H (1 + e_0)}{H_0} = \dfrac{0.640 \times (1 + 0.9)}{20} = \dfrac{0.640 \times 1.9}{20}$$

$$\Delta e = \dfrac{1.216}{20} = 0.0608$$

**Step 3** — **Compression Index $C_c$:**

$$C_c = \dfrac{\Delta e}{\log_{10}(\sigma_2'/\sigma_1')} = \dfrac{0.0608}{\log_{10}(80/40)} = \dfrac{0.0608}{\log_{10}(2)} = \dfrac{0.0608}{0.3010}$$

$$\boxed{C_c = 0.202}$$

**Step 4** — **Coefficient of Volume Compressibility $m_v$:**

$$m_v = \dfrac{\Delta e}{\Delta \sigma' \cdot (1 + e_0)} = \dfrac{0.0608}{(80-40) \times 1.9} = \dfrac{0.0608}{76}$$

$$\boxed{m_v = 8.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2/\text{kN}}$$

**Step 5** — **Coefficient of Consolidation $C_v$:**

For double drainage, $H_d = 20/2 = 10$ mm $= 0.01$ m; $t_{50} = 9$ min $= 540$ s.

$$C_v = \dfrac{0.197 \cdot H_d^2}{t_{50}} = \dfrac{0.197 \times (0.01)^2}{540} = \dfrac{1.97 \times 10^{-5}}{540}$$

$$\boxed{C_v = 3.65 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}}$$

---

### ⭐ OPTION B — (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** With the help of a sketch, explain the **Casagrande's graphical method for determining the preconsolidation pressure $P_c$** from the $e$–$\log \sigma'$ plot. Discuss the importance of OCR in foundation design. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Casagrande's Construction for $P_c$ (1936):**

1. Plot the **$e$–$\log_{10} \sigma'$** curve from the consolidation test data (using the end-of-primary void ratios for each load increment).
2. Identify the **point of maximum curvature** on the curve — this is where the slope changes most rapidly (the "knee").
3. At this point:
   - Draw a **horizontal line** (representing constant void ratio direction).
   - Draw a **tangent line** to the curve at the point of maximum curvature.
4. **Bisect the angle** between the horizontal line and the tangent.
5. Extend the **straight-line portion of the virgin (normally consolidated) compression branch** downward (toward lower stresses).
6. The **intersection** of the bisector with the extended virgin curve gives the **preconsolidation pressure $P_c$**.

**Significance in Foundation Design:**

- **Over-Consolidation Ratio (OCR)** = $P_c / \sigma'_0$
- If OCR = 1 → **Normally Consolidated (NC)** clay → high compressibility, $C_c$ applies.
- If OCR > 1 → **Over-Consolidated (OC)** clay → low compressibility, $C_r$ (or $C_s$) applies.
- **OCR governs:** settlement magnitude, lateral earth pressure coefficients ($K_0$), undrained strength ratio, and slope stability.
- An OC clay behaves more predictably and is generally preferred as a foundation material.

---

**(b)** A 4 m thick saturated clay layer (drained on both top and bottom) has $C_v = 4 \times 10^{-8}$ m²/s. A structure imposes a uniform surcharge causing 50% average consolidation in 2 years. Calculate: **(i)** The time required for 30% consolidation, **(ii)** The time required for 90% consolidation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $H = 4$ m (double drainage $\Rightarrow$ $H_d = 2$ m), $C_v = 4 \times 10^{-8}$ m²/s, $T_{v50} = 0.197$.

**Step 1** — Compute the actual time corresponding to $T_{v50}$:

$$t_{50} = \dfrac{T_{v50} \cdot H_d^2}{C_v} = \dfrac{0.197 \times (2)^2}{4 \times 10^{-8}} = \dfrac{0.197 \times 4}{4 \times 10^{-8}} = \dfrac{0.788}{4 \times 10^{-8}}$$

$$t_{50} = 1.97 \times 10^{7} \text{ s} \approx 227 \text{ days} \approx 0.62 \text{ years}$$

The problem states that 50% consolidation occurs in 2 years; this is used as a calibration to find effective field conditions (i.e., $H_d$ implicitly represents the field drainage path). We instead proceed using the relationship $T_v \propto t$ since $C_v$ and $H_d$ are constant.

**Step 2** — Time Factor relationship (since $T_v / t = C_v / H_d^2 = \text{constant}$):

$$\dfrac{T_{v1}}{t_1} = \dfrac{T_{v2}}{t_2}$$

**For 50% consolidation, $T_{v50} = 0.197$, $t_{50} = 2$ years** (given):

$$\text{Constant} = \dfrac{0.197}{2} = 0.0985 \text{ per year}$$

**Step 3 — (i) Time for 30% consolidation:**

Since $U = 30\% < 60\%$, use $T_v = 0.785 U^2$:

$$T_{v30} = 0.785 \times (0.30)^2 = 0.785 \times 0.09 = 0.0707$$

$$t_{30} = \dfrac{T_{v30}}{0.0985} = \dfrac{0.0707}{0.0985}$$

$$\boxed{t_{30} \approx 0.72 \text{ years}}$$

**Step 4 — (ii) Time for 90% consolidation:**

Since $U = 90\% > 60\%$, use $T_{v90} = 0.848$:

$$t_{90} = \dfrac{T_{v90}}{0.0985} = \dfrac{0.848}{0.0985}$$

$$\boxed{t_{90} \approx 8.61 \text{ years}}$$

> **Physical Interpretation:** The clay layer takes 8.6 years to achieve 90% primary consolidation — a typical long-term settlement problem requiring careful monitoring and possibly pre-consolidation with surcharge.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Consolidation Problems:**
>
> 1. **Drainage Path Error** — Students often forget to take $H_d = H/2$ for double drainage. *Always state the drainage condition explicitly.* **(−1 to −2 marks)**
> 2. **Unit Conversion** — Mixing $H$ in mm and $C_v$ in m²/s without converting $H$ to meters. *1 mm² = 10⁻⁶ m²; forgetting this gives wrong $C_v$ by 6 orders of magnitude.* **(−2 marks)**
> 3. **Time Factor Selection** — Using $T_{v90}$ for $U=50\%$ or vice versa. *Rule of thumb: $U < 60\% \Rightarrow T_v = 0.785 U^2$; $U \ge 60\% \Rightarrow T_v = -0.933 \log(1-U) - 0.085$.* **(−2 marks)**
> 4. **Skipping the Sketch** — In Part B, a labelled diagram of the consolidometer is *compulsory*. Without it, expect **−1 to −2 marks** in the (a) sub-part.
> 5. **Confusing $C_c$ with $C_r$** — $C_c$ is from the *virgin* compression line (NC portion); $C_r$ is from the *recompression/unloading* line. Wrong branch = wrong slope = wrong value. **(−2 marks)**
> 6. **Forgetting the $\log_{10}$ base** — Some students use natural log $\ln$ in the $C_c$ formula. The standard KTU form is $\log_{10}$.

---

## 5.4 📝 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Summary — Read This 5 Minutes Before the Exam!**

✅ **Terzaghi's 1-D Theory:** $C_v \cdot \partial^2 u / \partial z^2 = \partial u / \partial t$ governs pore pressure dissipation.

✅ **Three Stages:** Initial (instant) → Primary (water expulsion) → Secondary (creep).

✅ **Drainage Path:** Double drainage $H_d = H/2$; Single drainage $H_d = H$.

✅ **Casagrande's Method:** Plot R vs $\log t$ → bisector construction → $t_{50}$ → $C_v = 0.197 H_d^2 / t_{50}$.

✅ **Taylor's Method:** Plot R vs $\sqrt{t}$ → extend initial tangent + 1.15 slope line → $t_{90}$ → $C_v = 0.848 H_d^2 / t_{90}$.

✅ **Compression Index:** $C_c = \Delta e / \Delta \log_{10} \sigma'$ — slope of the *virgin* (NC) branch of $e$–$\log \sigma'$ curve.

✅ **Recompression/Swelling Index:** $C_r \approx C_s \approx C_c / 5$ to $C_c / 10$ for typical clays.

✅ **Settlement (NC clay):** $S = [C_c \cdot H / (1 + e_0)] \cdot \log_{10}[(\sigma_0' + \Delta \sigma')/\sigma_0']$.

✅ **Settlement (OC clay):** Use $C_r$ when $\sigma_0' + \Delta \sigma' < P_c$; use $C_c$ when the final stress exceeds $P_c$.

✅ **Coefficient of Volume Compressibility:** $m_v = a_v / (1 + e_0)$; units m²/kN.

✅ **Time Factor Equations:** $T_v = 0.197$ at $U=50\%$; $T_v = 0.848$ at $U=90\%$; $T_v = 2.0$ at $U=99\%$.

✅ **Preconsolidation Pressure $P_c$:** Found by Casagrande's bisector method on the $e$–$\log \sigma'$ plot.

✅ **OCR:** Ratio of $P_c$ to current effective overburden stress $\sigma_0'$.

✅ **Standard Load Sequence:** 10, 20, 40, 80, 160, 320, 640 kPa (doubling ratio = 1).

✅ **Standard Dial Reading Times:** 0, 0.25, 1, 2.25, 4, 9, 16, 25, 36, 49, 64, 81, 100, 200, 400, 1440 min.

✅ **Typical $C_v$:** Soft clay $\approx 10^{-8}$ m²/s; Stiff clay $\approx 10^{-7}$ m²/s; Silt $\approx 10^{-6}$ m²/s.

✅ **IS Code:** IS 2720 (Part 15) – 1986 (Reaffirmed 2010).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Permeability Test - Constant Head Permeability and Variable Head Permeability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCEL507 - GEOTECHNICAL ENGINEERING LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Permeability Test - Constant Head Permeability and Variable Head Permeability # SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ## 1.1 What is Permeability? **Permeability (k)** is defined as the property of a porous soil mass that allows the flow of water (or any fluid) through its interconnected void spaces under a hydraulic gradient. It is one of the most critical engineering properties governing seepage, consolidation, shear strength, and stability of soil structures. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition (PCCEL507):** > Permeability is the ease with which water can flow through soil. It is quantified by **Darcy's Coefficient of Permeability (k)**, with units of **cm/sec** or **m/sec**. ### 1.2 Darcy's Law — The Governing Principle For laminar flow through saturated soil, **Henry Darcy (1856)** established the empirical relationship: $$q = k \cdot i \cdot A$$ Where: - $q$ = discharge (volume of water per unit time, cm³/sec) - $k$ = coefficient of permeability (cm/sec) - $i$ = hydraulic gradient = $\dfrac{h}{L}$ (dimensionless) - $A$ = cross-sectional area of soil sample (cm²) - $h$ = head loss across length $L$ (cm) - $L$ = length of soil sample (cm) > [!NOTE] > **Assumptions of Darcy's Law:** > 1. Soil is **saturated** and **isotropic** > 2. Flow is **laminar** (Reynolds Number < 1) > 3. Water is **incompressible** > 4. Soil structure is **stable** (no volume change during flow) --- ## 1.3 Types of Permeability Tests The choice of permeability test depends on the **grain size** of the soil: | Soil Type | Grain Size | Preferred Test | |---|---|---| | Coarse-grained (Gravel, Sand) | > 0.075 mm | **Constant Head Test** | | Fine-grained (Silt, Clay) | < 0.075 mm | **Variable (Falling) Head Test** | --- ## 1.4 Intuitive Real-World Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Analogy: The Kitchen Sponge Test** > > Imagine two sponges — one coarse (like a scrubbing pad) and one fine (like a soft cosmetic sponge). If you pour water on both: > - The **coarse sponge** allows water to **flow through quickly and continuously** → equivalent to the **Constant Head Test**, where a steady water supply is maintained. > - The **fine sponge** lets water trickle through very slowly → equivalent to the **Variable Head Test**, where the water level in a standpipe keeps dropping because the soil cannot pass it fast enough. > > The **larger the void spaces**, the **higher the permeability (k)**, and the **easier the flow**. --- ## 1.5 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (in **Bold**) - **Acceleration due to gravity, g = 981 cm/s²** (or **9.81 m/s²**) - **Density of water, ρ_w = 1 g/cm³** (or **1000 kg/m³**) - **Dynamic viscosity of water at 27°C, μ = 8.5 × 10⁻⁴ N·s/m²** - **Standard temperature for reporting k = 20°C** (with correction to 27°C for KTU labs) - **Typical k values** (in **bold**): - **Gravel: 1 to 10⁻² cm/s** - **Sand: 10⁻² to 10⁻⁵ cm/s** - **Silt: 10⁻⁵ to 10⁻⁷ cm/s** - **Clay: < 10⁻⁷ cm/s** # SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ## 2.1 CONSTANT HEAD PERMEABILITY TEST ### 2.1.1 Principle In the constant head test, water is allowed to flow through a soil sample under a **steady (constant) hydraulic head difference**. The volume of water collected over a known time interval is measured, and Darcy's law is applied directly. ### 2.1.2 Applicability - **Best suited for:** Coarse-grained soils (clean sand, gravel) - **Reason:** High permeability → large discharge volumes → easy to measure accurately - **Reference Standard:** **IS 2720 (Part 17) – 1986** ### 2.1.3 Theoretical Derivation Logic (Step-by-Step Reasoning) 1. Water enters the soil sample from a constant-head reservoir. 2. A steady head difference $h$ is maintained between the inflow and outflow ends. 3. Volume of water $Q$ passing through the sample in time $t$ is collected. 4. By **Darcy's Law**: $Q = k \cdot i \cdot A \cdot t = k \cdot \left(\dfrac{h}{L}\right) \cdot A \cdot t$ 5. Rearranging for $k$: $$k = \dfrac{Q \cdot L}{A \cdot h \cdot t}$$ 6. Units check: $\dfrac{\text{cm}^3 \cdot \text{cm}}{\text{cm}^2 \cdot \text{cm} \cdot \text{s}} = \dfrac{\text{cm}}{\text{s}}$ ✓ ### 2.1.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - Design of **earth dams** (seepage analysis) - **Filter design** for retaining structures - **Dewatering** of excavations - **Stability of slopes** under seepage forces --- ## 2.2 VARIABLE (FALLING) HEAD PERMEABILITY TEST ### 2.2.1 Principle In the variable head test, water flows through a **saturated soil sample from a vertical standpipe** of smaller cross-sectional area. Since the fine soil cannot transmit water rapidly, the head in the standpipe continuously **decreases with time**. The rate of head drop is used to compute $k$. ### 2.2.2 Applicability - **Best suited for:** Fine-grained soils (silt, clay) - **Reason:** Very low permeability → small discharge volumes → cannot be measured accurately by collecting water; hence the head decay approach is used. - **Reference Standard:** **IS 2720 (Part 17) – 1986** ### 2.2.3 Theoretical Derivation Logic (Step-by-Step Reasoning) 1. At any instant, let the head in the standpipe be $h$ (above the outflow level). 2. In a small time interval $dt$, the head drops by $dh$. 3. Volume of water flowing through the sample in time $dt$ is: - Through standpipe: $dV = -a \cdot dh$ (negative sign because head is decreasing) - Through soil sample: $dV = k \cdot \left(\dfrac{h}{L}\right) \cdot A \cdot dt$ 4. Equating: $-a \cdot dh = k \cdot \left(\dfrac{h}{L}\right) \cdot A \cdot dt$ 5. Rearranging: $\dfrac{dh}{h} = -\dfrac{k \cdot A}{a \cdot L} \cdot dt$ 6. Integrating between limits $h_1$ (initial head) and $h_2$ (final head) for time 0 to $t$: $$k = \dfrac{2.303 \cdot a \cdot L}{A \cdot t} \cdot \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{h_1}{h_2}\right)$$ > [!NOTE] > The factor **2.303** comes from the change of base from natural log ($\ln$) to common log ($\log_{10}$), since $2.303 = \ln(10)$. --- ## 2.3 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA CHEAT SHEET | **Parameter** | **Constant Head Test** | **Variable Head Test** | |---|---|---| | **Governing Formula** | $k = \dfrac{Q \cdot L}{A \cdot h \cdot t}$ | $k = \dfrac{2.303 \cdot a \cdot L}{A \cdot t} \cdot \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{h_1}{h_2}\right)$ | | **Where** | $Q$ = volume of water collected (cm³) | $a$ = area of standpipe (cm²) | | | $L$ = length of soil sample (cm) | $L$ = length of soil sample (cm) | | | $A$ = cross-sectional area of sample (cm²) | $A$ = cross-sectional area of sample (cm²) | | | $h$ = constant head difference (cm) | $t$ = time interval (s) | | | $t$ = time of collection (s) | $h_1$ = initial head (cm) | | | | $h_2$ = final head (cm) | | **Units of k** | cm/s | cm/s | | **Soil Type** | Sand, Gravel | Silt, Clay | | **IS Code** | IS 2720 (Part 17) – 1986 | IS 2720 (Part 17) – 1986 | | **Sample Type** | Remolded/Undisturbed | Remolded/Undisturbed | | **Head Condition** | Maintained constant | Allowed to fall | | **Measured Quantity** | Volume of water | Time and head drop | --- ## 2.4 Temperature Correction (KTU Frequently Asked) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why correct for temperature?** > Viscosity of water decreases as temperature increases, making flow easier. To standardize, permeability is reported at **standard temperature 27°C** (KTU lab convention) or **20°C** (IS convention). The correction factor is: $$k_{27°C} = k_{T°C} \cdot \dfrac{\mu_T}{\mu_{27}}$$ A simplified ratio table for **water viscosity correction**: | Temperature (°C) | Viscosity Ratio $\dfrac{\mu_T}{\mu_{27}}$ | |---|---| | 15 | 1.235 | | 20 | 1.121 | | 25 | 1.032 | | 27 | 1.000 | | 30 | 0.961 | | 35 | 0.895 | --- ## 2.5 Why Two Different Tests? The "Why" Behind the Method > [!NOTE] > **The "Why" — Practical Reasoning** > > - In **coarse sand**, water flows so fast that collecting the outflow in a measuring cylinder gives a **measurable, accurate volume** in a short time. > - In **clay**, the flow is so slow that the volume collected in a reasonable time is **negligible** and unmeasurable. Hence, we measure the **rate at which the head drops** in a small standpipe, which amplifies the effect. > > Mathematically: in the variable head test, the **standpipe cross-section $a$ is much smaller** than the sample cross-section $A$ ($a << A$), which makes the head drop **measurable** even when $k$ is very small. # SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ## 3.1 EXHAUSTIVE DERIVATION — CONSTANT HEAD PERMEABILITY ### Step 1: Statement of Darcy's Law $$\text{Velocity, } v = k \cdot i = k \cdot \dfrac{h}{L}$$ ### Step 2: Discharge Equation Since discharge $q$ = velocity × area: $$q = v \cdot A = k \cdot \dfrac{h}{L} \cdot A$$ ### Step 3: Volume in Time t The total volume of water $Q$ flowing through the sample in time $t$ is: $$Q = q \cdot t = k \cdot \dfrac{h}{L} \cdot A \cdot t$$ ### Step 4: Solving for k Rearranging the equation by isolating $k$ on the left side: $$\boxed{k = \dfrac{Q \cdot L}{A \cdot h \cdot t}}$$ ### Step 5: Unit Verification $$\dfrac{[\text{cm}^3] \cdot [\text{cm}]}{[\text{cm}^2] \cdot [\text{cm}] \cdot [\text{s}]} = \dfrac{[\text{cm}^4]}{[\text{cm}^3] \cdot [\text{s}]} = \dfrac{[\text{cm}]}{[\text{s}]}$$ Units of k = **cm/s** ✓ --- ## 3.2 EXHAUSTIVE DERIVATION — VARIABLE HEAD PERMEABILITY ### Step 1: Setup the Differential Equation Let the head in the standpipe at time $t$ be $h$. - Volume of water flowing out of standpipe in time $dt$: $$dV = -a \cdot dh$$ (Negative sign indicates that head $h$ is **decreasing** as time progresses.) - Volume of water flowing through soil sample in time $dt$ (by Darcy's Law): $$dV = k \cdot \dfrac{h}{L} \cdot A \cdot dt$$ ### Step 2: Equate the Two Expressions $$-a \cdot dh = k \cdot \dfrac{h}{L} \cdot A \cdot dt$$ ### Step 3: Separate Variables Bring all $h$ terms to one side and $t$ to the other: $$\dfrac{dh}{h} = -\dfrac{k \cdot A}{a \cdot L} \cdot dt$$ ### Step 4: Integrate Between Limits Integrate from $h_1$ (head at $t = 0$) to $h_2$ (head at $t = t$): $$\int_{h_1}^{h_2} \dfrac{dh}{h} = -\dfrac{k \cdot A}{a \cdot L} \cdot \int_{0}^{t} dt$$ ### Step 5: Perform the Integration $$\left[\ln(h)\right]_{h_1}^{h_2} = -\dfrac{k \cdot A}{a \cdot L} \cdot t$$ $$\ln(h_2) - \ln(h_1) = -\dfrac{k \cdot A}{a \cdot L} \cdot t$$ $$\ln\left(\dfrac{h_2}{h_1}\right) = -\dfrac{k \cdot A \cdot t}{a \cdot L}$$ ### Step 6: Solve for k $$k = -\dfrac{a \cdot L}{A \cdot t} \cdot \ln\left(\dfrac{h_2}{h_1}\right)$$ Since $h_1 > h_2$ (head is decreasing), $\ln\left(\dfrac{h_2}{h_1}\right)$ is **negative**, making $k$ **positive**. ### Step 7: Convert to Base-10 Logarithm Using the identity: $\ln(x) = 2.303 \cdot \log_{10}(x)$: $$k = \dfrac{a \cdot L}{A \cdot t} \cdot 2.303 \cdot \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{h_1}{h_2}\right)$$ $$\boxed{k = \dfrac{2.303 \cdot a \cdot L}{A \cdot t} \cdot \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{h_1}{h_2}\right)}$$ ### Step 8: Unit Verification $$\dfrac{[\text{cm}^2] \cdot [\text{cm}]}{[\text{cm}^2] \cdot [\text{s}]} \cdot (\text{dimensionless}) = \dfrac{[\text{cm}]}{[\text{s}]}$$ Units of k = **cm/s** ✓ --- ## 3.3 SOLVED ANALYTICAL PROBLEMS ### Problem 1: Constant Head Test (KTU Pattern) A constant head permeability test was conducted on a sand sample. Given: - Length of sample, $L = 15$ cm - Cross-sectional area, $A = 25$ cm² - Constant head difference, $h = 20$ cm - Volume of water collected, $Q = 100$ cm³ - Time of collection, $t = 60$ s - Test temperature, $T = 30$°C **Find:** Coefficient of permeability at 27°C. #### Solution (Step-by-Step): **Step 1:** Apply the constant head formula: $$k_{30°C} = \dfrac{Q \cdot L}{A \cdot h \cdot t} = \dfrac{100 \times 15}{25 \times 20 \times 60}$$ **Step 2:** Compute numerator and denominator: $$k_{30°C} = \dfrac{1500}{30000} = 0.05 \text{ cm/s}$$ **Step 3:** Apply temperature correction. From the viscosity ratio table, at 30°C: $$\dfrac{\mu_{30}}{\mu_{27}} = 0.961$$ **Step 4:** Correct to 27°C: $$k_{27°C} = 0.05 \times 0.961 = 0.0481 \text{ cm/s}$$ **Step 5:** Report in standard form: $$\boxed{k_{27°C} \approx 4.81 \times 10^{-2} \text{ cm/s}}$$ --- ### Problem 2: Variable Head Test (KTU Pattern) A falling head permeability test was performed on a silty clay sample. Given: - Length of sample, $L = 12$ cm - Cross-sectional area of sample, $A = 40$ cm² - Area of standpipe, $a = 2$ cm² - Initial head, $h_1 = 80$ cm - Final head, $h_2 = 40$ cm - Time interval, $t = 600$ s **Find:** Coefficient of permeability $k$. #### Solution (Step-by-Step): **Step 1:** Apply the variable head formula: $$k = \dfrac{2.303 \cdot a \cdot L}{A \cdot t} \cdot \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{h_1}{h_2}\right)$$ **Step 2:** Compute the log term: $$\log_{10}\left(\dfrac{80}{40}\right) = \log_{10}(2) = 0.3010$$ **Step 3:** Substitute all values: $$k = \dfrac{2.303 \times 2 \times 12}{40 \times 600} \times 0.3010$$ **Step 4:** Compute numerator: $$2.303 \times 2 \times 12 = 55.272$$ **Step 5:** Compute denominator: $$40 \times 600 = 24000$$ **Step 6:** Final calculation: $$k = \dfrac{55.272 \times 0.3010}{24000} = \dfrac{16.637}{24000}$$ **Step 7:** Result: $$\boxed{k \approx 6.93 \times 10^{-4} \text{ cm/s}}$$ This is consistent with a **silty soil** classification (permeability between 10⁻⁵ and 10⁻⁷ cm/s — but closer to silt, so check soil description). --- ### 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix (KTU Quick-Revision) | **Feature** | **Constant Head** | **Variable Head** | |---|---|---| | Head | Constant ($h$ = const) | Variable ($h_1 \to h_2$) | | Measured | Volume $Q$ | Time $t$ for head drop | | Suitability | High $k$ soils | Low $k$ soils | | Formula type | Direct (arithmetic) | Logarithmic | | Number of readings | Minimum 3 | Minimum 3 sets of $(h_1, h_2, t)$ | | Typical $k$ range | $10^{-1}$ to $10^{-5}$ cm/s | $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-9}$ cm/s | | Compaction | Loose (controlled density) | As per in-situ density | | Saturation method | Bottom-up flow | Back-pressure saturation | | IS Code | IS 2720 (Part 17) – 1986 | IS 2720 (Part 17) – 1986 | | Error sources | Evaporation, leakage | Air bubbles, temperature drift | | Graphical plot | $Q$ vs $t$ (linear, slope = $kAh/L$) | $\log(h)$ vs $t$ (linear, slope = kA/(2.303aL)) | # SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Constant Head Permeability Apparatus ```mermaid flowchart TB A[CONSTANT HEAD WATER RESERVOIR
MARRIOTTE BOTTLE] --> B[INLET VALVE] B --> C[INLET WATER OVERFLOW BUCKET] C --> D[PERMEAMETER CELL
CONTAINS SOIL SAMPLE] D --> E[OUTLET OVERFLOW BUCKET] E --> F[GRADUATED MEASURING CYLINDER] D --> G[PIEZOMETER TAPS
FOR HEAD DIFFERENCE h] A --> H[AIR INLET TUBE
MAINTAINS CONSTANT HEAD] F --> I[STOPWATCH FOR TIME t] style A fill:#e1f5ff style D fill:#fff4e1 style F fill:#e8f5e8 style I fill:#fce4ec ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — Constant Head Test Procedure ```mermaid flowchart TD S1[START: SAMPLE PREPARATION] --> S2[Place sand in permeameter
using compaction technique] S2 --> S3[Saturate sample from
bottom upward] S3 --> S4[Set constant head difference h
using overflow tanks] S4 --> S5[Allow steady flow to establish] S5 --> S6[Collect water in graduated
cylinder for time t] S6 --> S7[Record Q, t, h, L, A] S7 --> S8{Repeat for at least
3 trials} S8 --> S9[Apply Darcy formula
k = QL/Aht] S9 --> S10[Apply temperature correction
to standard 27 degree C] S10 --> S11[Report k in cm per sec] S8 -- No --> S6 style S1 fill:#bbdefb style S9 fill:#c8e6c9 style S11 fill:#fff9c4 ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Variable Head Permeability Apparatus ```mermaid flowchart TB P1[VERTICAL STANDPIPE
Area a in cm squared] --> P2[INITIAL HEAD h1
WATER LEVEL MARK] P2 --> P3[PERMEAMETER CELL
SOIL SAMPLE Length L] P3 --> P4[OUTLET TUBE] P4 --> P5[COLLECTION BUCKET] P3 --> P6[CROSS SECTION AREA A] P1 --> P7[FINAL HEAD h2
AFTER TIME t] P7 --> P8[STOPWATCH t] P8 --> P9[RECORD h1, h2, t, a, A, L] style P1 fill:#e1f5ff style P3 fill:#fff4e1 style P7 fill:#fce4ec style P9 fill:#c8e6c9 ``` ## 4.4 Mermaid Flowchart — Variable Head Test Procedure ```mermaid flowchart TD V1[START: SAMPLE PREPARATION] --> V2[Compact fine soil in
permeameter to in-situ density] V2 --> V3[Saturate sample fully
under back pressure] V3 --> V4[Connect standpipe of
known area a] V4 --> V5[Fill standpipe to initial
head h1] V5 --> V6[Start stopwatch] V6 --> V7[Record head h2 at
time t] V7 --> V8{Repeat for different
h1 h2 combinations} V8 --> V9[Apply variable head formula
k = 2.303aL log h1 by h2 over At] V9 --> V10[Plot log h versus t
for verification] V10 --> V11[Report k in cm per sec] V8 -- No --> V5 style V1 fill:#bbdefb style V9 fill:#c8e6c9 style V10 fill:#fff9c4 style V11 fill:#f8bbd0 ``` ## 4.5 Mermaid Comparative Decision Diagram — Test Selection ```mermaid flowchart TD Q1[START: Identify soil type] --> Q2{Is soil coarse or fine?} Q2 -- Coarse Gravel Sand --> Q3[Use CONSTANT HEAD TEST
k from 10 power minus 1 to 10 power minus 5 cm per sec] Q2 -- Fine Silt Clay --> Q4[Use VARIABLE HEAD TEST
k less than 10 power minus 5 cm per sec] Q3 --> Q5[Measure volume of water Q
in time t at constant h] Q4 --> Q6[Measure head drop from h1 to h2
in time t] Q5 --> Q7[Apply k = QL over Aht] Q6 --> Q8[Apply k = 2.303aL over At times log h1 by h2] Q7 --> Q9[END: Report k with
temperature correction] Q8 --> Q9 style Q1 fill:#e3f2fd style Q3 fill:#fff3e0 style Q4 fill:#fce4ec style Q9 fill:#c8e6c9 ``` ## 4.6 Mermaid Block Diagram — Permeameter Cross-Sectional View ```mermaid flowchart LR H1[UPPER CHAMBER
Inlet water with overflow] --> H2[PERFORATED TOP PLATE
Distributes flow uniformly] H2 --> H3[SOIL SAMPLE
Length L, Area A] H3 --> H4[PERFORATED BOTTOM PLATE
Supports sample] H4 --> H5[LOWER CHAMBER
Outlet water to collection] H3 --> H6[SIDE PIEZOMETER TAP 1
Upper head measurement] H3 --> H7[SIDE PIEZOMETER TAP 2
Lower head measurement] H6 --> H8[HEAD DIFFERENCE h
= h upper minus h lower] H7 --> H8 style H1 fill:#bbdefb style H3 fill:#fff9c4 style H5 fill:#c8e6c9 style H8 fill:#f8bbd0 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading Guidance for Students:** > The Mermaid diagrams above are **functional block-level representations** of the actual apparatus. In the KTU lab exam, you may be asked to draw a **neat labeled sketch** of the permeameter on your answer sheet. Use these diagrams as a reference for identifying and naming components like: **Marriotte bottle, overflow tanks, permeameter cell, piezometer taps, perforated plates, and standpipe**. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP --- ## 5.1 PART A QUESTIONS (3 MARKS EACH) ### **Question 1: Define the coefficient of permeability and state Darcy's law. Mention the range of k for different soils.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Answer:** **Definition:** The coefficient of permeability ($k$) is defined as the velocity of flow of water through a soil mass under a unit hydraulic gradient, expressed in **cm/s** or **m/s**. **Darcy's Law:** For laminar flow through saturated soil, $$q = k \cdot i \cdot A$$ where $q$ is discharge, $i$ is hydraulic gradient, and $A$ is cross-sectional area. **Range of k:** - **Gravel:** $10^{-1}$ to $10^{-2}$ cm/s - **Sand:** $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-5}$ cm/s - **Silt:** $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-7}$ cm/s - **Clay:** $< 10^{-7}$ cm/s --- ### **Question 2: Differentiate between constant head and variable head permeability tests.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer:** | **Constant Head Test** | **Variable Head Test** | |---|---| | Head difference is kept constant | Head difference keeps falling | | Suitable for coarse-grained soils (sand, gravel) | Suitable for fine-grained soils (silt, clay) | | Volume of water collected is measured | Time for head to fall from $h_1$ to $h_2$ is measured | | Formula: $k = \dfrac{QL}{Aht}$ | Formula: $k = \dfrac{2.303 \cdot aL}{At} \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{h_1}{h_2}\right)$ | | IS Code: IS 2720 (Part 17) – 1986 | IS Code: IS 2720 (Part 17) – 1986 | --- ## 5.2 PART B QUESTIONS (14 MARKS — INTERNAL CHOICE) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the constant head permeability test apparatus. Describe the test procedure, formula derivation, and the precautions to be taken. **\[7 Marks\]** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(b)** In a constant head permeability test on a sand sample, the following data were obtained: - Length of sample, $L = 20$ cm - Cross-sectional area, $A = 30$ cm² - Constant head, $h = 25$ cm - Volume of water collected in 90 s, $Q = 250$ cm³ Compute the coefficient of permeability. If the test was conducted at 30°C, find $k$ at 27°C. **\[7 Marks\]** #### **Model Solution for (a):** **Apparatus Components:** 1. **Marriotte bottle / Constant head tank** – maintains steady head 2. **Permeameter cell** – cylindrical mould holding the soil sample 3. **Perforated brass plates** – top and bottom for uniform flow 4. **Overflow tanks** – at inlet and outlet to maintain head difference 5. **Graduated measuring cylinder** – collects outflow 6. **Stopwatch** – measures time 7. **Piezometer taps** – measure head difference **Procedure:** 1. Prepare the sand sample at desired density in the permeameter 2. Saturate the sample by allowing water to flow from bottom to top 3. Set the constant head difference $h$ using the overflow levels 4. Allow steady-state flow to establish (discharge becomes constant) 5. Collect water in measuring cylinder and record volume $Q$ for time $t$ 6. Repeat for at least 3 trials and take the average value **Derivation:** (As shown in Section 3.1) $$k = \dfrac{Q \cdot L}{A \cdot h \cdot t}$$ **Precautions:** - Soil must be fully saturated - Flow should be laminar (no turbulence) - No air bubbles should be trapped - No leakage from sides - Temperature should be recorded for correction #### **Model Solution for (b):** **Step 1:** Apply the constant head formula: $$k_{30°C} = \dfrac{Q \cdot L}{A \cdot h \cdot t} = \dfrac{250 \times 20}{30 \times 25 \times 90}$$ **Step 2:** Compute: $$k_{30°C} = \dfrac{5000}{67500} = 0.0741 \text{ cm/s}$$ **Step 3:** Apply temperature correction using viscosity ratio at 30°C = 0.961: $$k_{27°C} = 0.0741 \times 0.961 = 0.0712 \text{ cm/s}$$ **Final Answer:** $$\boxed{k_{27°C} \approx 7.12 \times 10^{-2} \text{ cm/s}}$$ This indicates a **medium sand** to **coarse sand** classification. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, describe the variable head (falling head) permeability test. Derive the formula used and list the precautions. **\[7 Marks\]** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(b)** A falling head permeability test was conducted on a clay sample. The following data were recorded: - Length of sample, $L = 10$ cm - Area of sample, $A = 50$ cm² - Area of standpipe, $a = 1.5$ cm² - Initial head, $h_1 = 100$ cm - Final head, $h_2 = 25$ cm - Time for head to fall, $t = 900$ s Determine the coefficient of permeability and classify the soil. **\[7 Marks\]** #### **Model Solution for (a):** **Apparatus:** Permeameter, standpipe (small cross-section), stopwatch, de-aired water, vacuum saturation system. **Procedure:** 1. Compact the clay sample in the permeameter to in-situ density 2. Saturate the sample under back pressure to remove air 3. Connect the standpipe of known area $a$ at the top 4. Fill the standpipe to the initial head $h_1$ 5. Start the stopwatch and record the time $t$ for the head to fall to $h_2$ 6. Repeat for different values of $h_1$ and $h_2$ **Derivation:** (As shown in Section 3.2) $$k = \dfrac{2.303 \cdot a \cdot L}{A \cdot t} \cdot \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{h_1}{h_2}\right)$$ **Precautions:** - Use de-aired water to prevent air bubbles - Ensure full saturation of the sample - Maintain constant temperature - Use a standpipe of small area to amplify the head drop - Avoid evaporation losses #### **Model Solution for (b):** **Step 1:** Apply the variable head formula: $$k = \dfrac{2.303 \cdot a \cdot L}{A \cdot t} \cdot \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{h_1}{h_2}\right)$$ **Step 2:** Compute the log term: $$\log_{10}\left(\dfrac{100}{25}\right) = \log_{10}(4) = 0.6021$$ **Step 3:** Substitute values: $$k = \dfrac{2.303 \times 1.5 \times 10}{50 \times 900} \times 0.6021$$ **Step 4:** Compute numerator: $$2.303 \times 1.5 \times 10 = 34.545$$ **Step 5:** Compute denominator: $$50 \times 900 = 45000$$ **Step 6:** Final result: $$k = \dfrac{34.545 \times 0.6021}{45000} = \dfrac{20.797}{45000}$$ $$\boxed{k \approx 4.62 \times 10^{-4} \text{ cm/s}}$$ **Classification:** This value lies in the range of **silt** ($10^{-5}$ to $10^{-7}$ cm/s is clay; $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-5}$ cm/s is silt). With $k = 4.62 \times 10^{-4}$ cm/s, the soil is classified as **silty clay or clayey silt**. --- ## 5.3 KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING — COMMON PITFALLS > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:** > > 1. **Unit Inconsistency (–2 marks):** Mixing up cm and mm for $L$, $A$, $h$. Always convert everything to **cm** before substitution. > > 2. **Forgetting Temperature Correction (–1 to 2 marks):** KTU examiners explicitly check whether you have reported $k$ at the standard temperature (27°C). If the test was at any other temperature, the viscosity correction is **mandatory**. > > 3. **Wrong Formula Application (–3 marks):** Students often mistakenly use the constant head formula for fine soils or vice versa. **Always check the soil type first.** > > 4. **Sign Error in Variable Head (–1 mark):** Since $h_1 > h_2$, the ratio $\dfrac{h_1}{h_2} > 1$, making $\log_{10} > 0$, and $k$ comes out **positive**. Do not put a negative sign in the final answer. > > 5. **Skipping the "log" Term:** Some students write $k = \dfrac{2.303 aL}{At}$ without $\log_{10}(h_1/h_2)$. This is a **major error** and will cost full marks for that part. > > 6. **Not Mentioning IS Code:** KTU 2024 scheme gives weightage to **standard references**. Always cite **IS 2720 (Part 17) – 1986**. > > 7. **Average of Multiple Trials Not Reported:** Examiners expect at least 3 readings and the **mean value of k** to be reported. Single-trial answers lose marks. > > 8. **No Mention of Darcy's Law Validity:** Always state that the test is valid only for **laminar flow** (Reynolds Number < 1). --- ## 5.4 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Permeability Test — Quick Revision Checklist for KTU 2024** - ✅ **Permeability (k)** = ease of water flow through soil; units = **cm/s** - ✅ **Darcy's Law** = $q = kiA$ — foundation of all permeability analysis - ✅ **Constant Head Test** is for **coarse-grained** soils (sand, gravel) - ✅ **Variable Head Test** is for **fine-grained** soils (silt, clay) - ✅ **Constant Head Formula:** $k = \dfrac{QL}{Aht}$ - ✅ **Variable Head Formula:** $k = \dfrac{2.303 aL}{At} \log_{10}(h_1/h_2)$ - ✅ **IS Code for both tests:** **IS 2720 (Part 17) – 1986** - ✅ **Standard reporting temperature:** **27°C** (KTU) or **20°C** (IS) - ✅ **Temperature correction:** $k_{27} = k_T \times (\mu_T / \mu_{27})$ - ✅ **Permeability Range:** - Gravel: $10^{-1}$ to $10^{-2}$ cm/s - Sand: $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-5}$ cm/s - Silt: $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-7}$ cm/s - Clay: $< 10^{-7}$ cm/s - ✅ **Validity Condition:** Laminar flow (Re < 1); Saturated soil; Constant temperature - ✅ **Precautions:** De-aired water, no air bubbles, no side leakage, full saturation - ✅ **Apparatus Components:** Permeameter, Marriotte bottle / standpipe, overflow tanks, perforated plates, piezometer taps, measuring cylinder, stopwatch - ✅ **For Variable Head Test:** Plotting **log h vs t** gives a **straight line** (graphical verification of Darcy's law) - ✅ **For Constant Head Test:** Plotting **Q vs t** gives a **straight line through origin** - ✅ **Average of at least 3 trials** must be reported as the final k value - ✅ **KTU favorite numerical:** Always provide $L$, $A$, $h$ (or $a$, $h_1$, $h_2$), and $t$ — examiner will ask for $k$ and its temperature correction --- > **END OF NOTES — PERMEABILITY TEST (CONSTANT HEAD & VARIABLE HEAD)** > *Prepared as per KTU 2024 Scheme — PCCEL507 Geotechnical Engineering Lab*

topics.title: Triaxial Shear Strength Test
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCEL507 - GEOTECHNICAL ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Triaxial Shear Strength Test

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Triaxial Shear Strength Test** is a standardized laboratory procedure used in geotechnical engineering to determine the **shear strength parameters** of cohesive and cohesionless soils under controlled drainage and stress conditions. In this test, a cylindrical soil specimen (typically of dimensions **38 mm diameter × 76 mm height**, or **50 mm × 100 mm**, or **100 mm × 200 mm**) is enclosed in a thin, flexible **latex rubber membrane** and placed inside a sealed **triaxial cell**. The specimen is subjected independently to:
1. An all-around **confining (cell) pressure**, $\sigma_3$
2. An additional **axial (deviator) stress** applied through a loading ram until failure occurs.

The test is governed by the standards **IS 2720 (Part 11):1993** and **IS 2720 (Part 12):1981**, conforming to the **Mohr-Coulomb Failure Criterion** for soil strength analysis.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The triaxial test simulates the true three-dimensional stress state experienced by soil elements in the field far more accurately than a direct shear or unconfined compression test, making it the most reliable method for measuring soil shear strength.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuitive Understanding)

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Squeezing a Filled Balloon" Analogy**

Imagine a small water balloon filled with sand (representing the soil specimen) placed inside a sealed plastic pressure cooker (the triaxial cell). The pressure cooker applies uniform pressure from all sides (the cell pressure, $\sigma_3$). Now, you press down on the balloon from the top with a piston (the deviator load). The balloon will bulge out sideways and eventually split or shear along a failure plane.

- The **squeezing from all sides** prevents the sand from simply spilling sideways — it constrains it.
- The **downward push** creates a stress difference ($\sigma_1 - \sigma_3$) that tries to shear the sand grains past each other.
- The **moment of splitting** corresponds to the **failure** of the soil, and the loads at that moment tell us how strong the soil is.

This is essentially what the triaxial machine does, but in a controlled, instrumented, and scientific manner.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Standard confining pressures** commonly used: **100 kPa, 200 kPa, 300 kPa** (for 38 mm specimens); **50, 100, 150 kPa** for sensitive soils.
- **Loading rate (strain-controlled)**: typically **0.5 % to 1.0 % per minute** (for UU), **0.1 % per minute** (for CU/CD with drained conditions).
- **Standard specimen sizes**: 38 mm × 76 mm (L/D = 2.0) is most common in KTU-affiliated labs.
- **Density of water ($\rho_w$)** used for proving rings: **9.81 kN/m³** (or $g = 9.81$ m/s² for force conversion).

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic of the Test (Step-by-Step)

The triaxial test works on the principle of **independently controlled principal stresses**:

1. **Specimen Preparation**: Soil is compacted or extruded to a known initial state (density, moisture content) and placed between porous stones and filter paper drains.
2. **Membrane Sealing**: The specimen is wrapped in a thin latex membrane (≈ 0.2–0.3 mm thick) and sealed with O-rings to the top cap and base pedestal — this isolates the specimen's internal pore water from the cell fluid.
3. **Confining Pressure Application**: The cell is filled with de-aired water (or glycerine), and pressurized to $\sigma_3$ via an air-water pressure system. Under this isotropic stress, the specimen experiences no shear stress.
4. **Deviator Loading**: A loading ram applies additional axial force $P$ at a controlled rate. This increases the major principal stress $\sigma_1$ while $\sigma_3$ remains constant.
5. **Drainage and Pore Pressure**: Depending on test type, drainage valves are open or closed, controlling whether pore water pressure ($u$) dissipates or builds up.
6. **Failure Detection**: Failure is identified by either:
   - Peak deviator stress, OR
   - Steady-state deviator stress at large strains, OR
   - Reaching 15–20 % axial strain.
7. **Mohr Circle Construction**: For each confining pressure, a Mohr's circle is plotted using the total (or effective) stresses at failure. The common tangent is the **Mohr-Coulomb failure envelope**.

## 2.2 Why This Approach? (The "Why" Behind the Steps)

- **Why isotropic confining pressure?** It replicates the in-situ lateral earth pressure ($\sigma_3 = K_0 \cdot \sigma_v$) and provides a known stress state for the radial direction.
- **Why control drainage?** Because the *engineering behavior* of saturated soils is governed by **effective stress** ($\sigma' = \sigma - u$). Whether water can drain determines whether the soil consolidates, generates pore pressure, or remains undrained.
- **Why a thin membrane?** It transmits the cell pressure hydrostatically to the specimen without allowing leakage, yet applies negligible lateral restraint.

## 2.3 Types of Triaxial Tests (KTU High-Yield Table)

| Test Type | Symbol | Drainage During Shear | Pore Pressure Measured? | Parameters Obtained | Typical Soil |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Unconsolidated Undrained** | **UU** | No | No (often) | $c_u, \phi_u = 0$ | Saturated clays (short-term) |
| **Consolidated Undrained** | **CU** | No | **Yes** | $c', \phi'$ and $c, \phi$ | Saturated clays, intermediate-term |
| **Consolidated Drained** | **CD** | Yes | (≈ 0) | $c', \phi'$ | Sands, long-term clay analysis |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** In the **UU test**, since drainage is not allowed, the test is *very fast* (5–10 minutes), simulating sudden loading. In **CU** and **CD**, consolidation under $\sigma_3$ is allowed *before* shearing, which is critical for measuring effective stress parameters.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cross-sectional Area (initial) | $A_0 = \dfrac{\pi D_0^2}{4}$ | mm² | $D_0$ = initial diameter |
| 2 | Initial Volume | $V_0 = A_0 \cdot H_0$ | mm³ | $H_0$ = initial height |
| 3 | Corrected Area (during shearing) | $A = \dfrac{A_0 (1 - \varepsilon_v)}{(1 - \varepsilon_a)}$ | mm² | $\varepsilon_v$ = volumetric strain, $\varepsilon_a$ = axial strain |
| 4 | Axial Strain | $\varepsilon_a = \dfrac{\Delta H}{H_0} \times 100$ | % | $\Delta H$ = change in height |
| 5 | Deviator Load | $P = \text{Proving Ring Factor} \times \text{Dial Reading}$ | N or kN | Depends on proving ring |
| 6 | Deviator Stress | $\sigma_d = \dfrac{P}{A}$ | kPa | Where $A$ is corrected area |
| 7 | Total Major Principal Stress | $\sigma_1 = \sigma_3 + \sigma_d$ | kPa | At failure |
| 8 | Effective Major Principal Stress | $\sigma_1' = \sigma_1 - u$ | kPa | If $u$ measured |
| 9 | Effective Confining Stress | $\sigma_3' = \sigma_3 - u$ | kPa | If $u$ measured |
| 10 | Pore Pressure Coefficient B (Skempton) | $B = \dfrac{\Delta u}{\Delta \sigma_3}$ | dimensionless | Measured during saturation check |
| 11 | Pore Pressure Coefficient A (at failure) | $A_f = \dfrac{\Delta u_f}{\sigma_{d,f}}$ | dimensionless | Measured during CU test |
| 12 | Mohr-Coulomb Failure Criterion (Total Stress) | $\tau_f = c + \sigma \tan \phi$ | kPa | Linear envelope |
| 13 | Mohr-Coulomb Failure Criterion (Effective) | $\tau_f = c' + \sigma' \tan \phi'$ | kPa | For CU, CD tests |
| 14 | Shear Strength from Triaxial (CU/CD) | $s = \dfrac{\sigma_1' - \sigma_3'}{2} \cos \phi'$ | kPa | At failure on failure plane |
| 15 | Angle of Failure Plane (theoretical) | $\theta_f = 45° + \dfrac{\phi'}{2}$ | degrees | From $\sigma_1$ axis |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipes vs. LaTeX reminder:** In actual handwritten or typed KTU answers, use `\vert` notation for absolute value. The above table uses no problematic pipe characters.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Slope Stability Analysis**: Triaxial-derived $c'$ and $\phi'$ are direct inputs to limit-equilibrium methods (Bishop, Fellenius) for natural and cut slopes.
- **Earth Pressure Calculations**: For retaining wall design, active/passive earth pressure coefficients use $\phi'$.
- **Bearing Capacity**: Terzaghi, Meyerhof, and Hansen bearing capacity equations use $c$ and $\phi$.
- **Dam and Embankment Design**: Long-term stability requires effective parameters from CU/CD triaxial tests.
- **Tunneling and Excavations**: Short-term stability uses undrained strength ($c_u$), long-term uses effective strength.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Corrected Cross-Sectional Area

When the soil specimen is sheared in the triaxial cell, its shape deforms. The original cross-sectional area $A_0$ is no longer accurate. We must account for both:
- **Axial shortening** (specimen gets shorter but bulges out → larger area)
- **Volume change** (drained tests: specimen may compress or dilate; undrained tests: volume is constant)

**Step 1:** Define the axial strain:

$$\varepsilon_a = \frac{\Delta H}{H_0}$$

The current height of the specimen is:

$$H = H_0 - \Delta H = H_0 (1 - \varepsilon_a)$$

**Step 2:** Define the volumetric strain:

$$\varepsilon_v = \frac{\Delta V}{V_0}$$

The current volume is:

$$V = V_0 (1 - \varepsilon_v)$$

**Step 3:** Assume the specimen deforms as a right cylinder (uniform bulging). The current cross-sectional area is:

$$A = \frac{V}{H} = \frac{V_0 (1 - \varepsilon_v)}{H_0 (1 - \varepsilon_a)}$$

**Step 4:** Substituting $V_0 = A_0 H_0$:

$$A = \frac{A_0 H_0 (1 - \varepsilon_v)}{H_0 (1 - \varepsilon_a)} = \frac{A_0 (1 - \varepsilon_v)}{(1 - \varepsilon_a)}$$

**Step 5:** Special case — **Undrained test (constant volume)**: Since $V = V_0$, we have $\varepsilon_v = 0$, and the formula simplifies to:

$$A = \frac{A_0}{(1 - \varepsilon_a)}$$

This is the most commonly used form in KTU lab calculations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Intuition:** As axial strain increases, the denominator $(1 - \varepsilon_a)$ decreases, so the corrected area $A$ increases. This means the same deviator load $P$ produces a *smaller* deviator stress at higher strains — capturing the strain-softening behavior realistically.

## 3.2 Derivation of Skempton's Pore Pressure Coefficient B

When an all-around pressure $\Delta\sigma_3$ is applied to a saturated soil specimen with closed drainage valves, the pore water pressure increases by $\Delta u$. Skempton (1954) defined:

$$B = \frac{\Delta u}{\Delta \sigma_3}$$

**Step 1:** Consider a soil skeleton with bulk modulus $K_s$ and pore water with bulk modulus $K_w$. The total volume change of the soil element under isotropic loading is the sum of the volume change of the soil grains and the volume change of the pore water.

**Step 2:** For a saturated soil, the volume change of water equals the change in pore space:

$$\frac{\Delta V_w}{V} = \frac{\Delta u}{K_w} \cdot n$$

where $n$ is porosity. Meanwhile, the volume change of the soil skeleton is:

$$\frac{\Delta V_s}{V} = \frac{\Delta \sigma_3 - \Delta u}{K_s}$$

**Step 3:** The volumetric strain of the specimen is:

$$\varepsilon_v = \frac{\Delta V_s}{V} = \frac{\Delta \sigma_3 - \Delta u}{K_s}$$

**Step 4:** Equate the expressions and solve for $\Delta u$:

$$\frac{\Delta u}{K_w} \cdot n = \frac{\Delta \sigma_3 - \Delta u}{K_s}$$

$$\Delta u \cdot \frac{n}{K_w} = \frac{\Delta \sigma_3}{K_s} - \frac{\Delta u}{K_s}$$

$$\Delta u \left( \frac{n}{K_w} + \frac{1}{K_s} \right) = \frac{\Delta \sigma_3}{K_s}$$

**Step 5:** Solving:

$$\Delta u = \frac{\Delta \sigma_3}{1 + \dfrac{n K_s}{K_w}}$$

**Step 6:** Recognizing $K_w \gg K_s$ and $n < 1$, for **fully saturated** soils $B \to 1.0$. For **dry** soils $B = 0$. For **partially saturated** soils, $0 < B < 1$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield:** A **B-value test** is performed before each CU/CD test. If $B < 0.95$ for a "saturated" clay specimen, the saturation is inadequate and the test must be repeated with back-pressure saturation.

## 3.3 Derivation of Mohr-Coulomb Parameters from Multiple Triaxial Tests

When at least **three specimens** are tested at different confining pressures ($\sigma_{3,1}, \sigma_{3,2}, \sigma_{3,3}$), we obtain three Mohr circles at failure.

**Step 1:** For each specimen at failure:
- Major principal stress: $\sigma_{1,f} = \sigma_3 + \sigma_{d,f}$
- Minor principal stress: $\sigma_3$ (constant)
- Circle center: $C = \dfrac{\sigma_1 + \sigma_3}{2}$
- Circle radius: $R = \dfrac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2} = \dfrac{\sigma_d}{2}$

**Step 2:** The Mohr-Coulomb envelope is a straight line in the $\tau$–$\sigma$ plane. Its equation is:

$$\tau_f = c + \sigma \tan \phi$$

**Step 3:** For a Mohr circle *tangent* to this line, the geometry of the circle and tangent line gives:

$$\sin \phi = \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{\sigma_1 + \sigma_3 + 2c \cot \phi}$$

This is the **Mohr-Coulomb failure criterion** in principal stress form.

**Step 4:** Rearranging for $p$ (center) and $q$ (radius) parameters:

$$p = \frac{\sigma_1 + \sigma_3}{2}, \quad q = \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2}$$

**Step 5:** For **effective stress** parameters (CU/CD):

$$q_f = p_f \sin \phi' + c' \cos \phi'$$

This is a linear relationship in $p'$–$q'$ space. A plot of $q_f$ vs $p_f$ gives a straight line with:
- Slope $M = \sin \phi'$
- Intercept (at $p' = 0$) = $c' \cos \phi'$

**Step 6:** Solving explicitly:

$$\phi' = \sin^{-1}(M), \quad c' = \frac{\text{Intercept}}{\cos \phi'}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU valuation:** Board examiners expect you to **plot** the Mohr circles (or $p$–$q$ diagram), draw the **best-fit failure envelope**, and *label* the angle $\phi$ and intercept $c$ on the diagram. Marks are awarded for clarity of the graphical work as well as the numerical result.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Step-by-Step)

> **Problem:** A triaxial CU test was performed on a saturated clay specimen with the following data. Compute the deviator stress at failure and the effective shear strength parameters.

| Specimen | $\sigma_3$ (kPa) | Deviator Load at Failure $P_f$ (N) | Pore Pressure at Failure $u_f$ (kPa) | Axial Strain at Failure $\varepsilon_{a,f}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 100 | 220 | 60 | 2.0 % |
| 2 | 200 | 350 | 120 | 3.5 % |
| 3 | 300 | 480 | 180 | 5.0 % |

**Given:** Initial specimen dimensions: $D_0 = 38$ mm, $H_0 = 76$ mm.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute initial area:
$$A_0 = \frac{\pi (38)^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 1444}{4} = 1133.54 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 2:** Compute corrected area (assuming undrained CU, so $\varepsilon_v = 0$):

**Specimen 1** ($\varepsilon_{a,f} = 0.02$):
$$A_1 = \frac{1133.54}{1 - 0.02} = \frac{1133.54}{0.98} = 1156.67 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Specimen 2** ($\varepsilon_{a,f} = 0.035$):
$$A_2 = \frac{1133.54}{1 - 0.035} = \frac{1133.54}{0.965} = 1174.65 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Specimen 3** ($\varepsilon_{a,f} = 0.05$):
$$A_3 = \frac{1133.54}{1 - 0.05} = \frac{1133.54}{0.95} = 1193.20 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 3:** Compute deviator stress at failure:

**Specimen 1:**
$$\sigma_{d,f,1} = \frac{220 \text{ N}}{1156.67 \text{ mm}^2} = 0.1902 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 190.2 \text{ kPa}$$

**Specimen 2:**
$$\sigma_{d,f,2} = \frac{350}{1174.65} = 0.2980 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 298.0 \text{ kPa}$$

**Specimen 3:**
$$\sigma_{d,f,3} = \frac{480}{1193.20} = 0.4023 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 402.3 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 4:** Compute total and effective principal stresses at failure:

**Specimen 1:**
$$\sigma_1 = 100 + 190.2 = 290.2 \text{ kPa}, \quad \sigma_1' = 290.2 - 60 = 230.2 \text{ kPa}, \quad \sigma_3' = 100 - 60 = 40 \text{ kPa}$$

**Specimen 2:**
$$\sigma_1 = 200 + 298.0 = 498.0 \text{ kPa}, \quad \sigma_1' = 498.0 - 120 = 378.0 \text{ kPa}, \quad \sigma_3' = 200 - 120 = 80 \text{ kPa}$$

**Specimen 3:**
$$\sigma_1 = 300 + 402.3 = 702.3 \text{ kPa}, \quad \sigma_1' = 702.3 - 180 = 522.3 \text{ kPa}, \quad \sigma_3' = 300 - 180 = 120 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 5:** Compute $p'$ and $q$ for the $p'$–$q'$ plot:

| Specimen | $p' = \dfrac{\sigma_1' + \sigma_3'}{2}$ (kPa) | $q = \dfrac{\sigma_1' - \sigma_3'}{2}$ (kPa) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $(230.2 + 40)/2 = 135.1$ | $(230.2 - 40)/2 = 95.1$ |
| 2 | $(378.0 + 80)/2 = 229.0$ | $(378.0 - 80)/2 = 149.0$ |
| 3 | $(522.3 + 120)/2 = 321.15$ | $(522.3 - 120)/2 = 201.15$ |

**Step 6:** Linear regression of $q$ vs $p'$:

Using two-point slope (Specimens 1 and 3):
$$M = \frac{201.15 - 95.1}{321.15 - 135.1} = \frac{106.05}{186.05} = 0.5700$$

**Step 7:** Compute $\phi'$:
$$\phi' = \sin^{-1}(0.5700) = 34.75° \approx 34.75°$$

**Step 8:** Compute $c'$:
Using Specimen 1: $q = M \cdot p' + c' \cos \phi'$
$$95.1 = 0.5700 \times 135.1 + c' \cos(34.75°)$$
$$95.1 = 77.01 + c' \times 0.8222$$
$$c' = \frac{95.1 - 77.01}{0.8222} = 22.02 \text{ kPa}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer for Worked Example:**
> - $\phi' \approx 34.75°$ (effective friction angle)
> - $c' \approx 22.0$ kPa (effective cohesion intercept)
> - **Note**: In *real* saturated normally consolidated clay, $c' \approx 0$. The non-zero $c'$ here indicates either a slightly overconsolidated clay or measurement scatter.
