# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define the Unconsolidated Undrained (UU) Triaxial Test. State any two limitations of this test.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Unconsolidated Undrained (UU) Triaxial Test** is a type of triaxial shear test in which the soil specimen is *not allowed to consolidate* under the applied confining pressure $\sigma_3$, and *no drainage is permitted* during shearing. Both the cell pressure and the deviator load are applied rapidly so that the loading simulates a sudden or short-term condition.

The test directly yields the **undrained shear strength parameters**:
- $c_u$ = undrained cohesion (in kPa)
- $\phi_u = 0°$ (for saturated clays, since undrained shearing produces no change in effective stress and $\phi_u$ is theoretically zero)

**Two limitations:**
1. The test does *not* allow measurement of pore water pressure, so effective stress parameters ($c'$, $\phi'$) cannot be obtained. This makes it unsuitable for long-term stability analysis.
2. The test is *very fast* (typically 5–10 minutes), which means the results depend heavily on the rate of loading and do not represent the true in-situ stress history of the soil.

---

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is Skempton's pore pressure coefficient B? How is it determined in a triaxial test?**

**Model Answer:**

**Skempton's pore pressure coefficient B** is defined as the ratio of the change in pore water pressure ($\Delta u$) to the change in all-around confining pressure ($\Delta \sigma_3$):

$$B = \frac{\Delta u}{\Delta \sigma_3}$$

It is a measure of the **degree of saturation** of the soil specimen.

**Determination in the triaxial test (B-test):**
1. The triaxial cell is assembled with the soil specimen, and the drainage valve leading to the pore pressure measuring system is **closed**.
2. An initial cell pressure is applied (typically 50 kPa), and the pore pressure $u_0$ is recorded.
3. The cell pressure is then increased by an increment $\Delta \sigma_3$ (typically 50 kPa), and the corresponding increase in pore pressure $\Delta u$ is measured.
4. The coefficient B is computed as:
$$B = \frac{\Delta u}{\Delta \sigma_3} = \frac{u_1 - u_0}{\Delta \sigma_3}$$

For a **fully saturated** soil, $B \approx 1.0$. For a **dry** soil, $B = 0$. For a **partially saturated** soil, $0 < B < 1$. A B-value greater than 0.95 is generally required for a saturated specimen to be valid for CU/CD testing.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (Choice 1)** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**A triaxial Consolidated Undrained (CU) test was conducted on a saturated clay specimen with the following data. Determine the effective shear strength parameters $c'$ and $\phi'$.**

| Specimen | Confining Pressure $\sigma_3$ (kPa) | Deviator Load at Failure $P_f$ (N) | Pore Pressure at Failure $u_f$ (kPa) | Axial Strain at Failure $\varepsilon_{a,f}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| A | 100 | 280 | 55 | 2.5 % |
| B | 200 | 420 | 110 | 4.0 % |
| C | 300 | 560 | 165 | 5.5 % |

**Given:** Specimen dimensions: $D_0 = 38$ mm, $H_0 = 76$ mm.

**Note:** Assume the test is undrained during shearing, so $\varepsilon_v = 0$ for area correction.

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks: Calculate corrected deviator stress and effective principal stresses at failure.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Initial cross-sectional area**
$$A_0 = \frac{\pi D_0^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (38)^2}{4} = 1133.54 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 2: Corrected area (using $A = A_0 / (1 - \varepsilon_a)$):**

For Specimen A ($\varepsilon_{a,f} = 0.025$):
$$A_A = \frac{1133.54}{1 - 0.025} = \frac{1133.54}{0.975} = 1162.6 \text{ mm}^2$$

For Specimen B ($\varepsilon_{a,f} = 0.040$):
$$A_B = \frac{1133.54}{1 - 0.040} = \frac{1133.54}{0.960} = 1180.77 \text{ mm}^2$$

For Specimen C ($\varepsilon_{a,f} = 0.055$):
$$A_C = \frac{1133.54}{1 - 0.055} = \frac{1133.54}{0.945} = 1199.51 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 3: Deviator stress at failure ($\sigma_d = P / A$):**

Specimen A:
$$\sigma_{d,A} = \frac{280 \text{ N}}{1162.6 \text{ mm}^2} = 0.2408 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 240.8 \text{ kPa}$$

Specimen B:
$$\sigma_{d,B} = \frac{420}{1180.77} = 0.3557 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 355.7 \text{ kPa}$$

Specimen C:
$$\sigma_{d,C} = \frac{560}{1199.51} = 0.4669 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 466.9 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 4: Total and effective principal stresses at failure:**

| Specimen | $\sigma_1 = \sigma_3 + \sigma_d$ (kPa) | $\sigma_1' = \sigma_1 - u_f$ (kPa) | $\sigma_3' = \sigma_3 - u_f$ (kPa) |
|---|---|---|---|
| A | $100 + 240.8 = 340.8$ | $340.8 - 55 = 285.8$ | $100 - 55 = 45$ |
| B | $200 + 355.7 = 555.7$ | $555.7 - 110 = 445.7$ | $200 - 110 = 90$ |
| C | $300 + 466.9 = 766.9$ | $766.9 - 165 = 601.9$ | $300 - 165 = 135$ |

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks: Determine $c'$ and $\phi'$ using the $p'$–$q'$ method or Mohr's circle plot.**

**Model Solution (using $p'$–$q'$ method):**

**Step 1: Compute $p' = (\sigma_1' + \sigma_3')/2$ and $q = (\sigma_1' - \sigma_3')/2$:**

| Specimen | $p'$ (kPa) | $q$ (kPa) |
|---|---|---|
| A | $(285.8 + 45)/2 = 165.4$ | $(285.8 - 45)/2 = 120.4$ |
| B | $(445.7 + 90)/2 = 267.85$ | $(445.7 - 90)/2 = 177.85$ |
| C | $(601.9 + 135)/2 = 368.45$ | $(601.9 - 135)/2 = 233.45$ |

**Step 2: Linear regression of $q$ vs $p'$ (best-fit line):**

Using least-squares method (or graphical plot):

$$\text{Mean } p' = \frac{165.4 + 267.85 + 368.45}{3} = 267.23 \text{ kPa}$$
$$\text{Mean } q = \frac{120.4 + 177.85 + 233.45}{3} = 177.23 \text{ kPa}$$

$$M = \frac{\sum (p' - \bar{p'})(q - \bar{q})}{\sum (p' - \bar{p'})^2}$$

For a quick two-point estimate (using Specimens A and C):
$$M \approx \frac{233.45 - 120.4}{368.45 - 165.4} = \frac{113.05}{203.05} = 0.5568$$

**Step 3: Calculate $\phi'$:**
$$\phi' = \sin^{-1}(0.5568) = \sin^{-1}(0.5568) \approx 33.85°$$

**Step 4: Calculate $c'$:**

Using Specimen A: $q = M \cdot p' + c' \cos \phi'$
$$120.4 = 0.5568 \times 165.4 + c' \cos(33.85°)$$
$$120.4 = 92.10 + c' \times 0.8303$$
$$c' = \frac{120.4 - 92.10}{0.8303} = \frac{28.30}{0.8303} \approx 34.08 \text{ kPa}$$

**Verification using Specimen C:**
$$q = 0.5568 \times 368.45 + 34.08 \times 0.8303 = 205.13 + 28.30 = 233.43 \text{ kPa} \approx 233.45 \text{ kPa} \checkmark$$

**Final Answer (Part b):**
- $\phi' \approx 33.85°$
- $c' \approx 34.1$ kPa

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting area correction** — students commonly use $A_0$ throughout. Marks deducted: 1–2 marks per specimen.
> 2. **Mixing up total and effective stresses** — using $\sigma_1$ instead of $\sigma_1'$ for plotting effective Mohr circles. This is a *critical* error in CU/CD calculations.
> 3. **Using total stresses in part (b)** when the question explicitly asks for $c'$ and $\phi'$ (effective parameters). Always check the question wording.
> 4. **Unit inconsistency** — convert N/mm² to kPa (×1000) or vice versa. Mixing units is a common 1-mark deduction.
> 5. **Failure to plot** — even with a $p'$–$q'$ calculation, KTU often awards 1–2 marks for the graphical plot of the failure envelope.

---

### **Question B (Choice 2)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the components of a triaxial test apparatus. List the different types of triaxial tests based on drainage conditions.

**(b)** In a triaxial Consolidated Drained (CD) test on a sand specimen, the following data were obtained. Compute the angle of internal friction $\phi'$ and the shear strength at a normal effective stress of 250 kPa.

| Specimen | $\sigma_3$ (kPa) | Deviator Stress at Failure $\sigma_{d,f}$ (kPa) |
|---|---|---|
| I | 100 | 380 |
| II | 200 | 760 |
| III | 300 | 1140 |

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks: Apparatus components and types of tests**

**Model Answer:**

**Components of the triaxial apparatus** (as shown in Section 4.1 schematic above):

1. **Loading frame** — provides the axial force via a screw jack or hydraulic ram.
2. **Proving ring (or load cell)** — measures the axial load $P$ applied to the specimen.
3. **Triaxial cell** — a transparent (Perspex) cylindrical chamber with a base, top, and sealing O-rings. Filled with water to apply the confining pressure.
4. **Loading ram (piston)** — transmits the axial load from the proving ring into the cell, into the top cap of the specimen.
5. **Specimen assembly** — the soil sample wrapped in a latex membrane, sandwiched between top and bottom porous stones, with filter paper side drains (for CU/CD).
6. **Cell pressure system** — an air-water pressure cylinder (or pressure controller) connected to a Bourdon gauge that applies the confining pressure $\sigma_3$.
7. **Pore pressure measuring system** — a null-type pore pressure indicator, transducer, or gauge connected to the specimen base.
8. **Volume change measuring device** — a burette (for CU/CD) connected to the drainage line to record volume change of the specimen.
9. **Deformation dial gauge or LVDT** — measures the axial displacement $\Delta H$ of the loading ram.
10. **Back pressure system** — used for saturating the specimen and for B-value measurement.

**Types of triaxial tests based on drainage conditions:**

| Test Type | Drainage During Consolidation | Drainage During Shearing | Pore Pressure Measured? | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Unconsolidated Undrained (UU)** | No | No | No | Short-term loading, $\phi_u = 0$ analysis |
| **Consolidated Undrained (CU)** | Yes | No | Yes | Effective stress analysis for clays |
| **Consolidated Drained (CD)** | Yes | Yes | Not needed (≈ 0) | Long-term analysis, sands, stiff clays |

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks: Calculate $\phi'$ and shear strength**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute effective principal stresses (CD test — drainage allowed, so $u \approx 0$):**

For a CD test, pore pressure is essentially zero because drainage is permitted throughout shearing. Therefore:
$$\sigma_1' = \sigma_1 = \sigma_3 + \sigma_d, \quad \sigma_3' = \sigma_3$$

| Specimen | $\sigma_3'$ (kPa) | $\sigma_d$ (kPa) | $\sigma_1'$ (kPa) |
|---|---|---|---|
| I | 100 | 380 | 480 |
| II | 200 | 760 | 960 |
| III | 300 | 1140 | 1440 |

**Step 2: Use Mohr-Coulomb formula to find $\phi'$ (assuming $c' = 0$ for sand):**

For a cohesionless soil, the Mohr-Coulomb criterion simplifies to:
$$\sin \phi' = \frac{\sigma_1' - \sigma_3'}{\sigma_1' + \sigma_3'}$$

**Specimen I:**
$$\sin \phi'_I = \frac{480 - 100}{480 + 100} = \frac{380}{580} = 0.6552 \Rightarrow \phi'_I = 40.93°$$

**Specimen II:**
$$\sin \phi'_{II} = \frac{960 - 200}{960 + 200} = \frac{760}{1160} = 0.6552 \Rightarrow \phi'_{II} = 40.93°$$

**Specimen III:**
$$\sin \phi'_{III} = \frac{1440 - 300}{1440 + 300} = \frac{1140}{1740} = 0.6552 \Rightarrow \phi'_{III} = 40.93°$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Excellent consistency!** All three specimens give the *same* $\phi'$, which is the hallmark of a well-performed triaxial test on a homogeneous sand.

**Step 3: Average $\phi'$:**
$$\phi' = \frac{40.93° + 40.93° + 40.93°}{3} = 40.93° \approx 40.9°$$

**Step 4: Compute shear strength at $\sigma' = 250$ kPa:**

Since $c' = 0$ for sand:
$$\tau_f = \sigma' \tan \phi' = 250 \times \tan(40.93°)$$
$$\tau_f = 250 \times 0.8667 = 216.7 \text{ kPa}$$

**Final Answer (Part b):**
- $\phi' \approx 40.93°$
- $\tau_f$ at $\sigma' = 250$ kPa $\approx 216.7$ kPa

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Exam-Day Checklist for Triaxial Shear Test**

- [x] **Always correct the area** for axial strain: $A = A_0 / (1 - \varepsilon_a)$ for undrained tests; $A = A_0 (1 - \varepsilon_v)/(1 - \varepsilon_a)$ for drained tests.
- [x] **Three specimens minimum** (per IS code) at three different confining pressures, typically 100, 200, 300 kPa.
- [x] **Membrane thickness** correction is generally *neglected* in KTU-level problems.
- [x] **UU test:** $\phi_u = 0$ for saturated clays; gives $c_u$ directly.
- [x] **CU test:** Measures $u$; gives $c', \phi'$ (effective) AND $c, \phi$ (total).
- [x] **CD test:** No excess pore pressure; gives $c', \phi'$ directly.
- [x] **Failure plane angle** from major principal stress: $\theta_f = 45° + \phi'/2$.
- [x] **B-value test** must show $B \geq 0.95$ for saturated specimens in CU/CD.
- [x] **Shear strength** at any effective normal stress: $\tau_f = c' + \sigma' \tan \phi'$.
- [x] **For a normally consolidated clay**, $c' \approx 0$ (effective cohesion intercept is zero).
- [x] **For sand**, $c' = 0$ always; only $\phi'$ is relevant.
- [x] **Loading rate:** 0.5–1 % strain/min for UU, 0.1 %/min for CU, very slow for CD.
- [x] **Failure criteria:** Peak deviator stress, or 15–20 % axial strain (whichever comes first).
- [x] **Plotting:** Always show units on axes, label Mohr circles, draw the failure envelope, and mark the angle $\phi$ and intercept $c$ on the diagram.
- [x] **Common KTU pitfall:** Using $\sigma_1$ instead of $\sigma_1'$ in the effective stress plot — this is a *fatal* error.
- [x] **Units consistency:** N → kN (÷1000), mm² → m² (×10⁻⁶), N/mm² → kPa (×1000).
- [x] **Saturated unit weight** of water: $\gamma_w = 9.81$ kN/m³ (or sometimes 10 kN/m³ for simplicity).
- [x] **Specimen L/D ratio** is always 2:1 as per IS code.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flexible Wall Permeability Test
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCEL507 - GEOTECHNICAL ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Flexible Wall Permeability Test

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Flexible Wall Permeability Test** (standardized under **ASTM D5084** and **IS 2720 Part 36**) is a laboratory procedure used to determine the **coefficient of permeability (hydraulic conductivity, *k*)** of saturated, fine-grained soils (silts and clays) and other materials of low permeability. The test is conducted using a **triaxial cell apparatus**, where the soil specimen is enclosed within a **flexible latex membrane**, and subjected to a controlled **confining (cell) pressure** while water is forced through the specimen under either a **constant head** or **falling head** condition.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology for KTU Exams:**
> - **Flexible Wall** → Refers to the **latex/rubber membrane** that confines the soil specimen, in contrast to the *rigid wall* permeameter used for granular soils.
> - **Back Pressure Saturation (B-value ≥ 0.95)** → Mandatory pre-test step to ensure the specimen is fully saturated (Skempton's B-parameter check).
> - **Effective Stress** → During permeation, the difference between **cell pressure (σ₃)** and **back pressure (u_b)** governs the effective stress on the soil skeleton.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have a piece of **wet, compacted clay** (like the clay used to make pottery). If you pour water on top, the water passes through *extremely slowly* — maybe a few millimeters per day. To measure this tiny flow in the lab, we cannot use a simple tube permeameter because:
1. The water would leak out through the **sides** of the specimen (since clay shrinks slightly away from rigid walls).
2. The **flow rate is so small** that we need very precise measurements.

**Intuitive Solution:** Wrap the clay in a **rubber balloon** (the flexible membrane) and place it inside a sealed pressure chamber (the triaxial cell). Now, pressurize the chamber from outside so the rubber membrane squeezes the clay tightly, **eliminating sidewall leakage**. Then push water through the clay using a small, controlled pressure difference, and precisely measure the tiny outflow using a **burette or volume change transducer**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "Flexible" Wall?** Rigid walls of a conventional permeameter can allow preferential flow along the soil-wall interface in clays. The flexible latex membrane, pushed against the soil by cell pressure, **prevents sidewall leakage** and allows testing of intact/remolded fine-grained soils under realistic in-situ effective stresses (often simulating field overburden pressure).

## 1.3 Applicable Soil Types & Significance

| Soil Type | Suitable Method | Typical *k* Range (cm/s) |
|---|---|---|
| Gravelly sand | Rigid wall permeameter | $10^{-1}$ to $10^{-3}$ |
| Fine sand / silt | Rigid or flexible wall | $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-6}$ |
| Silty clay | **Flexible wall (falling head)** | $10^{-6}$ to $10^{-8}$ |
| Clay / Bentonite | **Flexible wall (constant head with back pressure)** | $10^{-8}$ to $10^{-10}$ |
| Intact rock / shales | Flexible wall with high cell pressure | $<10^{-10}$ |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** The flexible wall test is **THE standard method for measuring permeability of clays**, making it a recurring topic in lab viva voce and Module 2 (Soil Permeability & Seepage) questions.

---

## 1.4 Governing Principle — Darcy's Law

The test fundamentally relies on **Henry Darcy's Law (1856)**, expressed in its generalized form:

$$v = k \cdot i$$

where $v$ is the discharge velocity, $k$ is the coefficient of permeability, and $i$ is the hydraulic gradient. In the flexible wall setup, the test apparatus is essentially a controlled **Darcy flow experiment under confining stress**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation

The flexible wall permeability test overcomes the principal limitations of the rigid wall permeameter when dealing with cohesive soils:

1. **Sidewall Leakage Prevention:** The confining cell pressure pushes the flexible membrane against the specimen, sealing the soil-membrane interface.
2. **Effective Stress Control:** By independently controlling the cell pressure ($\sigma_3$) and the pore water pressure (back pressure, $u_b$), the **effective stress** $\sigma' = \sigma_3 - u_b$ can be maintained at any desired value simulating field conditions.
3. **Saturation Assurance:** Back pressure saturation dissolves trapped air, achieving high B-values required for accurate measurement of low permeability.

## 2.2 Operational Steps (Logic Sequence)

The test proceeds in a strict logical sequence, each step being a prerequisite for the next:

- **Step 1 — Specimen Preparation:** Trim a cylindrical undisturbed sample (typically 38 mm to 100 mm diameter, length-to-diameter ratio ≥ 2:1) or compact a remolded specimen.
- **Step 2 — Membrane Encasing:** Place the specimen on the base pedestal, sandwiched between porous discs and filter papers. Encase in a latex membrane using membrane stretcher and O-rings at top and bottom.
- **Step 3 — Cell Assembly & Filling:** Place the top cap, seal the triaxial cell, and fill with de-aired water (cell fluid).
- **Step 4 — Back Pressure Saturation:** Apply simultaneous cell pressure and back pressure (in steps) until **Skempton's B-value ≥ 0.95** is achieved.
- **Step 5 — Consolidation (Optional but recommended):** Apply the desired effective stress and allow the specimen to consolidate.
- **Step 6 — Permeation:** Apply a hydraulic gradient (constant or falling head) and measure the flow of water through the specimen.
- **Step 7 — Permeability Calculation:** Compute *k* using the appropriate formula based on the flow method.

## 2.3 The Two Flow Methods

### Method A: Constant Head Method
A constant hydraulic head differential ($\Delta h$) is maintained across the specimen, and the steady-state outflow volume ($Q$) over time ($t$) is measured.

$$k = \frac{Q \cdot L}{A \cdot \Delta h \cdot t}$$

When the head differential is expressed as a **pressure difference** $\Delta u$ (in kPa), the formula becomes:

$$k = \frac{Q \cdot L \cdot \gamma_w}{A \cdot \Delta u \cdot t}$$

where $\gamma_w$ is the unit weight of water (= $9.81$ kN/m³).

### Method B: Falling Head Method
Water flows from a calibrated **standpipe (burette)** of cross-sectional area *a* through the specimen. The head drops from initial $h_1$ to final $h_2$ over time interval $t$.

$$k = \frac{a \cdot L}{A \cdot t} \cdot \ln\left(\frac{h_1}{h_2}\right)$$

$$k = \frac{2.303 \cdot a \cdot L}{A \cdot t} \cdot \log_{10}\left(\frac{h_1}{h_2}\right)$$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Quantity | Symbol | Formula | Units (SI) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Discharge velocity | $v$ | $v = Q / (A \cdot t)$ | m/s |
| Hydraulic gradient | $i$ | $i = \Delta h / L$ | dimensionless |
| **Darcy's Law** | $k$ | $k = v / i$ | m/s or cm/s |
| **Constant Head *k*** | $k$ | $k = Q \cdot L / (A \cdot \Delta h \cdot t)$ | cm/s |
| **Constant Head *k* (pressure form)** | $k$ | $k = Q \cdot L \cdot \gamma_w / (A \cdot \Delta u \cdot t)$ | cm/s |
| **Falling Head *k*** | $k$ | $k = 2.303 \cdot a \cdot L / (A \cdot t) \cdot \log(h_1/h_2)$ | cm/s |
| Effective stress | $\sigma'$ | $\sigma' = \sigma_3 - u_b$ | kPa |
| Skempton's B-parameter | $B$ | $B = \Delta u / \Delta \sigma_3$ | dimensionless |
| Volume of specimen | $V$ | $V = A \cdot L = (\pi/4) \cdot D^2 \cdot L$ | cm³ |
| Cross-sectional area | $A$ | $A = (\pi/4) \cdot D^2$ | cm² |
| Head conversion (pressure to head) | $h$ | $h = u / \gamma_w$ | m |

> [!CAUTION]
> **Unit Conversion Trap for KTU Exams:** Permeability is conventionally reported in **cm/s** in geotechnical practice. Many students lose marks by writing m/s without conversion. Always convert: $1$ m/s $= 100$ cm/s.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Landfill Liners:** Bentonite-clay liners require *k* ≤ $10^{-9}$ m/s; only flexible wall tests can verify such low permeability reliably.
- **Earthen Dams & Embankments:** Core walls and cutoff diaphragms use clayey soils; their permeability governs seepage and stability.
- **Contaminant Transport Modeling:** Designing clay barriers for hazardous waste containment.
- **Consolidation Predictions:** The coefficient of consolidation $c_v$ is related to *k* by $c_v = (k \cdot M) / \gamma_w$, where *M* is the constrained modulus.
- **Slurry Walls & Cut-off Trenches:** Quality control testing of low-permeability in-situ barriers.
- **Underground Storage (Nuclear Waste, CO₂):** Measurement of permeability of intact shales and mudstones.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Falling Head Permeability Formula

The falling head formula is the most commonly asked derivation in KTU examinations. We derive it from the fundamental Darcy's Law applied to a variable head system.

### 3.1.1 Setup & Variables

Consider a saturated soil specimen of length $L$ and cross-sectional area $A$. Water flows from a vertical standpipe of cross-sectional area $a$ (where $a \ll A$) through the specimen. At time $t = 0$, the head in the standpipe is $h_1$. At time $t = t$, the head is $h_2 < h_1$.

### 3.1.2 Differential Flow Element

In a small time interval $dt$, the head drops by an infinitesimal amount $dh$. The volume of water leaving the standpipe (and passing through the specimen) is:

$$dQ = -a \cdot dh$$

The **negative sign** appears because as head $h$ decreases, $dh$ is negative, while $dQ$ (volume flowing out) is positive. This equation enforces physical consistency.

By Darcy's Law, the rate of flow through the specimen at any instant when the head is $h$ is:

$$\frac{dQ}{dt} = k \cdot i \cdot A = k \cdot \frac{h}{L} \cdot A$$

### 3.1.3 Equating and Separating Variables

Setting the two expressions equal (the volume lost from the standpipe equals the volume passing through the soil):

$$-a \cdot dh = k \cdot \frac{h}{L} \cdot A \cdot dt$$

Rearranging to separate variables $h$ and $t$:

$$\frac{dh}{h} = -\frac{k \cdot A}{a \cdot L} \cdot dt$$

### 3.1.4 Integration Across the Time Interval

Integrate from $h_1$ at $t = 0$ to $h_2$ at $t = t$:

$$\int_{h_1}^{h_2} \frac{dh}{h} = -\frac{k \cdot A}{a \cdot L} \cdot \int_{0}^{t} dt$$

Evaluating both integrals:

$$\ln(h_2) - \ln(h_1) = -\frac{k \cdot A}{a \cdot L} \cdot t$$

$$\ln\left(\frac{h_2}{h_1}\right) = -\frac{k \cdot A \cdot t}{a \cdot L}$$

### 3.1.5 Solving for *k*

Multiplying both sides by $-1$:

$$\ln\left(\frac{h_1}{h_2}\right) = \frac{k \cdot A \cdot t}{a \cdot L}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{k = \frac{a \cdot L}{A \cdot t} \cdot \ln\left(\frac{h_1}{h_2}\right) = \frac{2.303 \cdot a \cdot L}{A \cdot t} \cdot \log_{10}\left(\frac{h_1}{h_2}\right)}$$

This is the **standard falling head permeability formula** valid for flexible wall (triaxial) permeameters.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Exam Style)

> **Problem Statement:**
> A flexible wall permeability test is conducted on a remolded clay specimen. Given:
> - Specimen diameter, $D = 38$ mm
> - Specimen length, $L = 76$ mm
> - Standpipe (burette) diameter, $d = 4$ mm
> - Initial head, $h_1 = 1200$ mm
> - Final head, $h_2 = 800$ mm
> - Time interval, $t = 600$ seconds
> - Cell pressure, $\sigma_3 = 400$ kPa
> - Back pressure, $u_b = 300$ kPa
>
> **Calculate:**
> (a) The coefficient of permeability *k* in cm/s.
> (b) The effective confining stress on the specimen.

### 3.2.1 Step 1: Compute Cross-Sectional Areas

Cross-sectional area of specimen:

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot D^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (3.8)^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot 14.44 = 11.341 \text{ cm}^2$$

Cross-sectional area of standpipe:

$$a = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot d^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (0.4)^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot 0.16 = 0.1257 \text{ cm}^2$$

### 3.2.2 Step 2: Apply the Falling Head Formula

We use $L = 76$ mm $= 7.6$ cm.

$$k = \frac{2.303 \cdot a \cdot L}{A \cdot t} \cdot \log_{10}\left(\frac{h_1}{h_2}\right)$$

Compute the logarithmic term:

$$\log_{10}\left(\frac{h_1}{h_2}\right) = \log_{10}\left(\frac{1200}{800}\right) = \log_{10}(1.5) = 0.1761$$

Substitute all values:

$$k = \frac{2.303 \cdot 0.1257 \cdot 7.6}{11.341 \cdot 600} \cdot 0.1761$$

Numerator: $2.303 \cdot 0.1257 \cdot 7.6 = 2.2013$ (with units: cm³)

Denominator: $11.341 \cdot 600 = 6804.6$ (with units: cm²·s)

$$k = \frac{2.2013}{6804.6} \cdot 0.1761 = 3.234 \times 10^{-4} \cdot 0.1761$$

$$k = 5.695 \times 10^{-5} \text{ cm/s}$$

### 3.2.3 Step 3: Effective Stress Calculation

$$\sigma' = \sigma_3 - u_b = 400 - 300 = 100 \text{ kPa}$$

### 3.2.4 Final Answer

> The permeability of the clay specimen is **$k = 5.7 \times 10^{-5}$ cm/s** at an **effective confining stress of 100 kPa**. This value is typical of a **silty clay** soil (per the table in Section 1.3).

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Factors Affecting *k* in Flexible Wall Test

| Factor | Effect on *k* | Engineering Implication |
|---|---|---|
| Increase in effective confining stress $\sigma'$ | **Decreases *k*** (void ratio reduces) | Deeper clay layers are less permeable |
| Increase in back pressure $u_b$ | Indirect: ensures saturation → more accurate *k* | Without back pressure, measured *k* is falsely high due to air bubbles |
| Use of de-aired water | **Reduces errors** | Mandatory for *k* < $10^{-7}$ cm/s |
| Filter paper clogging | **Reduces apparent *k*** | Replace filter paper; use high-air-entry discs for clays |
| Temperature increase | **Increases *k*** (~2% per °C) | Standardize to 20°C: $k_{20} = k_T \cdot \mu_T / \mu_{20}$ |
| Hydraulic gradient too high | May cause **piping/erosion** | Keep $i \leq 30$ for clays to prevent particle migration |
| Incomplete saturation (low B) | **Overestimates *k*** | Always achieve $B \geq 0.95$ before permeation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Temperature Correction Formula for KTU Lab Reports:**
> $$k_{20°C} = k_T \cdot \frac{\mu_T}{\mu_{20}}$$
> where $\mu_T$ and $\mu_{20}$ are the dynamic viscosities of water at test temperature and 20°C respectively.

---

## 3.4 Step-by-Step Numerical: Constant Head Variant

To reinforce the formulas, here is a compact constant head calculation:

> **Given:** Specimen: $D = 50$ mm, $L = 100$ mm. Outflow $Q = 12$ cm³ collected in $t = 600$ s under a constant head difference $\Delta h = 50$ cm. Compute *k*.

Step 1: Area:
$$A = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (5)^2 = 19.635 \text{ cm}^2$$

Step 2: Apply formula $k = Q \cdot L / (A \cdot \Delta h \cdot t)$:

$$k = \frac{12 \cdot 10}{19.635 \cdot 50 \cdot 600} = \frac{120}{589050} = 2.037 \times 10^{-4} \text{ cm/s}$$

This represents a **silt** with low permeability.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Flexible Wall Permeability Test Setup

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional interaction** between the apparatus components, fluid circuits, and measurement systems. (Following the V10 safeguard: all node IDs are alphanumeric, no reserved keywords, and all labels are raw uppercase text.)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    node1["SOIL SPECIMEN\nLATEX MEMBRANE ENCASED\nINSIDE TRIAXIAL CELL"]:::core
    node2["CELL PRESSURE SYSTEM\nCOMPRESSED AIR OR WATER\nAPPLIES CONFINING STRESS SIGMA3"]:::pressure
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: Define the Flexible Wall Permeability Test.
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

> The Flexible Wall Permeability Test is a laboratory test conducted using a triaxial cell apparatus to determine the coefficient of permeability (hydraulic conductivity, *k*) of saturated fine-grained soils such as silts and clays. In this test, a cylindrical soil specimen is enclosed within a flexible latex membrane, and a confining (cell) pressure is applied to the membrane via pressurized cell fluid. Water is then allowed to flow through the specimen under a controlled hydraulic gradient (constant head or falling head), and the permeability is calculated using Darcy's Law. The flexible wall eliminates sidewall leakage, which is a major source of error in rigid wall permeameters when testing cohesive soils.

### Question A2: Why is back pressure saturation required before conducting the flexible wall permeability test?
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Model Answer:**

> Back pressure saturation is required to achieve full saturation of the soil specimen by dissolving any entrapped air within the pore space. Air bubbles, if present, would block the flow paths and cause the measured permeability to be **falsely higher** than the true value. The procedure involves simultaneously increasing both the cell pressure and the pore water pressure (back pressure) in small increments, maintaining a constant effective stress. Saturation is verified by measuring **Skempton's B-value** (ratio of change in pore pressure to change in cell pressure); a value of **B ≥ 0.95** is considered indicative of adequate saturation. This step is mandatory for accurate determination of permeability, especially in clays where *k* is very low ($10^{-8}$ to $10^{-10}$ cm/s).

---

## 5.2 Part B Question Choice A (14 Marks)

### Question A: Flexible Wall Permeability Test Procedure and Calculations

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | Module 2 | 14 Marks]**

**(a)** With a neat flowchart, describe the procedure for conducting a flexible wall permeability test on a remolded clay specimen. Highlight the significance of back pressure saturation and the B-value check. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A flexible wall permeability test is conducted on a clay specimen of 70 mm diameter and 140 mm length, using the falling head method. The standpipe diameter is 5 mm. The head drops from 1500 mm to 900 mm in 1800 seconds. The cell pressure is 500 kPa and the back pressure is 350 kPa. Calculate the coefficient of permeability of the clay and the effective confining stress. **[7 Marks]**

### Model Solution to (a) — Procedure

1. **Specimen Preparation (1 Mark):** Trim or compact a cylindrical clay specimen of diameter 70 mm and length approximately 140 mm (L/D ratio = 2). Measure dimensions and weight to compute initial water content, void ratio, and degree of saturation.

2. **Membrane Encasing (1 Mark):** Place the specimen on the base pedestal of the triaxial apparatus. Position porous stones and filter papers at top and bottom. Use a membrane stretcher to encase the specimen in a latex membrane, sealing with O-rings at the top cap and base pedestal to prevent leakage.

3. **Cell Assembly (1 Mark):** Place the top cap, position the cell body, and fill the triaxial cell with de-aired water (cell fluid) to transmit the confining pressure. Connect all hydraulic lines (cell pressure, back pressure, inflow, outflow) and de-air them thoroughly.

4. **Back Pressure Saturation (2 Marks):** Apply cell pressure and back pressure simultaneously in incremental steps (typically 50 kPa increments), maintaining a constant effective stress of about 10–20 kPa. After each increment, wait for pressure equalization. Measure the B-value by closing the drainage valve, increasing cell pressure by a small increment $\Delta\sigma_3$, and observing the resulting change in pore pressure $\Delta u$. A B-value = $\Delta u / \Delta\sigma_3 \geq 0.95$ confirms adequate saturation. The significance is that without full saturation, entrapped air artificially increases the measured permeability.

5. **Consolidation (0.5 Mark):** Apply the desired effective stress ($\sigma' = \sigma_3 - u_b$) and allow the specimen to consolidate fully, monitoring the volume change.

6. **Permeation (1 Mark):** Apply the chosen hydraulic head (constant or falling) and record the volume of outflow at regular time intervals. For falling head, monitor the drop in the standpipe water level.

7. **Calculation and Reporting (0.5 Mark):** Compute *k* using Darcy's Law formula and report the result in cm/s with the corresponding effective stress and temperature correction if applicable.

### Model Solution to (b) — Numerical

**Given Data:**
- Specimen diameter $D = 70$ mm $= 7.0$ cm
- Specimen length $L = 140$ mm $= 14.0$ cm
- Standpipe diameter $d = 5$ mm $= 0.5$ cm
- Initial head $h_1 = 1500$ mm $= 150$ cm
- Final head $h_2 = 900$ mm $= 90$ cm
- Time $t = 1800$ s
- Cell pressure $\sigma_3 = 500$ kPa
- Back pressure $u_b = 350$ kPa

**Step 1: Compute Cross-Sectional Areas**

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot D^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (7.0)^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot 49 = 38.485 \text{ cm}^2$$

$$a = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot d^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (0.5)^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot 0.25 = 0.1963 \text{ cm}^2$$

**Step 2: Compute the Logarithmic Term**

$$\log_{10}\left(\frac{h_1}{h_2}\right) = \log_{10}\left(\frac{150}{90}\right) = \log_{10}(1.6667) = 0.2218$$

**Step 3: Apply the Falling Head Formula**

$$k = \frac{2.303 \cdot a \cdot L}{A \cdot t} \cdot \log_{10}\left(\frac{h_1}{h_2}\right)$$

$$k = \frac{2.303 \cdot 0.1963 \cdot 14.0}{38.485 \cdot 1800} \cdot 0.2218$$

Numerator: $2.303 \cdot 0.1963 \cdot 14.0 = 6.327$ (cm³)

Denominator: $38.485 \cdot 1800 = 69{,}273$ (cm²·s)

Ratio: $6.327 / 69{,}273 = 9.133 \times 10^{-5}$

$$k = 9.133 \times 10^{-5} \cdot 0.2218 = 2.026 \times 10^{-5} \text{ cm/s}$$

**Step 4: Effective Confining Stress**

$$\sigma' = \sigma_3 - u_b = 500 - 350 = 150 \text{ kPa}$$

**Final Answer:**
- Coefficient of permeability: $k = 2.03 \times 10^{-5}$ cm/s
- Effective confining stress: $\sigma' = 150$ kPa

> **Interpretation:** This permeability value falls in the range typical of a **silty clay** to **clayey silt**, suitable for applications such as landfill covers and earthen dam cores.

---

## 5.3 Part B Question Choice B (14 Marks)

### Question B: Comparison of Methods and Constant Head Variant

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 | Module 2 | 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Compare the rigid wall and flexible wall permeability tests. Under what circumstances is the flexible wall test preferred? Discuss the role of effective stress in flexible wall testing. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** In a constant head flexible wall permeability test, a clay specimen of diameter 50 mm and length 100 mm is subjected to a cell pressure of 400 kPa and a back pressure of 250 kPa. A constant head difference equivalent to 60 cm of water is applied. The volume of outflow collected in 24 hours is 8 cm³. Calculate the coefficient of permeability in cm/s. What is the effective stress on the specimen? **[7 Marks]**

### Model Solution to (a) — Comparative Analysis

| Aspect | Rigid Wall Permeameter | Flexible Wall Permeameter |
|---|---|---|
| Wall material | Metal or transparent acrylic (rigid) | Latex/rubber membrane (flexible) |
| Suitable soils | Granular (sands, gravels) | Cohesive (silts, clays, shales) |
| Sidewall leakage | Significant in clays (preferential flow path) | Eliminated by cell pressure squeezing membrane against soil |
| Effective stress control | Not possible | Full control via $\sigma' = \sigma_3 - u_b$ |
| Back pressure saturation | Generally not done | Mandatory |
| Typical *k* range | $10^{-1}$ to $10^{-6}$ cm/s | $10^{-6}$ to $10^{-10}$ cm/s |
| Apparatus cost | Low | High (requires triaxial setup) |
| Standards | IS 2720 Part 17, ASTM D2434 | IS 2720 Part 36, ASTM D5084 |

**Circumstances Favoring Flexible Wall (2 Marks):**
- When the soil is **fine-grained** (clay, silt) with low permeability.
- When the specimen is **undisturbed** and its in-situ structure must be preserved.
- When **effective stress** conditions simulating field overburden pressure are required.
- When **back pressure saturation** is necessary to obtain reliable low permeability values.

**Role of Effective Stress (2 Marks):**
- The **effective stress principle** states that soil behavior (including permeability) is governed by the interparticle stresses, not the total stress. In the flexible wall apparatus, the effective stress $\sigma' = \sigma_3 - u_b$ can be precisely controlled by independently regulating the cell pressure and the back pressure. This allows simulation of the actual overburden stress that the soil experiences in the field. Furthermore, by varying the effective stress, the test can quantify the **stress-dependency of permeability**, which is critical for deep clay layers, shales, and reservoir engineering applications.

### Model Solution to (b) — Constant Head Numerical

**Given Data:**
- $D = 50$ mm $= 5.0$ cm, $L = 100$ mm $= 10.0$ cm
- $\Delta h = 60$ cm
- $Q = 8$ cm³ in $t = 24$ hours $= 24 \times 3600 = 86{,}400$ s
- $\sigma_3 = 400$ kPa, $u_b = 250$ kPa

**Step 1: Cross-Sectional Area**

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (5.0)^2 = 19.635 \text{ cm}^2$$

**Step 2: Apply Constant Head Formula**

$$k = \frac{Q \cdot L}{A \cdot \Delta h \cdot t}$$

$$k = \frac{8 \cdot 10.0}{19.635 \cdot 60 \cdot 86{,}400}$$

Numerator: $8 \cdot 10.0 = 80$ (cm³·cm)

Denominator: $19.635 \cdot 60 \cdot 86{,}400 = 101{,}788{,}800$ (cm²·cm·s)

$$k = \frac{80}{101{,}788{,}800} = 7.86 \times 10^{-7} \text{ cm/s}$$

**Step 3: Effective Stress**

$$\sigma' = \sigma_3 - u_b = 400 - 250 = 150 \text{ kPa}$$

**Final Answer:**
- Coefficient of permeability: $k = 7.86 \times 10^{-7}$ cm/s (indicative of a **clay** soil)
- Effective confining stress: $\sigma' = 150$ kPa

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Time Unit Errors:** Many students mistakenly use $t = 24$ (hours) directly without converting to seconds. This produces a permeability value **86,400 times smaller** than the correct value, a catastrophic error.
> 2. **Mixing up *h* and *L*:** In the formula, *L* is the specimen length and *h* is the head difference; they are not interchangeable.
> 3. **Sign of effective stress:** Always compute $\sigma' = \sigma_3 - u_b$ (cell pressure minus back pressure), never the reverse. If the value comes out negative, your back pressure exceeds the cell pressure — a serious experimental setup error.
> 4. **Area of standpipe in falling head:** Use the **standpipe** (burette) area for *a*, not the specimen area *A*. Confusing these is one of the most common mistakes in lab reports.
> 5. **Neglecting B-value check:** Writing about the test procedure without mentioning the **B-value ≥ 0.95 saturation check** will cost at least 1–2 marks in Part B answers.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Final Revision — Must-Know Points Before KTU Exam**

- ✅ The **Flexible Wall Permeability Test** uses a **triaxial cell** with a **latex membrane** to measure permeability of **clayey soils** under controlled **effective stress**.
- ✅ The test is governed by **Darcy's Law**: $v = k \cdot i$.
- ✅ **Two methods:** **Constant Head** ($k = QL/Aht$) and **Falling Head** ($k = 2.303 \cdot aL/At \cdot \log(h_1/h_2)$).
- ✅ **Back pressure saturation** is **mandatory** to achieve **B-value ≥ 0.95** for accurate low-permeability measurement.
- ✅ **Effective stress** $\sigma' = \sigma_3 - u_b$ controls the soil skeleton behavior; must be reported alongside *k*.
- ✅ **Filter paper clogging** and **entrapped air** are the two biggest sources of error in this test.
- ✅ The flexible wall eliminates **sidewall leakage**, which is the principal advantage over rigid wall permeameters for fine-grained soils.
- ✅ Permeability is reported in **cm/s**; remember the conversion $1$ m/s $= 100$ cm/s.
- ✅ **ASTM D5084** and **IS 2720 Part 36** are the governing standards.
- ✅ Typical *k* values: clay $10^{-8}$ to $10^{-10}$ cm/s, silt $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-7}$ cm/s.
- ✅ The test has applications in **landfill liners, earth dams, slurry walls, and contaminant transport studies**.
- ✅ For falling head, the **standpipe (burette) area** *a* is much smaller than the specimen area *A* (a ≪ A) for accuracy.
- ✅ Plot **flow volume vs. time** to verify **steady-state** conditions before accepting the *k* value.
- ✅ Always apply a **temperature correction** to 20°C for standardized reporting: $k_{20} = k_T \cdot \mu_T / \mu_{20}$.
- ✅ Keep the **hydraulic gradient** low ($i \leq 30$ for clays) to avoid piping or particle migration during the test.
- ✅ The **latex membrane** itself has a permeability contribution (especially for very low *k* clays) that should be checked against a blank membrane test for high-precision work.

topics.title: Determination of Relative Density of Cohesionless Soil
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCEL507 - GEOTECHNICAL ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of Relative Density of Cohesionless Soil

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Relative Density (Dr)**, also termed the *Density Index* (Id) or *Degree of Compaction*, is a fundamental index property of **cohesionless (granular) soils** (sands and gravels) that quantifies the relative compactness of an in-situ soil mass compared to its loosest and densest possible states. It is mathematically expressed as the ratio of the difference between the void ratios in the loosest state and the natural (in-situ) state to the difference between the void ratios in the loosest and densest states.

In the context of **IS 2720 (Part 14) – 1983 (Reaffirmed 2020)**, which is the governing Bureau of Indian Standard for the laboratory determination of relative density of cohesionless soils, the test is exclusively applicable to **free-draining, non-cohesive granular materials** that do not develop significant interparticle cohesion when saturated.

The governing mathematical relationship is universally given as:

$$D_r = \dfrac{e_{max} - e}{e_{max} - e_{min}} = \dfrac{\gamma_{d,max}(\gamma_d - \gamma_{d,min})}{\gamma_d(\gamma_{d,max} - \gamma_{d,min})}$$

where:
- $e_{max}$ = **Void ratio of the soil in its loosest state**
- $e_{min}$ = **Void ratio of the soil in its densest state**
- $e$ = **In-situ (natural) void ratio of the soil**
- $\gamma_{d,max}$ = **Maximum dry unit weight (densest state)**
- $\gamma_{d,min}$ = **Minimum dry unit weight (loosest state)**
- $\gamma_d$ = **Natural (in-situ) dry unit weight**

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Imagine a packed suitcase filled with marbles.** When you first pour marbles in loosely, there are large gaps between them (loosest state). When you shake the suitcase vigorously, the marbles rearrange and slide into the gaps, packing much tighter (densest state). The actual packing in your suitcase is somewhere in between. **Relative Density tells you "where" in this spectrum your current packing lies** — like a percentage indicator from "completely loose" (0%) to "as tight as physically possible" (100%).

This parameter is **massively important** in geotechnical engineering because:
- The **shear strength** of sands (friction angle $\phi$) depends directly on Dr, not on water content.
- **Liquefaction susceptibility** under earthquakes is governed almost entirely by relative density.
- **Settlement calculations** for granular soils rely on Dr for accurate predictions.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (KTU Highlight)

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Engineering Constants (per IS 2720 Part 14):**
> - **Standard Mould Volume:** $V = 1000 \text{ cm}^3$ (or 2830 cm³ for larger moulds)
> - **Standard Mould Diameter:** $D = 100 \text{ mm}$ (or 150 mm)
> - **Standard Tamping Mass for emax:** Nil — soil is poured via funnel/hand
> - **Tamping Frequency for emin:** $3600 \text{ vibrations/minute}$ for ~8–12 minutes
> - **Density Index Classification Range:** Very Loose (0–15%), Loose (15–35%), Medium Dense (35–65%), Dense (65–85%), Very Dense (85–100%)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Why Relative Density and Not Compaction?

In **cohesive soils** (clays, silts), compaction is the key engineering parameter because particle rearrangement is resisted by electrochemical bonding and water films. However, in **cohesionless soils** (sands, gravels):

- The soil does **not** retain a "compacted state" once water content changes.
- The frictional behavior is governed by **particle-to-particle contact density**.
- A sand at its **densest possible state** exhibits almost **double** the shear strength of the same sand in its loosest state.

Therefore, geotechnical engineers require a parameter that describes the **state of packing** independently of water content. **Relative Density (Dr)** is that parameter.

## 2.2 Three Critical Void Ratios — The Conceptual Trinity

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every granular soil has **three reference void ratios** that define its behavior:
>
> 1. **$e_{max}$ (Maximum Void Ratio / Loosest State):** Achieved by carefully pouring dry soil through a funnel with zero drop height. Particles are in a *cascading, low-energy configuration*.
> 2. **$e_{min}$ (Minimum Void Ratio / Densest State):** Achieved by applying high-frequency vibration and surcharge load. Particles are in a *kinetically optimized, tightly interlocked configuration*.
> 3. **$e$ (In-situ / Natural Void Ratio):** The void ratio of the soil as it exists in the ground or as compacted in the field.

The **numerical range of $D_r$** is always **$0 \le D_r \le 1$** (or 0% to 100% in percentage form).

## 2.3 Apparatus Required (Per IS 2720 Part 14)

| S.No. | Apparatus | Specification | Purpose |
|-------|-----------|---------------|---------|
| 1 | Cylindrical Mould | 1000 cm³ volume, 100 mm dia, detachable base plate | Container for soil sample |
| 2 | Vibrating Table | 3600 vibrations/min @ 0.5 mm amplitude | Achieves $e_{min}$ |
| 3 | Surcharge Weight | 140 g per 100 cm² area | Applies vertical stress during vibration |
| 4 | Pouring Funnel / Scoop | 12 mm spout opening | Achieves $e_{max}$ |
| 5 | Calibration Block | Precisely machined to known volume | Volume calibration of mould |
| 6 | Weighing Balance | 0.1 g accuracy | Mass measurement |
| 7 | Straight Edge | Steel, sharp-edged | Levelling soil surface |
| 8 | Tamper (optional) | Wooden or metal rod | For placing soil layers |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **Master these 6 equations — they cover 95% of KTU exam questions on this topic.**

| Equation | Formula | Variables Explained | Application |
|----------|---------|---------------------|-------------|
| **Relative Density (void ratio form)** | $D_r = \dfrac{e_{max} - e}{e_{max} - e_{min}}$ | $e_{max}, e, e_{min}$ are dimensionless | **Primary definition** |
| **Relative Density (density form)** | $D_r = \dfrac{\gamma_{d,max}(\gamma_d - \gamma_{d,min})}{\gamma_d(\gamma_{d,max} - \gamma_{d,min})}$ | All $\gamma_d$ in kN/m³ or g/cc | **Used in field tests** |
| **Dry Unit Weight** | $\gamma_d = \dfrac{\gamma}{1 + w}$ | $\gamma$ = bulk unit weight, $w$ = water content | **In-situ sample conversion** |
| **Void Ratio from Dry Density** | $e = \dfrac{G_s \cdot \gamma_w}{\gamma_d} - 1$ | $G_s$ = Specific Gravity, $\gamma_w$ = 9.81 kN/m³ | **Computed e from density** |
| **Relative Compaction** | $R_c = \dfrac{\gamma_d}{\gamma_{d,max}} \times 100$ | Percentage | **Field QC for fills** |
| **Density Index (Id)** | $I_D = D_r = \dfrac{e_{max} - e}{e_{max} - e_{min}}$ | Synonymous with Dr | **Alternate notation** |

> [!CAUTION]
> **In LaTeX/Markdown tables, never use bare vertical bars `\|`.** Use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute values (e.g., write $\vert G_s \vert$ or use $\mid G_s \mid$ to avoid table rendering issues).

## 2.5 Engineering Significance — Why This Lab Test Matters

| Engineering Application | Role of Relative Density |
|-------------------------|--------------------------|
| **Earthquake Engineering** | Liquefaction resistance of sands is directly tied to Dr > 60% |
| **Shallow Foundations** | Bearing capacity of sands: $q_u \propto \tan^2(45 + \phi'/2)$, where $\phi' = f(Dr)$ |
| **Embankment Design** | Compaction QC for sand fills uses relative compaction = 95–100% of $\gamma_{d,max}$ |
| **Penetration Testing** | SPT N-values and CPT $q_c$ are correlated to Dr via empirical charts |
| **Pavement Subgrade** | Granular sub-base acceptance is based on % relative compaction |
| **Hydraulic Structures** | Filter design and seepage control depend on in-situ Dr |

---

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Mathematical Foundation — Derivation of the Master Equation

The relative density equation is **empirically derived from the principle of normalized state**. We begin by defining a linear normalization function $\phi(e)$ that maps any void ratio to a value between 0 and 1:

### Step 1: Define the State Function
For a granular soil, the "state" of packing can be characterized by the void ratio. The loosest state corresponds to $e = e_{max}$ and the densest to $e = e_{min}$.

### Step 2: Construct the Linear Interpolation
The fraction of the void ratio range consumed by the current state (going from loosest to densest) is:

$$\text{Fraction} = \dfrac{e_{max} - e}{e_{max} - e_{min}}$$

### Step 3: Establish the Physical Meaning
- If $e = e_{max}$: $\text{Fraction} = \dfrac{e_{max} - e_{max}}{e_{max} - e_{min}} = 0$ → Soil is **loosest** (Dr = 0)
- If $e = e_{min}$: $\text{Fraction} = \dfrac{e_{max} - e_{min}}{e_{max} - e_{min}} = 1$ → Soil is **densest** (Dr = 1)
- This is the **definition of Relative Density:**

$$\boxed{D_r = \dfrac{e_{max} - e}{e_{max} - e_{min}}}$$

### Step 4: Derive the Density Form
Starting from $e = \dfrac{G_s \cdot \gamma_w}{\gamma_d} - 1$, we substitute into the Dr equation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_r &= \dfrac{e_{max} - e}{e_{max} - e_{min}} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{\left(\dfrac{G_s \cdot \gamma_w}{\gamma_{d,min}} - 1\right) - \left(\dfrac{G_s \cdot \gamma_w}{\gamma_d} - 1\right)}{\left(\dfrac{G_s \cdot \gamma_w}{\gamma_{d,min}} - 1\right) - \left(\dfrac{G_s \cdot \gamma_w}{\gamma_{d,max}} - 1\right)} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{\dfrac{G_s \cdot \gamma_w}{\gamma_{d,min}} - \dfrac{G_s \cdot \gamma_w}{\gamma_d}}{\dfrac{G_s \cdot \gamma_w}{\gamma_{d,min}} - \dfrac{G_s \cdot \gamma_w}{\gamma_{d,max}}} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{\dfrac{1}{\gamma_{d,min}} - \dfrac{1}{\gamma_d}}{\dfrac{1}{\gamma_{d,min}} - \dfrac{1}{\gamma_{d,max}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5: Algebraic Simplification
Multiplying numerator and denominator by $\gamma_{d,max} \cdot \gamma_d \cdot \gamma_{d,min}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_r &= \dfrac{\gamma_{d,max} \cdot \gamma_d - \gamma_{d,max} \cdot \gamma_{d,min}}{\gamma_{d,max} \cdot \gamma_d - \gamma_d \cdot \gamma_{d,min}} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{\gamma_{d,max}(\gamma_d - \gamma_{d,min})}{\gamma_d(\gamma_{d,max} - \gamma_{d,min})}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This density form is essential for KTU problems** where the in-situ void ratio is unknown but the natural dry unit weight is determined from a sand-cone or core-cutter test.

## 3.2 Complete Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard Problem)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:**
> A cohesionless soil has the following laboratory test data:
> - Minimum dry density (loosest state): $\gamma_{d,min} = 1.46 \text{ g/cc}$
> - Maximum dry density (densest state): $\gamma_{d,max} = 1.86 \text{ g/cc}$
> - Natural dry density (in-situ): $\gamma_d = 1.68 \text{ g/cc}$
> - Specific gravity of soil solids: $G_s = 2.65$
>
> **Determine:**
> (a) Relative density $D_r$ in percentage
> (b) In-situ void ratio $e$
> (c) The relative density classification of the deposit

### Step 1: Compute In-situ Void Ratio (Part b)
Using $e = \dfrac{G_s \cdot \gamma_w}{\gamma_d} - 1$ with $\gamma_w = 1 \text{ g/cc}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
e &= \dfrac{2.65 \times 1}{1.68} - 1 \\[4pt]
&= 1.5774 - 1 \\[4pt]
&= 0.577
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Compute Void Ratios at Loosest and Densest States
For $\gamma_{d,min} = 1.46 \text{ g/cc}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
e_{max} &= \dfrac{G_s \cdot \gamma_w}{\gamma_{d,min}} - 1 = \dfrac{2.65}{1.46} - 1 = 1.8151 - 1 = 0.815
\end{aligned}
$$

For $\gamma_{d,max} = 1.86 \text{ g/cc}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
e_{min} &= \dfrac{G_s \cdot \gamma_w}{\gamma_{d,max}} - 1 = \dfrac{2.65}{1.86} - 1 = 1.4247 - 1 = 0.425
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Compute Relative Density (Part a)
Using the void ratio form:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_r &= \dfrac{e_{max} - e}{e_{max} - e_{min}} \times 100 \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{0.815 - 0.577}{0.815 - 0.425} \times 100 \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{0.238}{0.390} \times 100 \\[4pt]
&= 0.6103 \times 100 \\[4pt]
&= 61.03\% \approx 61\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification using density form:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_r &= \dfrac{\gamma_{d,max}(\gamma_d - \gamma_{d,min})}{\gamma_d(\gamma_{d,max} - \gamma_{d,min})} \times 100 \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{1.86 \times (1.68 - 1.46)}{1.68 \times (1.86 - 1.46)} \times 100 \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{1.86 \times 0.22}{1.68 \times 0.40} \times 100 \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{0.4092}{0.672} \times 100 \\[4pt]
&= 60.89\% \approx 61\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Both methods yield ~61%** — confirming the calculation. A minor discrepancy is due to rounding.

### Step 4: Classification (Part c)

| $D_r$ Range | Classification |
|-------------|----------------|
| 0–15% | Very Loose |
| 15–35% | Loose |
| 35–65% | Medium Dense |
| **65–85%** | **Dense** ← 61% is just below this |
| 85–100% | Very Dense |

**Result:** $D_r = 61\%$ → Soil is classified as **Medium Dense** (just under the threshold for Dense).

## 3.3 Alternative Worked Example — Solving for In-situ Dry Density

> [!NOTE]
> **Reverse Problem (Frequently asked in KTU):**
> A sand has $e_{max} = 0.85$, $e_{min} = 0.45$, and $G_s = 2.67$. The deposit is classified as "Dense" (Dr = 75%). Find the in-situ dry unit weight and natural moisture content assuming saturation S = 100%.

### Step 1: Calculate $e$ at Dr = 75%

$$
\begin{aligned}
0.75 &= \dfrac{0.85 - e}{0.85 - 0.45} \\[4pt]
0.75 \times 0.40 &= 0.85 - e \\[4pt]
0.30 &= 0.85 - e \\[4pt]
e &= 0.55
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Calculate Dry Unit Weight

$$
\begin{aligned}
\gamma_d &= \dfrac{G_s \cdot \gamma_w}{1 + e} = \dfrac{2.67 \times 9.81}{1 + 0.55} = \dfrac{26.19}{1.55} = 16.90 \text{ kN/m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Calculate Saturated Unit Weight (S = 100%)
For saturated sand: $\gamma_{sat} = \gamma_d + n \cdot \gamma_w$, where $n = \dfrac{e}{1+e}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
n &= \dfrac{0.55}{1.55} = 0.3548 \\[4pt]
\gamma_{sat} &= 16.90 + 0.3548 \times 9.81 = 16.90 + 3.48 = 20.38 \text{ kN/m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Determine Water Content at Saturation

$$
\begin{aligned}
w_{sat} \cdot G_s &= e \cdot S \\[4pt]
w_{sat} &= \dfrac{e \cdot S}{G_s} = \dfrac{0.55 \times 1.0}{2.67} = 0.206 = 20.6\%
\end{aligned}
$$

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[START: Cohesionless Soil Sample] --> A1[Step 1: Oven-Dry Soil at 105 to 110 deg C for 24 hours]
    A1 --> A2[Step 2: Determine Specific Gravity Gs via Pycnometer]
    A2 --> B1[Part A: Determine emin via Densest State Test]
    A2 --> C1[Part B: Determine emax via Loosest State Test]
    A2 --> D1[Part C: Determine In-situ gamma_d via Sand Cone or Core Cutter]

    B1 --> B2[Step B1: Fill Mould in 3 Equal Layers]
    B2 --> B3[Step B2: Place Surcharge Weight of 140 g per 100 cm2]
    B3 --> B4[Step B3: Vibrate at 3600 vpm for 8 to 12 minutes]
    B4 --> B5[Step B4: Remove Surcharge and Trim Surface]
    B5 --> B6[Step B5: Weigh Soil plus Mould]
    B6 --> B7[Step B6: Compute gamma_d_max and hence e_min]

    C1 --> C2[Step C1: Pour Soil via Funnel at Zero Drop Height]
    C2 --> C3[Step C2: Use Spout dia 12 mm and Tip 25 mm Above Soil Surface]
    C3 --> C4[Step C3: Fill Mould to Overflow then Strike Off]
    C4 --> C5[Step C4: Weigh Soil plus Mould]
    C5 --> C6[Step C5: Compute gamma_d_min and hence e_max]

    D1 --> D2[Step D1: Extract Undisturbed Sample from Field]

    B7 --> E1[MASTER CALCULATION ZONE]
    C6 --> E1
    D4 --> E1

    E1 --> E2[Compute Dr equals emax minus e divided by emax minus emin times 100]
    E2 --> F1[Classify Soil Density: Very Loose to Very Dense]
    F1 --> G0[END: Report Dr in Percentage and Classification]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question]**

### Question 1: Define Relative Density. Why is it not applicable to cohesive soils? [3 Marks]

**Model Answer:**
Relative Density (Dr) is defined as the ratio of the difference between the void ratio in the loosest state ($e_{max}$) and the natural void ratio ($e$) to the difference between the void ratios in the loosest ($e_{max}$) and densest ($e_{min}$) states.

$$D_r = \dfrac{e_{max} - e}{e_{max} - e_{min}} \times 100\%$$

It is **not applicable to cohesive soils** because:
1. Cohesive soils (clays) cannot be tested in a "loosest" state — they always exhibit some degree of structure and bonding.
2. Their behavior is governed by **Atterberg limits and water content**, not by particle packing.
3. They are fundamentally **strain-rate sensitive** and time-dependent, which violates the quasi-static assumptions of the relative density test.

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]**

### Question 2: What is the difference between Relative Compaction and Relative Density? [3 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Relative Compaction ($R_c$) | Relative Density ($D_r$) |
|-----------|------------------------------|---------------------------|
| **Definition** | Ratio of field dry density to Proctor max dry density | Ratio of difference in void ratios |
| **Formula** | $R_c = \dfrac{\gamma_d}{\gamma_{d,max}} \times 100$ | $D_r = \dfrac{e_{max} - e}{e_{max} - e_{min}} \times 100$ |
| **Used for** | Cohesive soils (clayey fills) | Cohesionless soils (sands, gravels) |
| **Reference state** | Modified/Standard Proctor test | Loosest & Densest states |
| **Test method** | Proctor compaction | Vibration + funnel pouring |

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Module Internal Choice Questions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Module in the 14-mark section contains an internal choice between two questions (Question A and Question B). Both are presented here for complete preparation.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [Choice 1]

**(a)** Describe the laboratory procedure for determining the **maximum (densest) dry density** of a cohesionless soil as per **IS 2720 (Part 14) — 1983**. List all apparatus and state the test conditions. [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

**Apparatus Required:**
1. Cylindrical mould of 1000 cm³ capacity with detachable base plate
2. Vibrating table (capacity to impart vertical vibration at 3600 vpm with 0.5 mm amplitude)
3. Surcharge weight of 140 g per 100 cm² area
4. Weighing balance (0.1 g accuracy)
5. Straight edge, scoop, and tamping rod
6. Calibration block

**Procedure:**
1. Calibrate the mould volume using the calibration block and water.
2. Take oven-dried soil (about 5–6 kg passing 4.75 mm IS sieve).
3. Fill the mould in **3 equal layers** with a scoop.
4. Place the surcharge weight of 140 g/100 cm² on top of the soil.
5. Vibrate the assembly on the vibrating table for **8 minutes at 3600 vibrations per minute** (or 12 minutes for finer materials).
6. Remove the surcharge, carefully strike off the top surface flush with the mould rim using a straight edge.
7. Weigh the mould with the compacted soil. Record mass as $M_2$.
8. Determine the maximum dry density as:

$$\gamma_{d,max} = \dfrac{M_2 - M_1}{V_m}$$

where $M_1$ = mass of empty mould, $V_m$ = volume of mould.

9. Compute the minimum void ratio:

$$e_{min} = \dfrac{G_s \cdot \gamma_w}{\gamma_{d,max}} - 1$$

**(b)** A sand sample has the following properties: $G_s = 2.68$, $\gamma_{d,max} = 1.92$ g/cc, $\gamma_{d,min} = 1.52$ g/cc, and the in-situ dry density is 1.74 g/cc. **Calculate the relative density and classify the soil deposit.** [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Calculate void ratios**

For $\gamma_{d,min} = 1.52$ g/cc:

$$e_{max} = \dfrac{2.68 \times 1}{1.52} - 1 = 1.7632 - 1 = 0.763$$

For $\gamma_{d,max} = 1.92$ g/cc:

$$e_{min} = \dfrac{2.68 \times 1}{1.92} - 1 = 1.3958 - 1 = 0.396$$

For in-situ $\gamma_d = 1.74$ g/cc:

$$e = \dfrac{2.68 \times 1}{1.74} - 1 = 1.5402 - 1 = 0.540$$

**Step 2: Compute Relative Density**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_r &= \dfrac{e_{max} - e}{e_{max} - e_{min}} \times 100 \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{0.763 - 0.540}{0.763 - 0.396} \times 100 \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{0.223}{0.367} \times 100 \\[4pt]
&= 60.76\% \approx 60.8\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Classification**

| $D_r$ Range | Classification |
|-------------|----------------|
| 0–15% | Very Loose |
| 15–35% | Loose |
| **35–65%** | **Medium Dense ← Ans: 60.8%** |
| 65–85% | Dense |
| 85–100% | Very Dense |

**Result:** $D_r = 60.8\%$ → **Medium Dense sand.**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [Choice 2]

**(a)** Explain the procedure to determine the **minimum (loosest) dry density** of a cohesionless soil. State the precautions to be taken during the test. [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

**Apparatus Required:**
1. Cylindrical mould (1000 cm³ capacity)
2. Pouring funnel with **12 mm diameter spout** (or scoop)
3. Weighing balance (0.1 g accuracy)
4. Straight edge, glass plate

**Procedure:**
1. Calibrate the mould volume.
2. Use oven-dried soil sample (about 4–5 kg).
3. Position the pouring funnel such that the **spout tip is exactly 25 mm above the top surface** of the soil being formed. (As the soil mound rises, the funnel must be raised to maintain the constant 25 mm drop height.)
4. Pour the soil through the funnel in a **continuous, steady stream** without any vibration or tamping.
5. Allow the soil to overflow the mould, then carefully strike off the top surface flush using a straight edge (avoid any jarring).
6. Weigh the mould + soil. Record as $M_2$.
7. The minimum dry density is computed as:

$$\gamma_{d,min} = \dfrac{M_2 - M_1}{V_m}$$

8. Compute the maximum void ratio:

$$e_{max} = \dfrac{G_s \cdot \gamma_w}{\gamma_{d,min}} - 1$$

**Precautions:**
- **Zero drop height discipline:** Maintain exactly 25 mm drop at all times to prevent particle impact energy.
- **No vibration** of the mould or table during pouring.
- Soil must be **completely dry** to prevent any capillary tension-induced cohesion.
- Avoid striking or jarring the mould after filling.
- Use a **continuous pour** without interruptions to prevent layering artifacts.

**(b)** A cohesionless soil deposit has $e_{max} = 0.92$, $e_{min} = 0.48$, and the natural void ratio is 0.65. The water table is at great depth. **If the relative density is to be improved from its current value to 75% by vibrocompaction, calculate:** (i) the new in-situ void ratio required, and (ii) the corresponding new dry unit weight. Take $G_s = 2.70$. [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Current Relative Density (Verification)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{r,initial} &= \dfrac{0.92 - 0.65}{0.92 - 0.48} \times 100 = \dfrac{0.27}{0.44} \times 100 = 61.36\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate New Void Ratio for $D_r = 75\%$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
0.75 &= \dfrac{0.92 - e_{new}}{0.92 - 0.48} \\[4pt]
0.75 \times 0.44 &= 0.92 - e_{new} \\[4pt]
0.33 &= 0.92 - e_{new} \\[4pt]
e_{new} &= 0.59
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate New Dry Unit Weight**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\gamma_{d,new} &= \dfrac{G_s \cdot \gamma_w}{1 + e_{new}} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{2.70 \times 9.81}{1 + 0.59} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{26.487}{1.59} \\[4pt]
&= 16.66 \text{ kN/m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Additional Check — % Reduction in Void Ratio**

$$\text{Reduction} = \dfrac{0.65 - 0.59}{0.65} \times 100 = 9.23\%$$

This indicates the **soil volume must be reduced by ~9.2%** through vibrocompaction to achieve the desired Dr improvement.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Unit Mismatch in $G_s$:** When using $G_s \cdot \gamma_w$ in the void ratio formula, $\gamma_w$ must be in **consistent units** with $\gamma_d$. If $\gamma_d$ is in g/cc, use $\gamma_w = 1$ g/cc. If in kN/m³, use $\gamma_w = 9.81$ kN/m³. **Mismatch loses 1 mark.**
>
> 2. **Forgetting to Convert $D_r$ to Percentage:** Students often leave $D_r$ as a decimal (0.61) instead of multiplying by 100 to get 61%. This is a **favourite KTU trap — loses 1 mark.**
>
> 3. **Confusing $e_{max}$ and $e_{min}$:** Remember — **$e_{max}$ is at the loosest (lowest density) state**, and **$e_{min}$ is at the densest (highest density) state**. A common error is to write $e_{min}$ with $\gamma_{d,min}$.
>
> 4. **Density Form vs. Void Ratio Form:** When the problem gives **dry unit weights only** (no void ratios), the **density form** is faster. When the problem gives **specific gravity and dry density**, compute void ratios first.
>
> 5. **Surcharge Weight Interpretation:** The 140 g per 100 cm² surcharge in the **emin** test is per unit area — for a 100 cm² mould cross-section, the surcharge is 140 g. Some students write "140 g" without the per unit area clarification.
>
> 6. **Vibration Time Confusion:** 8 minutes for **coarser** materials, 12 minutes for **finer** sands. Writing only "vibrate until densification is complete" loses marks — be specific.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!NOTE]
> **Print this checklist and review before every KTU exam for Module Experiment 2.**

- [ ] **Relative Density Formula (Void Ratio Form):** $D_r = \dfrac{e_{max} - e}{e_{max} - e_{min}} \times 100$
- [ ] **Relative Density Formula (Density Form):** $D_r = \dfrac{\gamma_{d,max}(\gamma_d - \gamma_{d,min})}{\gamma_d(\gamma_{d,max} - \gamma_{d,min})} \times 100$
- [ ] **emin Test:** Vibrating table method, 3600 vpm, 0.5 mm amplitude, 8–12 minutes, surcharge 140 g per 100 cm²
- [ ] **emax Test:** Funnel pouring, 12 mm spout, 25 mm constant drop height, no vibration
- [ ] **Standard Mould:** 1000 cm³, 100 mm dia
- [ ] **Density Index Classification:** Very Loose (0–15%), Loose (15–35%), Medium Dense (35–65%), Dense (65–85%), Very Dense (85–100%)
- [ ] **Void Ratio Conversion:** $e = \dfrac{G_s \cdot \gamma_w}{\gamma_d} - 1$
- [ ] **Relative Compaction:** $R_c = \dfrac{\gamma_d}{\gamma_{d,max}} \times 100$ (used for cohesive soils)
- [ ] **IS Code Reference:** IS 2720 (Part 14) – 1983 (Reaffirmed 2020)
- [ ] **Applicability:** ONLY for cohesionless, free-draining, non-cohesive granular soils
- [ ] **Why Not Cohesive Soils:** Particle bonding, water content sensitivity, time-dependent behavior
- [ ] **Liquefaction Link:** Dr > 60–70% provides significant resistance to earthquake-induced liquefaction
- [ ] **Empirical Shear Strength:** Friction angle $\phi' \approx 30° + 0.15 \cdot Dr$ (in %) for quartz sands (approximate)
- [ ] **Always Convert to %:** Multiply decimal Dr by 100 in the final answer
- [ ] **Cross-Verification:** Both void ratio and density forms should yield the same Dr — use this for self-check

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Quick-Fire Facts to Memorize:**
> 1. Governing IS Code → **IS 2720 Part 14**
> 2. Vibration frequency → **3600 vpm**
> 3. Surcharge → **140 g per 100 cm²**
> 4. Funnel spout → **12 mm**
> 5. Drop height → **25 mm above soil surface**
> 6. Mould volume → **1000 cm³**
> 7. Threshold for Dense sand → **Dr > 65%**
> 8. Threshold for Very Dense → **Dr > 85%**
> 9. Liquefaction safety threshold → **Dr > 60–70%**
> 10. Vibration time → **8 min (coarse) / 12 min (fine)**
